# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Differentiate between Preventive, Detective, and Corrective controls with one example each from an Industrial Control System.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Preventive Controls** are security mechanisms that aim to **stop** a security incident *before* it happens by blocking unauthorized access or activity. 
*Example:* A network **firewall** between the corporate IT network and the SCADA OT network that blocks unauthorized Modbus/TCP traffic.

**Detective Controls** are security mechanisms that **identify and alert** on a security incident *during* or *after* its occurrence by monitoring system activity. 
*Example:* An **Intrusion Detection System (IDS)** that flags anomalous SCADA commands or unauthorized login attempts.

**Corrective Controls** are security mechanisms that **restore** the system to normal operation *after* a security incident has occurred, mitigating damage and preventing recurrence. 
*Example:* **Restoring a PLC's firmware from a known-good backup** after a malware infection.

> **Mark Distribution (3 Marks):** 1 mark for each correct type with example.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**Why are Detective Controls considered necessary even when strong Preventive Controls are in place? Justify with an ICS example.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

Detective Controls are essential because **no Preventive Control offers 100% effectiveness** — there is always some residual risk due to zero-day exploits, insider threats, or misconfigurations. Detective Controls fill this gap by **identifying breaches that bypass prevention**, enabling a timely response.

*ICS Example:* Even with a properly configured firewall, a sophisticated **Stuxnet-style USB-borne malware** can infiltrate an engineering laptop and reach the PLC. An **IDS monitoring Modbus traffic** would detect the anomalous command sequence and alert the SOC, allowing containment before catastrophic damage.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1.5 marks for justification + 1.5 marks for ICS example.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]

#### (a) **Explain in detail the three types of cybersecurity controls: Preventive, Detective, and Corrective. For each type, provide at least two ICS-specific examples and identify the phase of the security lifecycle in which it operates.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Introduction (0.5 Mark):**
Cybersecurity controls are the safeguards deployed to protect the **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — of an information system. In Industrial Control Systems, these controls are classified by the **phase of the attack lifecycle** they address: Before (Preventive), During (Detective), and After (Corrective).

**1. Preventive Controls (2 Marks):**
Preventive controls are proactive measures designed to *preemptively block* unauthorized activity before it can affect the system. They operate on the principle of *"deny by default."*

*ICS-Specific Examples:*
- **Air-gapping:** Physically isolating the Process Control Network from the corporate IT network, eliminating network-based attack vectors to legacy PLCs.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) on Engineering Workstations:** Requiring engineers to authenticate using a password *and* a hardware token before uploading ladder logic to a PLC.

**2. Detective Controls (2 Marks):**
Detective controls are reactive-monitoring measures that *identify and log* security events as they occur. They do not block the attack but provide visibility.

*ICS-Specific Examples:*
- **SCADA Alarm Logs with SIEM Correlation:** Detecting unusual sequences of HMI commands that deviate from the operator's normal pattern (e.g., 20 setpoint changes in 5 minutes from a single user).
- **Watchdog Timers on PLCs:** Detecting when a PLC's scan time exceeds safe thresholds, which may indicate a denial-of-service or firmware tampering.

**3. Corrective Controls (2 Marks):**
Corrective controls are reactive-restoration measures that *repair* the system to its pre-incident state and prevent recurrence.

*ICS-Specific Examples:*
- **Automatic Safety Shutdown (SIS):** A Safety Instrumented System triggering an emergency valve closure when process variables exceed safe limits due to a cyber-induced anomaly.
- **Firmware Restoration from Trusted Backup:** Re-flashing a compromised PLC with a known-good firmware image after a malware infection, then applying a security patch.

**Phase of Lifecycle (0.5 Mark):**
| Control Type | Phase |
|---|---|
| Preventive | $t < t_{\text{attack}}$ (Before) |
| Detective | $t = t_{\text{attack}}$ (During) |
| Corrective | $t > t_{\text{attack}}$ (After) |

---

#### (b) **Discuss the concept of "Defense-in-Depth" in ICS security. How do the three control types together reduce residual risk? Support your answer with a numerical example showing how layered controls achieve higher combined effectiveness than a single control.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Defense-in-Depth Concept (2 Marks):**
Defense-in-Depth is a security strategy that employs **multiple, overlapping layers of controls** so that the failure of one control does not result in a complete compromise. In ICS, this is implemented across the **Purdue Model** levels (0 through 5) using a combination of **Preventive, Detective, and Corrective** controls.

**Layered Mapping to Purdue Model (2 Marks):**
- **Level 0–1 (Field Devices):** Preventive: Tamper seals; Detective: Sensor anomalies; Corrective: Manual override.
- **Level 2–3 (Control & SCADA):** Preventive: Firewalls & DMZ; Detective: IDS & SIEM; Corrective: Patch & Restore.
- **Level 4–5 (Enterprise):** Preventive: Encryption & MFA; Detective: SOC monitoring; Corrective: DRP & BCP.

**Mathematical Justification (3 Marks):**
The residual risk after applying a control is:

$$
R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} \times (1 - E_{\text{control}})
$$

For **multiple independent layers**, the combined effectiveness is:

$$
E_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - E_i)
$$

**Numerical Example:**
Assume three controls with effectiveness $E_1 = 0.90$ (Firewall, Preventive), $E_2 = 0.85$ (IDS, Detective), $E_3 = 0.80$ (Backup, Corrective).

$$
E_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (1 - 0.90)(1 - 0.85)(1 - 0.80)
$$

$$
E_{\text{combined}} = 1 - (0.10)(0.15)(0.20)
$$

$$
E_{\text{combined}} = 1 - 0.003 = 0.997
$$

$$
\boxed{E_{\text{combined}} = 99.7\%}
$$

In contrast, a **single 90% effective control** leaves **10% residual risk exposure**. By layering all three control types, the residual exposure drops to just **0.3%** — a **~33-fold improvement**. This quantitatively proves that **layered Defense-in-Depth is mathematically superior** to any single control.

**Conclusion:** The three control types are **complementary, not redundant**. Preventive controls stop the bulk of attacks; Detective controls catch what slips through; Corrective controls restore operations and harden the system for the future.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]

#### (a) **Classify each of the following controls as Preventive, Detective, or Corrective, and justify your answer with one sentence each:**
**(i) Firewall (ii) Intrusion Detection System (iii) Data Backup (iv) Encryption (v) CCTV Surveillance (vi) Incident Response Plan (vii) Multi-Factor Authentication.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

| # | Control | Classification | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Firewall | **Preventive** | It blocks unauthorized network traffic from reaching the ICS network *before* any attack occurs. |
| (ii) | Intrusion Detection System | **Detective** | It monitors network traffic and *detects/identifies* suspicious activity by comparing it against known signatures. |
| (iii) | Data Backup | **Corrective** | It does not prevent an attack but enables *restoration* of data after a ransomware or data-loss incident. |
| (iv) | Encryption | **Preventive** | It prevents unauthorized parties from *reading* sensitive data even if they access it, blocking confidentiality breaches. |
| (v) | CCTV Surveillance | **Detective** | It does not stop intruders but *records and detects* their presence, providing evidence and real-time alerts. |
| (vi) | Incident Response Plan | **Corrective** | It defines the steps to *contain, eradicate, and recover* from a security incident after it has occurred. |
| (vii) | Multi-Factor Authentication | **Preventive** | It requires multiple proofs of identity, *preventing* unauthorized users from logging in even if a password is stolen. |

> **Mark Distribution (7 Marks):** 1 mark per correct classification + its justification.

---

#### (b) **With the help of a real-world ICS incident (such as Stuxnet or the 2015 Ukraine Power Grid attack), explain how Preventive, Detective, and Corrective controls would have been applied at each phase of the attack. Include a labeled block diagram in your description.** **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**Case Study: Stuxnet Attack (2010) on Iranian Uranium Enrichment Plant (3 Marks):**

The Stuxnet worm specifically targeted **Siemens S7-300 PLCs** controlling centrifuge rotor speeds at the Natanz facility. It altered the PLC logic to spin centrifuges at destructive speeds while displaying normal readings on HMIs.

| Attack Phase | What Happened | **Preventive Control (Should Have Been Used)** | **Detective Control (Should Have Been Used)** | **Corrective Control (Aftermath Action)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Initial Infection** | USB-borne worm entered via contractor's laptop | Air-gap enforcement; **disabled USB ports** on engineering workstations | USB port activity logging; file-integrity monitoring | Re-image infected laptops from clean media |
| **2. Lateral Movement** | Worm propagated via LAN to engineering stations | Network segmentation; **unidirectional gateways** | IDS detecting abnormal SMB traffic on OT network | Isolate infected VLANs; block lateral paths |
| **3. PLC Reprogramming** | Malicious ladder logic uploaded to S7-300 PLCs | **Code-signing and write-protection** on PLCs | Watchdog timer alarms; HMI process-value deviation alerts | Roll back PLC firmware to known-good image |
| **4. Physical Damage** | Centrifuges spun to failure | Safety interlocks with independent sensors | Vibration & current anomaly detection | Replace damaged centrifuges; manual safety shutdown |

**Block Diagram Representation (2 Marks):**

```
   [External Threat: USB Worm]
            |
            v
   +-------------------+
   |  PREVENTIVE        |  <-- Air-gap, USB disable, Code-signing
   |  (Should have      |
   |   blocked entry)   |
   +-------------------+
            |  (Bypassed)
            v
   +-------------------+
   |  DETECTIVE         |  <-- IDS, Watchdog, Anomaly alarms
   |  (Should have      |
   |   detected attack) |
   +-------------------+
            |  (Noticed too late)
            v
   +-------------------+
   |  CORRECTIVE        |  <-- SIS shutdown, PLC firmware restore
   |  (Restored         |
   |   operations)      |
   +-------------------+
            |
            v
   [System Back to Normal + Lessons Learned]
```

**Conclusion (2 Marks):**
The Stuxnet case study demonstrates that had **stronger Preventive controls** (USB disablement, code-signing) been in place, the worm may never have reached the PLCs. Even if it had, **Detective controls** (anomaly alarms on process variables) would have caught the deviation in centrifuge speed. Finally, **Corrective controls** (firmware restore, hardware replacement) enabled the plant to recover. The combination of all three is the hallmark of resilient ICS security.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Deduction Mistakes in This Topic:**
> 
> 1. **IDS vs IPS confusion:** Students frequently classify IDS as Preventive. The correct answer is **Detective** (IDS *detects and alerts*; IPS *prevents and blocks*). Marks lost: **1 mark per occurrence**.
> 
> 2. **Backup as Preventive:** Backup does not *prevent* an attack. It enables *recovery* after an attack. Correct classification: **Corrective**.
> 
> 3. **Single-word answers in 14-mark questions:** A 14-mark question requires *elaboration, examples, and diagrams*. A one-line answer will fetch at most 2–3 marks.
> 
> 4. **No diagram in long answers:** For questions worth $\geq 7$ marks, examiners expect at least one labeled block diagram or flowchart. Omitting it costs **1–2 marks**.
> 
> 5. **Confusing "encryption" with detective function:** Encryption is **Preventive** (it prevents unauthorized *reading*), not Detective. Marks lost: **1 mark**.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting ICS context:** Generic IT examples (like "email spam filter") without an ICS context (SCADA, PLC, HMI, Modbus) may receive **partial credit only**. Always map examples to **OT/ICS environments**.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Preventive Controls** = *Before* the attack = Stop the attack from happening (e.g., Firewall, MFA, Encryption, Air-gap, RBAC).
- ✅ **Detective Controls** = *During/After* occurrence = Identify and alert on the attack (e.g., IDS, SIEM, CCTV, Watchdog timers, Audit logs).
- ✅ **Corrective Controls** = *After* the attack = Restore and remediate (e.g., Backup, Patch, Incident Response, Safety Shutdown, Firmware restore).
- ✅ The **CIA Triad** underpins all controls; **Availability** is the most critical in ICS.
- ✅ **IDS = Detective**, **IPS = Preventive** — remember this distinction.
- ✅ **Backup = Corrective**, not Preventive.
- ✅ **Encryption = Preventive** (blocks unauthorized disclosure).
- ✅ **CCTV = Detective** (does not stop intrusion, only records it).
- ✅ **Defense-in-Depth** = Layered controls across the **Purdue Model** for maximum security.
- ✅ Combined effectiveness formula: $E_{\text{combined}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - E_i)$ — proves mathematically why layering works.
- ✅ Real-world cases: **Stuxnet (2010)**, **Ukraine Power Grid (2015)**, **Triton/TRISIS (2017)** — all illustrate the three controls in action.
- ✅ KTU exams test **classification**, **comparison**, **ICS-specific examples**, and the **defense-in-depth** concept.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to *classify* a control, always provide the **phase (Before/During/After)** and **one ICS-specific example** in your justification. This single habit alone can elevate your answer from a 1-mark to a full 2–3 mark response.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industry Standards and Frameworks (NIST, ISO/IEC 27001, CIS Controls)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industry Standards and Frameworks (NIST, ISO/IEC 27001, CIS Controls)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Standards and Frameworks** in the context of **Industrial Control System (ICS) Security** refer to the formalized, peer-reviewed, and globally recognized bodies of guidelines, controls, and procedural methodologies that govern the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (the **CIA Triad**) of Operational Technology (OT) and Industrial Automation & Control Systems (IACS).

These standards are not laws, but they constitute the **de facto engineering baseline** for designing, auditing, and certifying cyber-resilient control systems. The three pillars addressed in this module are:

1. **NIST (National Institute of Standards and Technology, U.S. Department of Commerce)** — A series of Special Publications (SP) and Frameworks (CSF) that provide a risk-based, tiered approach to managing cybersecurity risk.
2. **ISO/IEC 27001** — The international standard published by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)** and the **International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC)** that specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an **Information Security Management System (ISMS)**.
3. **CIS Controls (Center for Internet Security)** — A prioritized, prescriptive, and action-oriented set of cybersecurity best practices, originally derived from the SANS Top 20, structured into **Implementation Groups (IG1, IG2, IG3)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Code" View of Cyber Standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Constructing a Secure Factory**
> 
> Imagine you are building a chemical plant in Kerala. You would not invent your own electrical wiring rules or fire safety rules. Instead, you would follow the **National Building Code of India (NBC)** and the **Central Electricity Authority (CEA) Regulations**. You would then get the building **certified** by a third-party auditor.
> 
> Cybersecurity standards work the **exact same way** for an Industrial Control System:
> - **NIST CSF** → The detailed "engineering manual" written by U.S. government scientists. It tells you *what* to do and *why* (risk-based).
> - **ISO/IEC 27001** → The "international certification" (like an ISO 9001 quality mark) that proves to customers worldwide that your factory's information security is auditable and managed systematically using the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle.
> - **CIS Controls** → The "prioritized contractor's checklist" — a no-nonsense, opinionated list of the *first 18 things* you must do, sorted by which defenses stop the most real-world attacks (e.g., asset inventory first, then multi-factor authentication, then logging).

## 1.3 Why These Three Standards Coexist

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU examiner question is: *"Why not just use one standard?"*
> 
> **Answer:** They serve **complementary, not competing, roles**:
> - **NIST** = **Risk Management Philosophy** (What is the risk? What tier are we at?)
> - **ISO/IEC 27001** = **Management System Certification** (How do we manage the risk as a formal process?)
> - **CIS Controls** = **Tactical Implementation** (What specific technical safeguards do we deploy today?)

The official metrics these frameworks use to measure maturity or progress are commonly expressed in **tiers**, **levels**, or **Implementation Groups (IGs)**. The numeric ranges used in KTU questions and vendor literature are:

- **NIST CSF Tiers:** **1 (Partial)**, **2 (Risk Informed)**, **3 (Repeatable)**, **4 (Adaptive)**.
- **CIS Implementation Groups:** **IG1 (SMB)**, **IG2 (Mid-size)**, **IG3 (Large/Enterprise)**.
- **ISO/IEC 27001 Controls:** **93 controls** across **4 control themes** in the 2022 revision.

## 1.4 Scope Mapping to ICS / OT Environments

| Aspect | Pure IT (Enterprise) | ICS / OT (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Goal | Confidentiality of data | **Availability & Safety** of physical process |
| Lifecycle | 3–5 years | **15–30 years** (legacy PLCs, SCADA) |
| Patching | Frequent, automated | **Restricted, scheduled outages only** |
| Protocol | TCP/IP, HTTP, DNS | **Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA** |
| Best-fit Standard | ISO 27001, CIS v8 | **NIST SP 800-82** (overlay of CSF for OT) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) — The 5 Core Functions

The NIST CSF (current version **2.0**, released February 2024) organizes all cybersecurity activity into **5 concurrent and continuous functions**. The acronym is **IDPRR** — but the official order is **Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover**, with the new 6th function **Govern** added in v2.0.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NIST CSF 6 Functions (v2.0):**
> 1. **GV — Govern:** Establish and monitor the organization's cybersecurity risk management strategy, policies, and expectations. (New in v2.0)
> 2. **ID — Identify:** Develop an organizational understanding to manage cybersecurity risk to systems, people, assets, data, and capabilities. *In OT: map all PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, engineering workstations.*
> 3. **PR — Protect:** Implement appropriate safeguards to ensure delivery of critical services. *In OT: network segmentation, role-based access, patch management.*
> 4. **DE — Detect:** Define and implement activities to identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event. *In OT: ICS-aware IDS, log monitoring of Modbus/DNP3 traffic.*
> 5. **RS — Respond:** Take action regarding a detected cybersecurity incident. *In OT: safety instrumented system (SIS) activation, manual fallback.*
> 6. **RC — Recover:** Restore assets and operations affected by a cybersecurity incident. *In OT: validated backup restoration, forensic preservation.*

### NIST Tiers (Maturity Levels)

NIST defines **4 Tiers** to describe the organization's overall cybersecurity risk management practice:

- **Tier 1 — Partial:** Ad-hoc, reactive, no organization-wide risk awareness.
- **Tier 2 — Risk Informed:** Risk management is approved by management but not enterprise-wide.
- **Tier 3 — Repeatable:** Formal policies, regularly updated, organization-wide.
- **Tier 4 — Adaptive:** Continuous improvement, real-time alerts, integrated with risk culture.

## 2.2 NIST SP 800-82 — The OT/ICS Overlay

**NIST SP 800-82r3** ("Guide to Operational Technology (OT) System Security") is a specialized guide that **overlays** the CSF onto industrial environments. It is the single most important KTU-recommended document for ICS security practitioners.

> [!NOTE]
> Key adaptations in SP 800-82 for OT:
> - Re-prioritizes the **CIA Triad** to: **Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality** (the reverse of IT).
> - Recommends **conduit and zone-based network architecture** (ISA/IEC 62443 terminology).
> - Provides an **OT-tailored mapping table** linking CSF subcategories to specific ICS technologies (e.g., Level 0–3 Purdue Model layers).
> - Introduces the **OT taxonomy**: Field devices (sensors, actuators), Controllers (PLCs, RTUs, DCS), Supervisory (HMI, SCADA, EWS), Operations (MES, historians).

## 2.3 ISO/IEC 27001 — The Management System Standard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** is a **management system standard**, not a technical controls list. It certifies the *process* by which an organization manages information security, not the specific tools installed.

### 2.3.1 The PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) Lifecycle

ISO 27001 mandates the **PDCA cycle** for the ISMS:

- **Plan:** Establish the ISMS policy, objectives, processes, and risk treatment plans. Conduct a formal **risk assessment** with documented criteria.
- **Do:** Implement the risk treatment plan, deploy controls, train staff, operate the controls.
- **Check:** Monitor, measure, audit, and review the ISMS performance against the policy.
- **Act:** Take corrective actions and continually improve the ISMS based on audit findings.

### 2.3.2 ISO 27001 Control Structure (2022 Revision)

The 2022 revision reduced the controls from 114 (in 2013) to **93 controls**, reorganized into **4 themes**:

- **Organizational Controls — 37 controls** (e.g., policies, supplier relationships, incident management).
- **People Controls — 8 controls** (e.g., screening, awareness, disciplinary process).
- **Physical Controls — 14 controls** (e.g., physical entry, equipment maintenance, secure disposal).
- **Technological Controls — 34 controls** (e.g., authentication, encryption, backup, logging, network segregation).

> [!NOTE]
> **ISO/IEC 27019:2017** is a sector-specific extension that provides additional guidance for the **energy utility industry** and is highly relevant to ICS/SCADA environments (power grids, water treatment, oil & gas).

## 2.4 CIS Critical Security Controls (v8) — The Prioritized Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The CIS Controls (v8, released May 2021) are a **defender's prioritized action list**. There are exactly **18 top-level controls** (down from 20 in v7), each with a number of **Implementation Safeguards (IGs)**.

### 2.4.1 Implementation Groups (IGs)

| Implementation Group | Organization Profile | Safeguards Required |
|---|---|---|
| **IG1** | Small business (≤ 50 employees), limited IT/OT, data sensitivity low | **56 safeguards** — essential cyber hygiene |
| **IG2** | Mid-size, multiple departments, moderate data sensitivity | **74 additional safeguards** (130 total) |
| **IG3** | Large enterprise / critical infrastructure (e.g., power plant) | **23 additional safeguards** (153 total) |

### 2.4.2 The 18 CIS Controls (v8)

1. Inventory and Control of Enterprise Assets
2. Inventory and Control of Software Assets
3. Data Protection
4. Secure Configuration of Enterprise Assets and Software
5. Account Management
6. Access Control Management
7. Continuous Vulnerability Management
8. Audit Log Management
9. Email and Web Browser Protections
10. Malware Defenses
11. Data Recovery
12. Network Infrastructure Management
13. Network Monitoring and Defense
14. Security Awareness and Skills Training
15. Service Provider Management
16. Application Software Security
17. Incident Response Management
18. Penetration Testing

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix (Exam-Ready)

| Feature | NIST CSF | ISO/IEC 27001 | CIS Controls v8 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Publisher** | NIST (USA) | ISO / IEC (Global) | CIS (Consortium) |
| **Document Type** | Voluntary Framework | Certifiable Standard | Prescriptive Best Practice |
| **Primary Focus** | Risk-based management | ISMS process certification | Tactical technical safeguards |
| **Maturity Model** | 4 Tiers | Certification (Pass/Fail) | 3 Implementation Groups |
| **Number of Controls** | 108 subcategories (v1.1) | 93 controls (Annex A, 2022) | 18 controls, 153 safeguards |
| **Certification** | No | Yes (via accredited bodies) | No (self-attestation) |
| **OT-Specific** | Yes (via SP 800-82r3) | Yes (via 27019) | Partial (IT-leaning) |
| **Governing Cycle** | Continuous improvement | PDCA | Maturity progression |
| **Typical User** | U.S. critical infrastructure | Multinational corporations | All organizations, prioritized |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Grid Operator (e.g., KSEB in Kerala):** Uses **NIST CSF + SP 800-82** as the primary framework, often required by CEA / Ministry of Power regulations in India.
- **Petrochemical Refinery (e.g., BPCL Kochi):** Uses **ISO/IEC 27001** for corporate certification, with **27019** for the OT/Process Control domain.
- **Water Treatment Utility (e.g., KWWA):** Typically starts with **CIS IG1** to achieve baseline cyber hygiene in 6–12 months before moving to IG2.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Lifecycle Derivations

> [!NOTE]
> This module is **non-mathematical**, so the "derivations" below are **analytical step-by-step methodologies** for selecting, applying, and certifying against the standards. These are the exact procedural sequences a KTU examiner expects for a 14-mark question.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: The "Framework Selection" Decision Tree

A KTU 14-mark question often asks: *"Given an organization profile, which framework should they adopt first?"* The following is the standard KTU-recommended decision sequence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify Regulatory Pressure} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{U.S. critical infrastructure / Indian CEA-regulated} \Rightarrow \text{NIST CSF + SP 800-82} \\
\text{Global customers / contractually required} \Rightarrow \text{ISO/IEC 27001} \\
\text{No external mandate, fast tactical wins} \Rightarrow \text{CIS Controls}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2: Determine Size \& Risk Profile} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\leq 50 \text{ employees, low risk} \Rightarrow \text{CIS IG1} \\
50\text{–}500 \text{ employees, moderate risk} \Rightarrow \text{CIS IG2} \\
> 500 \text{ employees or high-risk OT} \Rightarrow \text{CIS IG3} \cup \text{ISO 27001}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3: Map OT-Specific Needs} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Legacy ICS with availability priority} \Rightarrow \text{Overlay with NIST SP 800-82r3} \\
\text{Energy / utility sector} \Rightarrow \text{Overlay with ISO/IEC 27019} \\
\text{General manufacturing} \Rightarrow \text{CIS Control 1–6 are mandatory}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A medium-sized pharmaceutical plant in Kerala (250 employees) wants to secure its batch control SCADA system and is bidding for a European contract that requires an ISMS audit. Recommend a standards adoption roadmap.

**Solution Walkthrough:**
1. **Step 1 — Regulatory Pressure:** European contract → ISO/IEC 27001 is mandatory. Indian pharmaceutical plant → also aligns with NIST CSF for OT.
2. **Step 2 — Size & Risk:** 250 employees + critical OT (batch integrity) → CIS IG2 baseline + full ISO 27001 ISMS.
3. **Step 3 — OT Overlays:** Pharmaceutical batch records are GMP-critical → adopt NIST SP 800-82r3 principles for the OT segment, even though it is not formally certified.
4. **Final Roadmap Order:** 
   - Year 1 → Implement **CIS IG1 + IG2** safeguards (asset inventory, MFA, logging, backups).
   - Year 2 → Build **ISMS documentation** per ISO 27001 Clause 4–10, conduct internal audit.
   - Year 3 → Achieve **ISO 27001 certification** + maintain CSF mapping.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: NIST CSF Tier Progression

A KTU question may ask: *"A company moves from Tier 1 to Tier 3. Explain the qualitative transformation."* Use the following step-wise mapping:

$$
\text{Tier 1 (Partial)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Tier 2 (Risk Informed)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Tier 3 (Repeatable)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Tier 4 (Adaptive)}
$$

| Tier | Risk Awareness | Process Maturity | OT Example (Refinery) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | No OT asset list exists | Ad-hoc, firefighting | No inventory of PLCs; attacks handled reactively |
| **2** | OT risk register exists | Approved by plant manager | List of critical assets; documented firewall rules |
| **3** | Risk register is updated quarterly | Formal, organization-wide, audited | Annual penetration test; SIEM on SCADA VLAN |
| **4** | Risk register is updated in near real-time | Continuous, integrated with safety culture | Active threat hunting on Modbus/DNP3; integrated with plant DCS |

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: ISO 27001 PDCA Mapping to ICS Operations

A frequent KTU question is: *"Apply the PDCA cycle to a SCADA system upgrade."* The following is the KTU-mapped answer:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{PLAN:} \quad & \text{Define ISMS scope (e.g., Boiler House SCADA).} \nonumber \\
& \text{Identify assets (PLCs, HMIs, historian, EWS).} \nonumber \\
& \text{Conduct risk assessment: likelihood $\times$ impact.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Select controls from Annex A (e.g., A.8.7 protection against malware).} \nonumber
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{DO:} \quad & \text{Deploy controls: install AV on EWS, segment PLC VLAN, enable MFA on HMI.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Train operators on phishing awareness.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Document everything in the Statement of Applicability (SoA).} \nonumber
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{CHECK:} \quad & \text{Run internal audits quarterly.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Monitor SIEM for unauthorized Modbus writes.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Measure: MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures), MTTR (Mean Time To Recover).} \nonumber
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{ACT:} \quad & \text{Corrective Action Requests (CARs) for any non-conformity.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Update risk register post-incident.} \nonumber \\
& \text{Management review annually for continual improvement.} \nonumber
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation: CIS Implementation Group Selection Logic

The CIS community provides a formal selection method. For an organization, the decision is made as follows:

$$
\text{CIS IG} = f(\text{Data Sensitivity}, \text{Staff Count}, \text{OT/IT Complexity}, \text{Regulatory Exposure})
$$

| Question | If YES → | If NO → |
|---|---|---|
| Does the organization employ dedicated cybersecurity staff? | IG2 minimum | IG1 |
| Does it hold regulated data (PHI, PCI, OT safety data)? | IG2 or IG3 | IG1 |
| Does it operate critical infrastructure (power, water, oil)? | **IG3 mandatory** | IG2 max |
| Is it subject to international contracts? | IG2 + ISO 27001 | IG1 sufficient |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Framework Cross-Mapping

The following matrix maps equivalent concepts across the three frameworks — a frequent KTU Part B question.

| Concept | NIST CSF Subcategory | ISO 27001:2022 Control | CIS Control v8 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Asset Inventory | ID.AM-1 (Hardware) | A.5.9 (Inventory of assets) | CIS 1 (Enterprise Assets) |
| Software Inventory | ID.AM-2 (Software) | A.5.9 | CIS 2 (Software Assets) |
| Access Control | PR.AA-1 (Identities) | A.5.15, A.8.2 | CIS 5 & 6 |
| Vulnerability Management | ID.RA-1, DE.CM-9 | A.8.8 | CIS 7 |
| Logging & Monitoring | DE.CM-1, DE.AE-2 | A.8.15, A.8.16 | CIS 8 |
| Incident Response | RS.MA, RS.AN, RS.MI | A.5.24–A.5.27 | CIS 17 |
| Data Backup & Recovery | RC.RP-1, PR.IP-4 | A.8.13 | CIS 11 |
| Network Segmentation | PR.IR-1 | A.8.22 (Segregation of networks) | CIS 12 |
| Penetration Testing | ID.RA-4, DE.CM-8 | A.8.29 (Testing security) | CIS 18 |
| Training & Awareness | PR.AT-1 | A.6.3 (Awareness) | CIS 14 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams render the structural architecture of each framework. Node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters per the protocol; no reserved keywords are used.

## 4.1 NIST CSF v2.0 — 6 Functions Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Govern - Establish Risk Strategy]
    nodeB[Identify - Asset and Risk Discovery]
    nodeC[Protect - Safeguards and Controls]
    nodeD[Detect - Anomaly and Event Identification]
    nodeE[Respond - Incident Containment and Mitigation]
    nodeF[Recover - Restoration and Lessons Learned]
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF -.->|Feedback Loop| nodeA
```

## 4.2 ISO/IEC 27001:2022 PDCA Cycle Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeP[PLAN - Establish ISMS Scope, Risk Assessment, Statement of Applicability]
    nodeD[DO - Implement Controls, Train Staff, Operate ISMS]
    nodeC[CHECK - Monitor, Measure, Audit, Management Review]
    nodeA[ACT - Corrective Actions, Continual Improvement]
    nodeP ==> nodeD
    nodeD ==> nodeC
    nodeC ==> nodeA
    nodeA -.->|Feed lessons learned| nodeP
```

## 4.3 CIS Controls v8 — Three Implementation Groups (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeIG1[Implementation Group 1 - IG1 - 56 Safeguards]
        node1a[CIS 1: Inventory Hardware]
        node1b[CIS 2: Inventory Software]
        node1c[CIS 3: Data Protection Baseline]
        node1d[CIS 4: Secure Configuration]
        node1e[CIS 5: Account Management]
        node1f[CIS 6: Access Control]
        node1g[CIS 11: Data Recovery]
    end
    subgraph nodeIG2[Implementation Group 2 - IG2 - Adds 74 Safeguards]
        node2a[CIS 7: Vulnerability Management]
        node2b[CIS 8: Audit Log Management]
        node2c[CIS 9: Email and Web Protections]
        node2d[CIS 10: Malware Defenses]
        node2e[CIS 12: Network Infrastructure]
        node2f[CIS 13: Network Monitoring and Defense]
        node2g[CIS 14: Security Awareness]
        node2h[CIS 15: Service Provider Management]
    end
    subgraph nodeIG3[Implementation Group 3 - IG3 - Adds 23 Safeguards]
        node3a[CIS 16: Application Software Security]
        node3b[CIS 17: Incident Response Management]
        node3c[CIS 18: Penetration Testing]
    end
    nodeIG1 --> nodeIG2
    nodeIG2 --> nodeIG3
```

## 4.4 ICS-Specific Framework Overlay — Purdue Model + CSF Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodePurdue[Purdue Model Levels]
        nodeL0[Level 0 - Process and Sensors]
        nodeL1[Level 1 - Basic Control PLCs and RTUs]
        nodeL2[Level 2 - Supervisory HMI and SCADA]
        nodeL3[Level 3 - Operations MES and Historian]
        nodeL4[Level 4 - Enterprise Business Network]
        nodeL5[Level 5 - Internet and External]
    end
    subgraph nodeCSF[NIST CSF Functions Applied]
        nodeIDF[Identify - Asset Inventory per Level]
        nodePRF[Protect - Zone and Conduit Firewalls]
        nodeDEF[Detect - ICS-Aware IDS]
        nodeRSF[Respond - Safety Instrumented System]
        nodeRCF[Recover - Level 3 Historian Backup]
    end
    nodeL0 --- nodeIDF
    nodeL1 --- nodePRF
    nodeL2 --- nodeDEF
    nodeL3 --- nodeRSF
    nodeL3 --- nodeRCF
    nodeL4 -.->|Controlled Conduit| nodeL3
    nodeL5 -.->|Firewalled DMZ| nodeL4
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Which Standard to Adopt (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Need Cybersecurity Framework for ICS])
    nodeQ1{Is the plant a US critical infrastructure or Indian CEA regulated?}
    nodeQ2{Is ISO certification contractually required by clients?}
    nodeQ3{Does the organization have dedicated OT security staff?}
    nodePathA[Adopt NIST CSF and NIST SP 800-82r3]
    nodePathB[Adopt ISO IEC 27001 and ISO IEC 27019]
    nodePathC[Adopt CIS Controls starting at IG1]
    nodePathD[Adopt CIS Controls at IG2 or IG3]
    nodeStart --> nodeQ1
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodePathA
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ2
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodePathB
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeQ3
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodePathC
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodePathD
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern. Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice between two complete alternatives, typically with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks.

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**Q: Define the NIST Cybersecurity Framework and list its 5 (now 6) core functions.**

**Model Answer:**
The **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF)** is a voluntary, risk-based framework developed by the U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology to manage cybersecurity risk across critical infrastructure.

The 5 original core functions (v1.1) are:
1. **Identify** — Develop organizational understanding of systems, assets, and risks.
2. **Protect** — Implement appropriate safeguards.
3. **Detect** — Identify the occurrence of a cybersecurity event.
4. **Respond** — Take action on a detected incident.
5. **Recover** — Restore capabilities and services impaired by an incident.

In **v2.0 (February 2024)**, a sixth function **Govern (GV)** was added to emphasize cybersecurity governance, risk management strategy, and supply chain risk.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 5/6 functions correctly.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is an ISMS? How is it implemented using the PDCA cycle in ISO/IEC 27001?**

**Model Answer:**
An **ISMS (Information Security Management System)** is a systematic, risk-based approach to managing sensitive company information so that it remains secure, defined in **ISO/IEC 27001**.

The **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** implementation:
- **Plan:** Define the ISMS scope, conduct a risk assessment, and prepare the **Statement of Applicability (SoA)**.
- **Do:** Deploy the selected controls (Annex A), train personnel, and operate the ISMS.
- **Check:** Conduct internal audits and management reviews to monitor performance.
- **Act:** Take corrective actions to address non-conformities and ensure continual improvement.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for ISMS definition + 2 marks for PDCA explanation.)*

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: (a)** Explain the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework** in detail. Discuss its **5 core functions** and **4 implementation tiers** with specific reference to an **Industrial Control System (ICS)** environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **NIST SP 800-82r3** guideline. How does it adapt the CSF for **Operational Technology (OT)** environments? Mention the **Purdue Model** and **Zone-Conduit architecture**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **NIST CSF** is a policy framework of computer security guidance for how private sector organizations can assess and improve their ability to prevent, detect, and respond to cyber attacks. It provides a common language and systematic methodology for managing cyber risk.

**5 Core Functions (v1.1) and their ICS Application:**

| Function | Description | ICS Application Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Identify (ID)** | Catalog systems, assets, data, and capabilities | Inventory all PLCs (Siemens S7-1200), RTUs, HMIs, Engineering Workstations, historians |
| **Protect (PR)** | Implement safeguards | Configure firewalls between Level 3 and Level 4, enforce role-based access on HMI |
| **Detect (DE)** | Identify occurrence of an event | Deploy ICS-aware IDS to detect unauthorized Modbus/TCP writes |
| **Respond (RS)** | Take action on detection | Activate Safety Instrumented System (SIS), isolate affected VLAN |
| **Recover (RC)** | Restore impaired services | Restore historian from validated offline backup |

**4 Implementation Tiers:**

1. **Tier 1 — Partial:** Reactive, no organization-wide risk understanding.
2. **Tier 2 — Risk Informed:** Risk-informed policies exist but not enterprise-wide.
3. **Tier 3 — Repeatable:** Formal, regularly updated, organization-wide practices.
4. **Tier 4 — Adaptive:** Continuously improved, real-time awareness, integrated into culture.

In an ICS context, a Tier 4 plant would have **continuous threat hunting on OT protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850)** and integrate cyber defense with plant safety culture.

*(7 marks: 2 for CSF definition + 3 for 5 functions with OT examples + 2 for 4 tiers.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**NIST SP 800-82r3** ("Guide to Operational Technology (OT) System Security") is a specialized companion to the CSF. It provides ICS-specific recommendations for implementing the CSF in industrial environments where availability and safety are paramount.

**Key OT Adaptations:**

1. **CIA Triad Re-prioritization:** In IT, the order is **Confidentiality → Integrity → Availability**. In OT, it is reversed: **Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality**. A shutdown of a refinery costs crores; a leaked document does not.

2. **Purdue Model (ISA-95 / IEC 62443):** The reference architecture divides the plant network into 6 hierarchical levels:
   - Level 0: Process (sensors, actuators)
   - Level 1: Basic Control (PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers)
   - Level 2: Supervisory (HMI, SCADA)
   - Level 3: Operations (MES, historians, control servers)
   - Level 4: Enterprise (business network, ERP)
   - Level 5: Internet/External

3. **Zone-Conduit Architecture (IEC 62443):** Logical grouping of physical and logical assets (zones) that share common security requirements, with communication paths between zones (conduits) protected by firewalls, IDSs, and access controls.

4. **Practical Recommendations:** Use unidirectional gateways (data diodes) for historian traffic, never connect Level 0/1 directly to the internet, and schedule patches only during planned plant shutdowns.

*(7 marks: 2 for SP 800-82 purpose + 2 for CIA re-prioritization + 2 for Purdue Model + 1 for Zone-Conduit.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: (a)** Discuss the **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** standard. Explain the **PDCA cycle**, the structure of **Annex A controls** (93 controls in 4 themes), and the role of the **Statement of Applicability (SoA)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What are the **CIS Critical Security Controls v8**? Explain the **18 controls** and the **3 Implementation Groups (IG1, IG2, IG3)**. How would you select the appropriate IG for a critical infrastructure plant? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**ISO/IEC 27001:2022** is the international standard that specifies the requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and continually improving an **Information Security Management System (ISMS)** within the context of an organization.

**Clauses 4–10 (Mandatory for Certification):**
- Clause 4: Context of the organization
- Clause 5: Leadership
- Clause 6: Planning (risk assessment & treatment)
- Clause 7: Support (resources, competence, awareness, communication)
- Clause 8: Operation
- Clause 9: Performance evaluation
- Clause 10: Improvement

**The PDCA Cycle (Applied to ISMS):**

- **Plan:** Establish ISMS scope, define risk criteria, conduct risk assessment, select controls, and produce the **SoA**.
- **Do:** Implement the controls from Annex A, conduct training, manage operations.
- **Check:** Monitor, measure, conduct internal audits, and perform management reviews.
- **Act:** Address non-conformities, take corrective actions, and pursue continual improvement.

**Annex A Controls (2022 Revision — 93 Controls in 4 Themes):**

| Theme | Count | Example Controls |
|---|---|---|
| Organizational (A.5) | 37 | A.5.7 Threat intelligence, A.5.19 Information security in supplier relationships, A.5.24 Information security incident management planning |
| People (A.6) | 8 | A.6.3 Awareness & training, A.6.8 Disciplinary process |
| Physical (A.7) | 14 | A.7.2 Physical entry, A.7.13 Equipment maintenance |
| Technological (A.8) | 34 | A.8.5 Secure authentication, A.8.7 Protection against malware, A.8.22 Segregation of networks, A.8.28 Secure coding |

**Statement of Applicability (SoA):** A mandatory document that lists **every Annex A control**, states whether it is **applicable** or **not applicable**, justifies the exclusion of any control, and describes the **implementation status** of each applicable control. It is the auditable proof of the organization's risk treatment decisions.

*(7 marks: 2 for ISO 27001 introduction + 2 for PDCA + 2 for Annex A structure + 1 for SoA.)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**CIS Critical Security Controls v8** (released May 2021 by the Center for Internet Security) are a **prioritized, prescriptive, and technically focused** set of 18 cybersecurity actions that defend against the most prevalent real-world attacks.

**The 18 CIS Controls (Top-level):**
1. Inventory of Enterprise Assets
2. Inventory of Software Assets
3. Data Protection
4. Secure Configuration
5. Account Management
6. Access Control Management
7. Continuous Vulnerability Management
8. Audit Log Management
9. Email and Web Browser Protections
10. Malware Defenses
11. Data Recovery
12. Network Infrastructure Management
13. Network Monitoring and Defense
14. Security Awareness and Skills Training
15. Service Provider Management
16. Application Software Security
17. Incident Response Management
18. Penetration Testing

**3 Implementation Groups (IGs):**

| IG | Target Organization | Safeguard Count | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **IG1** | Small businesses with limited IT/OT | 56 (essential) | Local pharmacy, small workshop |
| **IG2** | Mid-size with regulated data | 130 (IG1 + 74) | Regional hospital, district water utility |
| **IG3** | Large/enterprise, critical infrastructure | 153 (IG1+IG2 + 23) | Power plant, national telecom |

**Selection Logic for a Critical Infrastructure Plant:**

For a **critical infrastructure plant** (e.g., a hydro-electric station, oil refinery, or water treatment facility), the selection is mandatory:
1. The plant is by definition a **national critical asset** → **IG3** is non-negotiable.
2. **IG1 must be 100% implemented** before IG2 is started, and IG2 before IG3, following the cumulative model.
3. Special **OT overlays** must be applied: e.g., CIS 1 (asset inventory) must include all Level 0–3 Purdue devices; CIS 8 (log management) must include Modbus/DNP3 logs; CIS 12 (network management) must implement Purdue-level segmentation.
4. The 18 controls are not equally applicable to OT: e.g., CIS 9 (email/web protections) is less relevant to Level 0–1 but vital at Level 3–4.

*(7 marks: 2 for 18 controls list + 2 for 3 IGs + 3 for critical infrastructure selection logic with OT overlays.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 
> 1. **Do NOT confuse CSF v1.1 (5 functions) with CSF v2.0 (6 functions).** The new **"Govern"** function was added in February 2024. The 2024 Scheme syllabus may ask for either; clarify the version in your answer.
> 2. **Do NOT state "ISO 27001 is a technical controls list."** It is a **management system standard** that *uses* Annex A as a control catalog. This is a frequent 1–2 mark deduction.
> 3. **Do NOT mix IT CIA priority with OT CIA priority.** The reversed order (**Availability → Integrity → Confidentiality**) in OT is a KTU-favorite trick question worth 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Do NOT skip the Statement of Applicability (SoA).** A 14-mark answer that omits the SoA in part (a) typically loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Do NOT write generic answers for "what is a standard."** Every sentence must reference **NIST, ISO/IEC 27001, or CIS** by name, with the specific version or clause number where possible.
> 6. **Do NOT list CIS Top 20 (v7).** v8 has exactly **18 controls** and is the current version.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **NIST CSF v2.0** has **6 functions**: **Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover** (GV-IDPRR).
- [ ] **NIST CSF Tiers** are 4 levels: **Partial, Risk Informed, Repeatable, Adaptive** (1 → 4).
- [ ] **NIST SP 800-82r3** is the **OT-specific overlay** of the CSF; it adopts the **Purdue Model (Levels 0–5)** and **Zone-Conduit architecture** (IEC 62443).
- [ ] **CIA Triad in OT** is reversed: **Availability > Integrity > Confidentiality** (opposite of IT).
- [ ] **ISO/IEC 27001:2022** is a **management system standard**, not a controls list. It uses the **PDCA** cycle.
- [ ] **ISO 27001:2022** has **93 Annex A controls** in **4 themes** (Organizational-37, People-8, Physical-14, Technological-34).
- [ ] **Statement of Applicability (SoA)** is the **mandatory auditable document** that lists every Annex A control, applicability, and justification for any exclusion.
- [ ] **ISO/IEC 27019** is the **energy utility sector**-specific extension of ISO 27001 (relevant to KSEB, KWWA).
- [ ] **CIS Controls v8** has exactly **18 top-level controls** and **153 total safeguards** across 3 IGs.
- [ ] **CIS IG1 = 56 safeguards** (small business), **IG2 = 130 safeguards** (mid-size), **IG3 = 153 safeguards** (critical infrastructure / large enterprise).
- [ ] **CIS Controls are cumulative**: an organization at IG3 must satisfy **all 153** safeguards, not just the 23 added at IG3.
- [ ] **Standards complement each other**: NIST (risk philosophy) + ISO 27001 (management certification) + CIS (tactical checklist) → the recommended "**defense-in-depth standards stack**" for ICS security.
- [ ] **Key numeric constants for KTU**: **6** CSF functions, **4** Tiers, **93** ISO controls, **4** ISO themes, **18** CIS controls, **3** IGs, **153** total CIS safeguards, **6** Purdue levels.
- [ ] **Common exam traps**: confusing IT/OT CIA order, citing old CSF (5 vs 6 functions), omitting SoA, listing old CIS Top 20.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Access Control Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — ACCESS CONTROL MECHANISMS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Access Control** is the foundational security mechanism in Industrial Control Systems (ICS) that mediates every interaction between a *subject* (user, process, device) and an *object* (resource, file, sensor, actuator, PLC register, SCADA tag). It enforces the **CIA triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability)** by regulating *who* can do *what*, *when*, *where*, and *how*, against *which* resource, based on a pre-defined security policy.

In the formal RFC 4949 / NIST SP 800-53 vocabulary adopted by KTU's PECCT414 curriculum:

> **"Access Control is the process of granting or denying specific requests for obtaining and using information and/or related information-processing services; and granting or denying access to specific physical resources (buildings, rooms, ICS cabinets)."**

The three canonical primitives that drive every access control decision are:

1. **Authentication (AuthN)** — *Who are you?* Establishing the identity of the subject with high assurance.
2. **Authorization (AuthZ)** — *What are you allowed to do?* Mapping the authenticated identity to a set of permitted operations.
3. **Accountability (Audit)** — *What did you actually do?* Logging the action to a tamper-resistant audit trail for forensic and regulatory compliance (IEC 62443, NERC-CIP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The AAA Triad (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting):**
> In Industrial Control System Security, **Access Control is NOT just authorization.** A model answer that only defines Access Control as "checking if a user has permission" will lose marks. The KTU 2024 scheme expects the integrated **AAA** view, because in OT/ICS environments (think SCADA, DCS, PLCs), regulatory frameworks like **IEC 62443-3-3** mandate that every command issued to a controller must be authenticated, authorized, and recorded.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The ICS Plant as a Secure Building"

Imagine a large pharmaceutical plant (a real ICS environment) divided into restricted zones:

- The **lobby** is open to anyone — anyone can read the public brochure (analogous to *public read access* on a SCADA HMI overview screen).
- The **reactor room** requires an **ID badge** (Authentication via smart card / RADIUS).
- The **control panel** inside that room requires a second factor — a PIN typed into a HMI panel (Multi-Factor Authentication, MFA).
- Even with the badge + PIN, a junior operator can only **press the START button** (authorization is constrained by RBAC role: "Operator").
- An engineer can **tune setpoints and override interlocks** (role: "Engineer" with elevated permissions).
- Every door open, every button press is logged to a central security desk with timestamp and video footage (Audit/Accounting, often enforced via SIEM and immutable logs).

Now substitute the building with a **DCS network**: the badge becomes an X.509 certificate, the PIN becomes a Kerberos ticket, the role becomes an RBAC permission, and the video footage becomes the audit log sent to a SIEM. **Access Control is the entire system of locks, badges, role checks, and CCTV — not just the lock itself.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> - **Authentication** = Showing your ID.
> - **Authorization** = The door only opens if your role is on the list.
> - **Accounting** = The camera records that you walked in at 14:32:07 and pressed Button-7.

### 1.3 Subjects, Objects, and Access Rights — The Atomic Triad

Every access control decision, regardless of model, reduces to a triplet:

| Element | Definition | ICS Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject (S)** | An active entity requesting access. | Engineer laptop, HMI session, Modbus master, PLC ladder-logic subroutine. |
| **Object (O)** | A passive resource being protected. | A PLC tag (e.g., `PumpSpeed_SP`), an HMI screen, a controller firmware image. |
| **Access Right (A)** | The permitted mode of interaction. | `Read`, `Write`, `Execute`, `Delete`, `Acknowledge`, `Override`. |

The access decision is therefore a function:

$$ \text{Decision} = f(\text{Subject attributes}, \text{Object attributes}, \text{Requested right}, \text{Context}) $$

where the output is one of `ALLOW`, `DENY`, or `STEP-UP` (require additional authentication, common in ICS for dangerous setpoint writes).

### 1.4 Why Access Control is the Hardest Problem in ICS

Unlike IT, ICS environments suffer from three structural complications:

1. **Legacy devices** (e.g., Modbus/RTU, S7comm) often have **no native authentication** — the protocol itself assumes a trusted serial cable. Access control must therefore be enforced at the *network perimeter* or via protocol-aware gateways.
2. **Availability is paramount**: An overly aggressive lockout policy can crash a process. This forces a balance between **safety, availability, and security**.
3. **Regulatory scope**: Standards like **IEC 62443-3-3** define Security Level Targets (SL-T 1 through 4) and map every system requirement (SR) to a specific access control mechanism (e.g., SR 2.1 — Authorization Enforcement).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Access Control Decision Pipeline

Every modern industrial access control system funnels a request through a deterministic pipeline:

1. **Identity Assertion** — The subject presents a credential (password, certificate, biometric, hardware token).
2. **Authentication Verification** — The credential is validated against an Identity Provider (IdP), Active Directory, RADIUS, or TACACS+ server.
3. **Session Establishment** — A short-lived token (Kerberos TGT, OAuth 2.0 JWT, SAML assertion) is issued.
4. **Policy Retrieval** — The PEP (Policy Enforcement Point) consults the PDP (Policy Decision Point) to obtain the authorization verdict.
5. **Decision** — `ALLOW` / `DENY` / `STEP-UP` based on subject attributes, object sensitivity label, and environmental context (time of day, source IP, geofence, device posture).
6. **Audit Logging** — A signed, immutable log entry is written to the SIEM.
7. **Periodic Re-evaluation** — Long-lived sessions are periodically re-checked (especially critical for engineering workstations with persistent sessions to PLCs).

## 2.2 The Four Classical Access Control Models

These four models form the **unavoidable core** of every KTU question on this topic.

### 2.2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

- **Definition:** The *owner* of an object decides who can access it. ACLs (Access Control Lists) are the canonical mechanism.
- **ICS Example:** A plant administrator grants `READ` permission on a specific HMI screen to a contract engineer.
- **Pros:** Flexible, easy to implement.
- **Cons:** Vulnerable to *Trojan horse* attacks; no centralized policy enforcement. **Not preferred for safety-critical ICS zones.**

### 2.2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

- **Definition:** Access is determined by *security labels* (e.g., `UNCLASSIFIED < CONFIDENTIAL < SECRET < TOP SECRET`) assigned to both subjects and objects by a central authority, not the owner. The **Bell-LaPadula (confidentiality)** and **Biba (integrity)** models are the canonical implementations.
- **ICS Example:** A Safety Instrumented System (SIS) labeled `SAFETY-CRITICAL` cannot be written to by a subject labeled `OPERATOR`.
- **Pros:** Strong, centralized, resistant to insider threats.
- **Cons:** Rigid; difficult to retrofit into legacy OT.

### 2.2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

- **Definition:** Permissions are assigned to *roles*, and roles are assigned to users. The NIST RBAC standard (INCITS 359-2012) defines four components: Core RBAC, Hierarchical RBAC, Constrained RBAC, and Symmetric RBAC.
- **ICS Example:** Roles such as `Operator`, `Engineer`, `Maintenance`, `Auditor`, `Vendor-Read-Only` are defined, each with a tightly scoped permission set.
- **Pros:** Aligns perfectly with organizational structure; minimizes permission creep.
- **Cons:** Role explosion in large plants; static role definitions do not capture context (e.g., "is this engineer on-call right now?").

### 2.2.4 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

- **Definition:** Access decisions are computed dynamically from *attributes of the subject, object, action, and environment* (the XACML standard model: `<Subject, Resource, Action, Environment>`).
- **ICS Example:** *"Allow Engineer role to write to Pump-3 setpoint, BUT ONLY if (a) source IP is in the engineering VLAN, (b) current time is within the planned maintenance window, and (c) the device posture check shows the laptop is patched and encrypted."*
- **Pros:** Extremely expressive, context-aware, future-proof.
- **Cons:** Computationally heavier, requires mature policy management (PAP — Policy Administration Point).

### 2.3 The Hybrid Model Dominating Modern ICS

In real industrial deployments, the trend is to use **RBAC as the primary mechanism, with ABAC layered on top for context-sensitive enforcement**, and MAC-like labels at the zone level (Purdue Levels 0–5, or IEC 62443 Security Zones and Conduits). This hybrid is sometimes called **"RBAC + ABAC with MAC zones"** and is the de-facto best practice cited in IEC 62443-3-3.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the key quantitative and conceptual formulas that students must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE. **No pipes inside table cells** — using `\vert` and `\mid` where required.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol Glossary | ICS Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Access Decision Function | $D(s,o,r) \in \{ \text{ALLOW}, \text{DENY}, \text{STEP-UP} \}$ | $s$ = subject, $o$ = object, $r$ = requested right | Core evaluation primitive for any PEP. |
| 2 | Bell-LaPadula — No Read Up (NRU) | $\text{level}(s) \geq \text{level}(o)$ for READ | $\text{level}(\cdot)$ = clearance / classification | Protects confidentiality in MAC ICS zones. |
| 3 | Bell-LaPadula — No Write Down (NWD) | $\text{level}(s) \leq \text{level}(o)$ for WRITE | Same as above | Prevents leakage to lower-classification objects. |
| 4 | Biba — No Read Down (NRD) | $\text{integrity}(s) \leq \text{integrity}(o)$ for READ | $\text{integrity}(\cdot)$ = integrity label | Protects SIS integrity in safety-critical control. |
| 5 | Biba — No Write Up (NWU) | $\text{integrity}(s) \geq \text{integrity}(o)$ for WRITE | Same as above | Prevents low-integrity data from poisoning high-integrity controllers. |
| 6 | RBAC Permission Set | $P = \bigcup_{r \in \text{Roles}(u)} \text{Perms}(r)$ | $u$ = user, $r$ = role, $P$ = effective permission set | Computes effective rights for an engineering user. |
| 7 | ABAC Decision Tuple | $\langle s, o, a, e \rangle \xrightarrow{\text{Rules}} D$ | $s$ subject, $o$ object, $a$ action, $e$ environment | Full XACML decision tuple. |
| 8 | Access Matrix | $M[i][j] = \text{Rights}(s_i, o_j)$ | $M$ = state of the protection system | Formal model underlying DAC. |
| 9 | Capability Token | $\text{Cap} = \langle O, R, \tau \rangle$ | $O$ object set, $R$ right set, $\tau$ expiration | Used in capability-based ICS architectures. |
| 10 | Defense-in-Depth Layers | $L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} L_k$ where $L_k \in [0,1]$ | $L_k$ = efficacy of layer $k$, $N$ = number of layers | Justifies multi-layer access control in ICS. |
| 11 | Strength of Authentication (SoA) | $\text{SoA} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{SoA}_i$ | Product of per-factor strength | MFA scoring for IEC 62443 SR 1.7. |
| 12 | IEC 62443 SL-T Requirement | $\text{SL-T} \in \{1,2,3,4\}$ mapped per Zone | Targets — SL-T 1: casual, SL-T 4: nation-state | Drives depth of access control per ICS zone. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap Question Pattern:**
> Examiners love to ask: *"Can a subject with SECRET clearance WRITE to an UNCLASSIFIED object under Bell-LaPadula?"* The answer is **NO** (the \*-property, NWD). Many students write YES thinking "higher can do anything," losing the full 7 marks.

### 2.5 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Power grid substation automation (IEC 61850):** Uses RBAC with strict role separation between Protection Engineer and Operator, enforced via centralized AAA (RADIUS + TACACS+).
- **Pharmaceutical batch control (ISA-88 / ISA-95):** Enforces RBAC with ABAC where the *recipe phase* is an attribute — only certain phases allow certain writes.
- **Smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Uses ABAC to gate machine-to-machine (M2M) communications based on device identity, firmware version, and operational mode.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Although Access Control is largely conceptual, KTU 2024 Scheme questions frequently demand **formal derivations** of (a) access decision logic, (b) Bell-LaPadula enforcement algebra, and (c) RBAC/ABAC permission composition. The following derivations are exhaustive, step-by-step, and aligned with KTU valuation key patterns.

## 3.1 Derivation: Bell-LaPadula Mandatory Access Model

The Bell-LaPadula (BLP) model enforces **confidentiality** using two formal properties. We will derive both properties from first principles.

### 3.1.1 The Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU)

**Premise:** In a confidentiality-oriented system, a subject $s$ must not gain knowledge from a higher-classified object $o$.

**Step 1 — Define the clearance and classification ordering.**

Let $L: \mathbb{E} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ be a strictly monotone function mapping any entity (subject or object) to a numeric security level, where higher numbers indicate greater sensitivity.

$$
L(\text{UNCLASSIFIED}) = 1, \quad L(\text{CONFIDENTIAL}) = 2, \quad L(\text{SECRET}) = 3, \quad L(\text{TOP SECRET}) = 4
$$

**Step 2 — State the read authorization rule.**

A subject $s$ is permitted to READ object $o$ if and only if:

$$
\text{READ}(s, o) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; L(s) \geq L(o)
$$

**Step 3 — Translate to plain English.**

"The subject's clearance must be at least as high as the object's classification."

**Step 4 — Counter-example (which a student must be able to produce in the exam).**

If a user with clearance `CONFIDENTIAL` (`L = 2`) attempts to read a document classified `SECRET` (`L = 3`):

$$
L(s) = 2 < 3 = L(o) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{READ}(s,o) = \text{DENY}
$$

This is the **No Read Up (NRU)** or *ss-property*.

### 3.1.2 The \*-Property (No Write Down — NWD)

**Premise:** A high-clearance subject must not "leak" information to a lower-classified object by writing to it.

**Step 5 — State the write authorization rule.**

A subject $s$ is permitted to WRITE to object $o$ if and only if:

$$
\text{WRITE}(s, o) \;\Longleftrightarrow\; L(s) \leq L(o)
$$

**Step 6 — Worked example for the exam.**

A subject with `TOP SECRET` clearance (`L = 4`) attempts to write a file with classification `UNCLASSIFIED` (`L = 1`):

$$
L(s) = 4 > 1 = L(o) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{WRITE}(s,o) = \text{DENY}
$$

This enforces **No Write Down (NWD)**. The combination NRU + NWD prevents both *direct leakage* and *indirect covert-channel leakage*.

### 3.1.3 The Tranquility and ds-Property

**Step 7 — Define tranquility.** BLP assumes classification labels do not change during a session unless specific non-interference conditions hold. This is called the **Tranquility Principle**:

$$
\forall t \in [t_0, t_1], \quad L(s(t)) = L(s(t_0)) \quad \wedge \quad L(o(t)) = L(o(t_0))
$$

If this holds, the system is **tranquil**, and BLP's state-transition rules apply without violating confidentiality.

## 3.2 Derivation: RBAC Permission Composition

### 3.2.1 Effective Permission Set for a User

**Premise:** A user $u$ in RBAC inherits the union of all permissions of all roles assigned to $u$, minus those revoked by constraints.

**Step 1 — Define the role assignment relation.**

$$
\text{UA} \subseteq U \times R
$$

where $U$ is the user set and $R$ is the role set.

**Step 2 — Define the permission assignment relation.**

$$
\text{PA} \subseteq R \times P
$$

where $P$ is the permission set, each $p \in P$ being an (operation, object) pair.

**Step 3 — Derive the effective permission set.**

The effective permission set of user $u$ is the union of all permissions of all roles $r$ such that $(u, r) \in \text{UA}$:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(u) \;=\; \bigcup_{(u,r) \in \text{UA}} \bigl\{ p \in P \;:\; (r, p) \in \text{PA} \bigr\}
$$

**Step 4 — Example calculation.**

Given:

$$
\text{UA} = \{ (u_1, r_1),\; (u_1, r_2) \}
$$

$$
\text{PA} = \{ (r_1, \text{READ-SCREEN-A}),\; (r_2, \text{WRITE-SETPOINT-X}) \}
$$

Then:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(u_1) = \{\text{READ-SCREEN-A},\; \text{WRITE-SETPOINT-X}\}
$$

**Step 5 — Add Static Separation of Duties (SSoD) constraint.**

A constraint $\text{SSoD}$ specifies a pair of mutually exclusive roles. The constrained effective permission set is:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}^{\text{constr}}(u) \;=\; P_{\text{eff}}(u) \;\setminus\; \bigcup_{(r_a, r_b) \in \text{SSoD},\; (u,r_b) \in \text{UA}} \text{Perms}(r_b)
$$

This enforces, for example, that the same user cannot be both `Engineer` and `Approver` (to prevent one person from creating and approving a dangerous setpoint change).

## 3.3 Derivation: ABAC Decision Using XACML Tuple Algebra

### 3.3.1 The Formal XACML Decision Request

A decision request is a tuple:

$$
\mathcal{R} \;=\; \langle s,\; o,\; a,\; e \rangle
$$

where each element is a set of attribute–value pairs:

$$
s = \{ \text{role}=\text{Engineer},\; \text{dept}=\text{ProcessControl},\; \text{clearanceLevel}=3 \}
$$

$$
o = \{ \text{id}=\text{Pump-3-SP},\; \text{zone}=\text{Level-2-Cell},\; \text{sensitivity}=3 \}
$$

$$
a = \{ \text{id}=\text{write},\; \text{protocol}=\text{Modbus-TCP} \}
$$

$$
e = \{ \text{time}=\text{2024-08-15T02:30:00Z},\; \text{sourceVLAN}=\text{ENG-VLAN-10},\; \text{devicePosture}=\text{PATCHED} \}
$$

### 3.3.2 The Policy Rule Evaluation

A rule $P_k$ is a boolean function over the tuple:

$$
P_k(s,o,a,e) : \mathbb{A}^4 \rightarrow \{\text{TRUE}, \text{FALSE}\}
$$

**Step 1 — Compose the rule.**

$$
P_1 : (\text{role} = \text{Engineer}) \wedge (\text{sensitivity} \leq \text{clearanceLevel}) \wedge (\text{protocol} \in \{\text{Modbus-TCP}, \text{OPC-UA}\}) \wedge (\text{time} \in \text{MaintenanceWindow})
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $P_1$ on the sample tuple.**

Substitute:

$$
P_1 = (\text{Engineer} = \text{Engineer}) \wedge (3 \leq 3) \wedge (\text{Modbus-TCP} \in \{\text{Modbus-TCP}, \text{OPC-UA}\}) \wedge (\text{02:30} \in [02:00, 04:00])
$$

All four conjuncts are TRUE, so $P_1(s,o,a,e) = \text{TRUE}$.

**Step 3 — Combine rules using the XACML combining algorithm.**

The standard combining algorithm is *deny-overrides*:

$$
D(\mathcal{R}) = 
\begin{cases}
\text{PERMIT} & \text{if } \exists k: P_k(\mathcal{R}) = \text{TRUE} \text{ and no } k' \text{ returns DENY} \\
\text{DENY} & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4 — Final verdict.**

$$
D(\mathcal{R}) = \text{PERMIT}
$$

The write to `Pump-3-SP` is permitted.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Model for ICS Zones

| ICS Zone (Purdue / IEC 62443) | Recommended Primary Model | Secondary Model | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Level 0–1 (Field devices, sensors) | MAC (device labels) | Hardware-enforced capabilities | Strict integrity for safety-critical I/O. |
| Level 2 (Area Supervisory Control) | RBAC | MAC labels for critical assets | Aligns with operational role structure. |
| Level 3 (Plant Operations / SCADA) | RBAC + ABAC | Step-up auth for writes | Balance between usability and context-sensitivity. |
| Level 3.5 (DMZ / Industrial DMZ) | ABAC | RBAC for partner access | Inter-organization access must be context-aware. |
| Level 4–5 (Enterprise IT) | RBAC | ABAC for sensitive HR/finance | Mature IdP infrastructure available. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Derivation — Why "Defense in Depth" Mathematically Reduces Risk:**
> Suppose each independent access control layer $L_k$ independently blocks a fraction $1 - L_k$ of attacks (where $L_k \in [0,1]$ is the layer's efficacy). The probability that an attack passes through **all** $N$ layers is:
>
> $$
> P_{\text{breach}} \;=\; \prod_{k=1}^{N} (1 - L_k)
> $$
>
> For $N=3$ layers each with $L_k = 0.7$:
>
> $$
> P_{\text{breach}} = (0.3)^3 = 0.027 \quad \text{(a 97.3\% reduction compared to a single layer at 0.7 efficacy of 30%)}
> $$
>
> This numerical proof is a **high-yield KTU answer** for any "justify defense in depth" question.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Generic Access Control Decision Flow

The following block-level functional architecture maps every interaction in an ICS access control pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subject: Engineer Laptop / HMI Session / PLC Logic] --> B[Identity Assertion: Username + Password + Smart Card]
    B --> C{Authentication: Valid Credential?}
    C -- No --> X1[DENY + Audit Log Entry + Optional Lockout]
    C -- Yes --> D[Issue Session Token: Kerberos TGT / OAuth JWT]
    D --> E[Policy Enforcement Point: PEP on HMI Gateway / Jump Server]
    E --> F[PDP: Policy Decision Point consults XACML Rules]
    F --> G{Evaluate ABAC Tuple: Subject + Object + Action + Environment}
    G -- DENY --> X2[DENY + Step-Up Auth Prompt + Audit]
    G -- STEP-UP --> X3[Require MFA: OTP / Biometric]
    X3 --> G
    G -- PERMIT --> H[Command Routed to PLC / RTU / Sensor]
    H --> I[Audit Logging to SIEM: Who, What, When, Where, Why]
    I --> J[Periodic Re-Evaluation: Re-check policy for long sessions]
    J --> H
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — RBAC Hierarchy in a Typical ICS Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SuperAdmin[Super Administrator] --> PlantMgr[Plant Manager]
    PlantMgr --> ProcessEng[Process Engineer]
    PlantMgr --> MaintenanceEng[Maintenance Engineer]
    PlantMgr --> SafetyEng[Safety Engineer]
    ProcessEng --> Operator1[Senior Operator]
    ProcessEng --> Operator2[Junior Operator]
    MaintenanceEng --> VendorReadOnly[Vendor Read-Only Access]
    SafetyEng --> Auditor[Compliance Auditor]
    Operator1 --> ReadHMI[Read HMI Screens]
    Operator1 --> AckAlarm[Acknowledge Alarms]
    Operator2 --> ReadHMI
    ProcessEng --> TuneSP[Tune Setpoints]
    ProcessEng --> OverrideInterlock[Override Interlocks with Step-Up]
    MaintenanceEng --> FWUpdate[Firmware Update PLC]
    SafetyEng --> ReadSIS[Read SIS Logs]
    SafetyEng --> ModifySIL[Modify SIL Configuration]
    Auditor --> ReadAudit[Read-Only Audit Logs]
    VendorReadOnly --> ReadVendorData[Read Equipment Diagnostics Only]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Bell-LaPadula Confidentiality Lattice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TS[TOP SECRET Level 4] --> S[SECRET Level 3]
    S --> C[CONFIDENTIAL Level 2]
    C --> U[UNCLASSIFIED Level 1]
    TS -.NO READ UP.- TS
    TS -.NO WRITE DOWN.- TS
    S -.NO READ UP.- S
    S -.NO WRITE DOWN.- S
    C -.NO READ UP.- C
    C -.NO WRITE DOWN.- C
    U -.NO READ UP.- U
    U -.NO WRITE DOWN.- U
    Note1[Read flows upward only: clearance level must be greater or equal]
    Note2[Write flows downward only: subject clearance must be less or equal]
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — IEC 62443 Security Zone and Conduit Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z1[Security Zone: Safety Instrumented System SL-T 4] --> C1[Conduit: Encrypted VPN with Mutual Auth and RBAC]
    Z2[Security Zone: Basic Control DCS SL-T 2] --> C1
    Z3[Security Zone: SCADA HMI SL-T 2] --> C2[Conduit: Firewalled with ABAC and Logging]
    Z4[Security Zone: Engineering Workstation SL-T 3] --> C2
    Z5[Security Zone: Enterprise IT SL-T 1] --> C3[Conduit: Industrial DMZ with Reverse Proxy and IDMZ]
    C1 --> Z3
    C2 --> Z4
    C3 --> Z5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for KTU 2024:**
> When asked *"Sketch the access control architecture of an ICS plant,"* always include at least **three zones** (SIS, DCS, Enterprise), **labeled conduits**, and explicit **SL-T targets**. Examiners award marks for the boundary controls, not just the zone boxes.
