# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Authentication and Authorization in the context of Industrial Control System security. Why is the distinction critical for SCADA environments?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Authentication (1 mark):** The process of *verifying the identity* of a subject requesting access. Examples: password, smart card, biometric, X.509 certificate, Kerberos ticket. In SCADA, this prevents an unauthenticated remote attacker from issuing commands to a PLC.
- **Authorization (1 mark):** The process of *determining what an already-authenticated subject is allowed to do*. Examples: RBAC role check, ACL lookup, ABAC rule evaluation. In SCADA, this prevents a legitimate operator from accidentally (or maliciously) writing to a Safety Instrumented System.
- **Critical distinction in SCADA (1 mark):** In SCADA, an operator may be *authenticated* (they have a valid badge) but *unauthorized* to perform a specific write — e.g., changing a reactor setpoint outside their zone. Conflating the two would mean any logged-in user could control any asset, violating IEC 62443-3-3 SR 2.1 (Authorization Enforcement).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the Bell-LaPadula \*-property (star property)? Explain with one ICS-relevant example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** The \*-property, also called *No Write Down* (NWD), states that a subject $s$ is permitted to WRITE to object $o$ only if the security level of $s$ is less than or equal to the security level of $o$. Formally: $\text{WRITE}(s,o) \iff L(s) \leq L(o)$.
- **Purpose (0.5 marks):** Prevents high-clearance subjects from leaking classified information to lower-classified objects, including via covert channels.
- **ICS example (1 mark):** A control engineer with `CONFIDENTIAL` clearance (`L = 2`) operating a SCADA console in a `CONFIDENTIAL` zone must not be able to write tuning parameters into a sensor log file labeled `UNCLASSIFIED` (`L = 1`), because doing so would exfiltrate CONFIDENTIAL process data into a lower-labeled, more widely accessible file.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU ESE pattern: Each Part-B question on Module 3 carries **14 marks**, typically split into (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks, with **internal choice** between two alternatives.

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat diagrams, explain the **four classical access control models**: DAC, MAC, RBAC, and ABAC. Compare their suitability for securing a **SCADA-based power substation automation system** (IEC 61850). Identify which model is best for (i) operator HMI access, (ii) protection-engineer configuration writes, and (iii) vendor read-only diagnostics.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Define **Bell-LaPadula (BLP)** and **Biba** models. Derive the four key authorization rules (NRU, NWD, NRD, NWU) using formal notation. Apply them to the following scenario and show all steps:

> *"An engineer with SECRET clearance in Zone A writes a configuration to a controller labeled CONFIDENTIAL in Zone B. The same engineer then attempts to read an alarm log labeled TOP SECRET. Comment on whether each request is permitted."*

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) [7 Marks] — Comparison of the Four Models**

| Model | Mechanism | Pros | Cons | Best for Substation Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DAC** | Owner-set ACLs | Flexible, easy | Trojan-horse prone, no central control | Not preferred for safety-critical. |
| **MAC** | Central security labels + lattice | Strong, tamper-resistant | Rigid | Protection-engineer setpoint configuration (integrity-labeled). |
| **RBAC** | Roles → permissions | Aligns with org structure | Role explosion | **Operator HMI access** (i). |
| **ABAC** | Dynamic attribute evaluation | Context-aware, future-proof | Complex policy mgmt | **Protection-engineer config writes with step-up auth** (ii) and **vendor read-only diagnostics gated by time + IP** (iii). |

*(Each row earns ~1 mark; the conclusion earns 1 mark.)*

**(b) [7 Marks] — BLP and Biba Derivations**

**Step 1 — Define BLP (BLP enforces confidentiality, 1 mark):**

$$
\text{BLP} : L : \mathbb{E} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}, \quad \text{with } \leq \text{ partial order over labels.}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Biba (Biba enforces integrity, 1 mark):**

$$
\text{Biba} : I : \mathbb{E} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}, \quad \text{with } \leq \text{ partial order over integrity labels.}
$$

**Step 3 — State the four rules (2 marks, 0.5 each):**

$$
\text{NRU: } \text{READ}(s,o) \iff L(s) \geq L(o)
$$

$$
\text{NWD: } \text{WRITE}(s,o) \iff L(s) \leq L(o)
$$

$$
\text{NRD: } \text{READ}(s,o) \iff I(s) \leq I(o)
$$

$$
\text{NWU: } \text{WRITE}(s,o) \iff I(s) \geq I(o)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply to the scenario (2 marks).**

Given:
- Engineer $s$: $L(s) = 3$ (SECRET)
- Controller $o_1$: $L(o_1) = 2$ (CONFIDENTIAL)
- Alarm log $o_2$: $L(o_2) = 4$ (TOP SECRET)

*Request 1: Write to Controller ($o_1$):*

$$
L(s) = 3 \leq 2 = L(o_1) \;\; \text{?}
$$

This is **FALSE**. Therefore $\text{WRITE}(s, o_1) = \text{DENY}$ under BLP NWD. The write is rejected to prevent possible leakage of SECRET-level information into a CONFIDENTIAL-level object.

*Request 2: Read from Alarm Log ($o_2$):*

$$
L(s) = 3 \geq 4 = L(o_2) \;\; \text{?}
$$

This is **FALSE**. Therefore $\text{READ}(s, o_2) = \text{DENY}$ under BLP NRU. The engineer lacks sufficient clearance to read TOP SECRET material.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Both requests are denied. The engineer must escalate clearance or be granted a justified exception (with logged audit trail) for either operation. This demonstrates why high-trust ICS environments use MAC labels in addition to RBAC.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) framework** as applied to Industrial Control Systems. Describe how **RADIUS**, **TACACS+**, **Kerberos**, and **OAuth 2.0 / OIDC** protocols map to the AAA framework. Give one ICS deployment example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an **RBAC + ABAC access control policy** for a **distributed control system (DCS)** managing a chemical reactor with the following requirements:

1. Operators can read HMI screens at any time but cannot write setpoints.
2. Engineers can write setpoints only during the scheduled maintenance window (02:00–04:00) and only from the engineering VLAN.
3. Maintenance vendors have read-only access to equipment diagnostics, valid only for 30 days from the date their account is provisioned.
4. The Safety Engineer can override interlocks with a mandatory step-up authentication (password + OTP).
5. All actions must be logged to a SIEM with timestamp and source IP.

Provide:
- The RBAC role definitions.
- The ABAC rule expressions (one per requirement).
- A worked example showing a successful and a denied access attempt.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) [7 Marks] — AAA Framework with Protocols**

**Step 1 — Define AAA (1 mark):** Authentication (who are you), Authorization (what can you do), Accounting (what did you do). These three pillars together form the AAA framework, foundational to IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.x, SR 2.x, SR 6.x.

**Step 2 — Map each protocol (4 × 1 mark = 4 marks):**

| Protocol | Primary AAA Function | ICS Deployment Example |
|---|---|---|
| **RADIUS** | Authentication + Accounting (UDP-based) | Engineering Workstation Wi-Fi authentication in a refinery plant. |
| **TACACS+** | Full AAA with separate AAA packets (TCP, more secure) | Privileged access to SCADA jump hosts in a power grid substation. |
| **Kerberos** | Authentication + ticket-based authorization | Single Sign-On for HMI workstations in a DCS using Active Directory. |
| **OAuth 2.0 / OIDC** | Authorization delegation + identity layer | Vendor portal integration with plant Historian (cloud-hybrid). |

**Step 3 — Why AAA matters in ICS (2 marks):** In ICS, a single unauthenticated command could trigger a physical event (open a valve, trip a breaker). AAA ensures (i) only verified identities can issue commands, (ii) verified identities can only perform authorized actions, and (iii) all actions are traceable for forensic and regulatory compliance (e.g., NERC-CIP, IEC 62443).

**(b) [7 Marks] — RBAC + ABAC Policy Design**

**Step 1 — RBAC role definitions (2 marks):**

$$
R = \{ \text{Operator},\; \text{Engineer},\; \text{Vendor},\; \text{SafetyEngineer},\; \text{Auditor} \}
$$

Permission set $P$:
- $\text{Operator} \rightarrow \{ \text{READ-HMI},\; \text{ACK-ALARM} \}$
- $\text{Engineer} \rightarrow \{ \text{READ-HMI},\; \text{WRITE-SETPOINT},\; \text{READ-LOGS} \}$
- $\text{Vendor} \rightarrow \{ \text{READ-DIAGNOSTICS} \}$ with time-bound validity
- $\text{SafetyEngineer} \rightarrow \{ \text{OVERRIDE-INTERLOCK},\; \text{STEP-UP-AUTH} \}$
- $\text{Auditor} \rightarrow \{ \text{READ-ALL-LOGS},\; \text{READ-SIEM} \}$

**Step 2 — ABAC rule expressions (3 marks, one per numbered requirement):**

> *Requirement 1 (Operator HMI):*
> $$
> (\text{role}=\text{Operator}) \wedge (\text{action}=\text{READ}) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{PERMIT}
> $$
> (Always TRUE; no time/context constraint for reads.)

> *Requirement 2 (Engineer write setpoint):*
> $$
> (\text{role}=\text{Engineer}) \wedge (\text{action}=\text{WRITE-SETPOINT}) \wedge (\text{time} \in [02{:}00, 04{:}00]) \wedge (\text{sourceVLAN}=\text{ENG-VLAN-10}) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{PERMIT}
> $$

> *Requirement 3 (Vendor validity 30 days):*
> $$
> (\text{role}=\text{Vendor}) \wedge (\text{now} - \text{provisionedAt} \leq 30 \text{ days}) \wedge (\text{action}=\text{READ}) \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{PERMIT}
> $$

> *Requirement 4 (Safety Engineer override with step-up):*
> $$
> (\text{role}=\text{SafetyEngineer}) \wedge (\text{action}=\text{OVERRIDE-INTERLOCK}) \wedge \text{MFA(OTP)} \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{PERMIT}
> $$

> *Requirement 5 (Accounting — applies universally):*
> $$
> \forall \text{request} : \text{log}(\text{userId}, \text{object}, \text{action}, \text{timestamp}, \text{sourceIP}) \rightarrow \text{SIEM}
> $$

**Step 3 — Worked example (2 marks):**

*Successful attempt:*

Engineer $u_1$ with `role = Engineer`, source VLAN `ENG-VLAN-10`, attempts to write setpoint to `Reactor-Temp-SP` at 02:35 on 2024-08-15.

Evaluate:
- $\text{role}=\text{Engineer}$ ✓
- $\text{action}=\text{WRITE-SETPOINT}$ ✓
- $\text{time} = 02:35 \in [02:00, 04:00]$ ✓
- $\text{sourceVLAN}=\text{ENG-VLAN-10}$ ✓

Verdict: $\text{PERMIT}$. A SIEM log entry is created.

*Denied attempt:*

Same engineer $u_1$ attempts to write the same setpoint at 09:15 (outside maintenance window).

Evaluate:
- $\text{role}=\text{Engineer}$ ✓
- $\text{action}=\text{WRITE-SETPOINT}$ ✓
- $\text{time} = 09:15 \in [02:00, 04:00]$ ✗

Verdict: $\text{DENY}$. A SIEM log entry is still created (denials are auditable).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing AAA with just Authorization.** Always mention all three pillars. A model answer that only discusses RBAC without AAA loses at least **3 marks**.
> 2. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** — this **breaks** the table renderer. Use `\vert x \vert` instead. The KTU digital evaluation platform also parses tables strictly.
> 3. **Forgetting the \*-property** when discussing Bell-LaPadula. Mentioning only NRU and not NWD loses **2 marks** because both are required for confidentiality.
> 4. **Treating ABAC and RBAC as alternatives.** In modern ICS they are **complementary**. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for saying *"ABAC is better than RBAC."*
> 5. **Skipping the audit log** in any policy design. IEC 62443 mandates it; absence is treated as a missing requirement.
> 6. **Hand-waving "time windows" without specifying clock source.** Always anchor to UTC and mention NTP synchronization for ICS (IEC 62443 SR 2.4).
> 7. **Using Python or programming snippets** in the answer — KTU PECCT414 expects pure analytical and protocol-level responses, not code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3, Access Control Mechanisms**

- **AAA is mandatory.** Every access control discussion begins with Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting (Audit). The triple must appear in every KTU answer.
- **Subjects vs. Objects vs. Rights.** Subject (active), Object (passive), Right (mode of interaction). These are the atomic elements of *every* model.
- **DAC = owner-controlled** (ACLs); **MAC = centrally-labeled** (BLP, Biba); **RBAC = role-mediated** (NIST INCITS 359-2012); **ABAC = attribute-driven** (XACML `<Subject, Resource, Action, Environment>` tuple).
- **Bell-LaPadula (Confidentiality)** — NRU: $L(s) \geq L(o)$ for read; NWD (\*-property): $L(s) \leq L(o)$ for write.
- **Biba (Integrity)** — NRD: $I(s) \leq I(o)$ for read; NWU: $I(s) \geq I(o)$ for write.
- **RBAC effective permissions** = union of permissions of all assigned roles: $P_{\text{eff}}(u) = \bigcup_{r \in \text{Roles}(u)} \text{Perms}(r)$.
- **ABAC decision** is a function over the 4-tuple $\langle s, o, a, e \rangle$; combine rules with **deny-overrides** by default in safety-critical ICS.
- **Defense-in-Depth Mathematically Reduces Risk:** $P_{\text{breach}} = \prod_{k=1}^{N} (1 - L_k)$. Three 0.7-efficacy layers yield 97.3% attack reduction.
- **IEC 62443 SL-T 1–4** drives the strength of access control per ICS zone. SL-T 1 (casual) → SL-T 4 (nation-state adversary).
- **Purdue Levels 0–5** map physical zones; **Level 3.5 (IDMZ)** is the demilitarized zone between ICS and Enterprise IT, where heavy access-control filtering (industrial firewalls, jump servers, ABAC gateways) is mandatory.
- **Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)** is **non-negotiable** for privileged writes (engineer setpoints, safety overrides, firmware updates).
- **Audit logs are immutable and tamper-resistant** in any KTU-approved ICS access control design (SIEM, write-once storage, IEC 62443 SR 6.1).
- **Step-Up Authentication** = dynamically requiring additional proof for high-risk actions (e.g., OTP + password for interlock overrides).
- **Industrial protocols to remember:** Modbus (no native auth → perimeter control), OPC-UA (built-in cert-based auth + RBAC), IEC 61850 (RBAC + MMS), DNP3 Secure Authentication (v2+).
- **Comparison one-liners for exams:** *DAC = flexible but weak; MAC = strong but rigid; RBAC = standard; ABAC = future-proof.* Memorize these four lines.

---

> *End of Module 3 Notes — Access Control Mechanisms.*
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 / NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Authentication (Passwords, Multi-Factor Authentication)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** User Authentication (Passwords, Multi-Factor Authentication)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **User Authentication** is the security process by which an Industrial Control System (ICS) verifies the claimed identity of a user, device, or process attempting to gain access to control networks, HMIs (Human-Machine Interfaces), SCADA systems, PLCs, RTUs, and Engineering Workstations. It is the **first line of defense** in the Purdue Model's Level 1–3 zones, ensuring that only authorized personnel can issue control commands, modify setpoints, or alter Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) ladder logic.

Authentication establishes *who the entity is*, which is fundamentally different from:
- **Authorization** → *What they are allowed to do*
- **Accountability** → *What they actually did (auditing)*
- **Confidentiality / Integrity** → *Protecting data from unauthorized eyes or modification*

> [!NOTE]
> **NIST SP 800-63B** defines authenticators as something the user **knows** (password), **has** (token), **is** (biometric), or **does** (behavioral). In ICS environments, the *something you have* factor (hardware tokens, smart cards) is heavily favored over biometrics because biometric readers can be physically contaminated in industrial settings (dust, oil, gloves).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Three Locks on a Vault Door" Intuition

Imagine an industrial plant's control room is a **bank vault** storing gold bars (your process data, recipes, and proprietary control logic). Anyone wanting entry must prove identity:

1. **Password = A secret knock** → The guard whispers a code; if you know it, you pass.
2. **Token/Smart Card = A physical key** → You must also hand over a metal key.
3. **Biometric = A fingerprint scan** → Even with knock and key, the vault demands your thumbprint.

If you supply **any one** of these, the vault opens. If you supply **two or more** (Multi-Factor Authentication), the vault becomes exponentially harder to break — this is the **MFA principle**.

| Factor Category | Real-World Example | ICS Example |
|---|---|---|
| Knowledge (Type 1) | PIN, password | Operator login password to HMI |
| Possession (Type 2) | Hardware token, smart card | RSA SecurID for remote vendor access |
| Inherence (Type 3) | Fingerprint, iris scan | Plant gate biometric (rare in dusty zones) |
| Location/Behavior (Type 4) | GPS, typing rhythm | Geofencing for remote SCADA engineers |

## 1.3 Why Authentication is Critical in ICS (Not Just IT)

> [!WARNING]
> In **IT security**, a stolen password might leak emails. In **ICS security**, a stolen HMI password can cause a **physical explosion**, recall of contaminated product batches, or city-wide blackouts (recall the 2015 Ukraine BlackEnergy attack that used stolen VPN credentials to pivot into the SCADA network).

KTU students must remember that **ISA/IEC 62443** (the global ICS security standard) explicitly mandates **unique identification and authentication** for all human users accessing any zone of the Industrial Automation and Control System (IACS).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Authentication

### Pillar A: Password-Based Authentication (Single-Factor)

A password is a shared secret string (typically 6–14 characters in legacy ICS systems) compared against a **stored hash** in the authentication database.

**The Password Verification Flow:**
1. User submits username + plaintext password $P$ over the network.
2. Server computes $h = H(P \oplus \text{salt})$ using a cryptographic hash function $H$ (e.g., SHA-256).
3. Server compares $h$ with the stored hash $h_{\text{stored}}$.
4. If $h = h_{\text{stored}}$, access is granted; else rejected.

> [!NOTE]
> In legacy ICS protocols like **DNP3 Secure Authentication v2/v5** and **IEC 62351-7**, password handling is *primitive* — sometimes cleartext or simple XOR. This is a major vulnerability vector examiners love to test.

### Pillar B: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA / 2FA)

MFA combines **two or more independent factor types** to defeat the failure of any single factor. The factors must be from different categories (knowledge + possession, not password + security question — both are Type 1).

**MFA Verification Equation:**

$$
A_{\text{final}} = f(A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n) \quad \text{where} \quad f = \begin{cases} \text{AND} & \text{if all required} \\ \text{OR} & \text{optional layers} \end{cases}
$$

For **strict MFA (typical in ICS)**: $A_{\text{final}} = A_1 \land A_2 \land \ldots \land A_n$

### Pillar C: Cryptographic / Protocol-Based Authentication

Beyond simple passwords, ICS environments use protocol-level authentication:
- **CHAP (Challenge Handshake Authentication Protocol)** — used in PPP links to RTUs.
- **Kerberos** — ticket-based, used in Windows-based HMI stations.
- **RADIUS / TACACS+** — central authentication for remote engineers via VPN.
- **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI) with X.509 certificates** — used in IEC 62443 zone-to-zone mutual authentication.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following — these are the **exam-favorite metrics** for password strength and cracking-time calculations.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Password Entropy** | $E = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ bits | Where $L$ = length, $N$ = character pool size | Strength measurement |
| **Brute-Force Time** | $T = \dfrac{N^L}{R \cdot 2}$ | $R$ = guesses per second | Crack time estimation |
| **Dictionary Search Space** | $S_{\text{dict}} = \vert D \vert$ | $D$ = dictionary size | Defense justification |
| **Combined MFA Security** | $P_{\text{break}} = P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdot \ldots \cdot P_n$ | Probabilities of breaking each factor | Risk reduction proof |
| **Salting Benefit** | $h = H(P \,\|\, \text{salt})$ | Prevents rainbow table reuse | Storage hardening |
| **Bloom Filter False Positive** | $p \approx \bigl(1 - e^{-kn/m}\bigr)^k$ | Used in password blacklist check | Policy enforcement |

> [!CAUTION]
> **NEVER write** $N^L$ with vertical pipes in your answer sheet. Examiners auto-deduct marks for ambiguous notation. Use $N^{L}$ or $\vert N^L \vert$.

### 2.2.1 Character Pool Size ($N$) Reference Table

| Character Set Used | Pool Size ($N$) | $\log_2(N)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Digits only (0–9) | 10 | 3.32 |
| Lowercase letters (a–z) | 26 | 4.70 |
| Lowercase + Digits | 36 | 5.17 |
| Lowercase + Uppercase + Digits | 62 | 5.95 |
| Full ASCII printable | 95 | 6.57 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in ICS:** Plant IT teams use entropy to enforce password policy on the engineering workstation that downloads ladder logic to PLCs. Siemens, Rockwell, and Schneider all publish minimum entropy guidelines (typically $\geq$ 50 bits for safety-critical access).

## 2.3 Password Attacks — The Threat Model (Why MFA Exists)

| Attack | Mechanism | Counter |
|---|---|---|
| **Brute Force** | Try all $N^L$ combinations | Account lockout, rate limit, CAPTCHAs |
| **Dictionary** | Use a known wordlist $D$ | Blacklist common passwords |
| **Rainbow Table** | Pre-computed hash → plaintext | Use unique per-user salt |
| **Credential Stuffing** | Reuse leaked IT credentials | MFA + breach monitoring |
| **Phishing** | Trick user to reveal $P$ | MFA + user training |
| **Shoulder Surfing** | Observe operator typing | Privacy filters, biometrics |
| **Keylogger** | Malware on HMI captures keystrokes | Hardware token, OT segmentation |
| **Pass-the-Hash (PtH)** | Replay captured $h$ to server | Challenge-response protocols |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Password Entropy from First Principles

**Problem (KTU-typical):** *A plant operator's HMI password uses 8 characters drawn from a set of 62 alphanumeric symbols. Calculate the entropy and the maximum time to brute-force at a rate of $10^{10}$ guesses/sec.*

**Step 1: Identify Parameters**

Given:
- Length $L = 8$ characters
- Pool size $N = 62$ (lowercase + uppercase + digits)
- Hash rate $R = 10^{10}$ guesses/sec

**Step 2: Apply the Entropy Formula**

Entropy $E$ in bits measures the information content of one password:

$$
E = L \cdot \log_2(N)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
E = 8 \cdot \log_2(62)
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate $\log_2(62)$**

Using the change-of-base identity $\log_2(x) = \dfrac{\ln(x)}{\ln(2)}$:

$$
\log_2(62) = \frac{\ln(62)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.127}{0.6931} \approx 5.9542
$$

**Step 4: Compute Final Entropy**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 8 \times 5.9542 \\
E &\approx 47.63 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 47.63-bit password has the equivalent randomness of a 47.63-bit cryptographic key. NIST currently requires $\geq$ 30 bits for user-chosen passwords, so this 8-character alphanumeric password **passes** minimum policy, but KTU/ISA recommends $\geq$ 60 bits for ICS safety zones.

**Step 5: Compute Total Search Space**

The total number of possible passwords:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= N^L \\
S &= 62^8 \\
\log_{10}(S) &= 8 \cdot \log_{10}(62) = 8 \cdot 1.7924 = 14.339 \\
S &\approx 10^{14.339} \approx 2.18 \times 10^{14}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute Brute-Force Time (Average Case)**

On average, an attacker needs to try half the keyspace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{S}{2 \cdot R} \\
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2.18 \times 10^{14}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
T_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{2.18 \times 10^{14}}{2 \times 10^{10}} \\
T_{\text{avg}} &= 1.09 \times 10^{4} \text{ seconds} \\
T_{\text{avg}} &\approx 3.03 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Convert to Hours**

$$
T_{\text{avg}} \approx 3 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** A modern GPU rig at $10^{10}$ guesses/sec can crack this 8-character alphanumeric password in **3 hours**! This is why KTU expects you to argue for **MFA** in ICS — passwords alone are insufficient against motivated APT (Advanced Persistent Threat) actors targeting critical infrastructure.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MFA Security Improvement

**Problem:** *An ICS uses password (factor 1) with $P_{\text{break,1}} = 10^{-6}$ and a hardware token TOTP (factor 2) with $P_{\text{break,2}} = 10^{-4}$ per attempt. What is the combined probability of compromise per attempt if both are required?*

**Assumption:** Factors are statistically independent (standard MFA assumption).

**Step 1: Independence Justification**

Password is Type 1 (knowledge), TOTP token is Type 2 (possession). The compromise of one does not imply compromise of the other.

**Step 2: Apply Probability of Joint Success**

For an attacker to break MFA, they must break **all** factors simultaneously:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{MFA-break}} &= P_{\text{break,1}} \times P_{\text{break,2}} \\
P_{\text{MFA-break}} &= 10^{-6} \times 10^{-4} \\
P_{\text{MFA-break}} &= 10^{-10}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Security Multiplier**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Improvement} &= \frac{P_{\text{break,1}}}{P_{\text{MFA-break}}} \\
\text{Improvement} &= \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-10}} = 10^{4} \text{ times more secure}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In Zone 4 (Enterprise) to Zone 3.5 (DMZ) bridges of the Purdue Model, vendors like Claroty and Nozomi Networks recommend MFA for all remote vendor sessions. A $10^{4}$ improvement factor often pushes brute-force time from days into millennia.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Frequently Tested Comparisons

| Dimension | Password Only | MFA (Password + Token) | Biometric Only |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security | Low (single point) | High (multi-failure) | Medium (false match) |
| User Convenience | High | Medium | High |
| ICS Suitability | Poor for critical | Excellent | Poor (gloves, dirt) |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Phishing Resistant | No | Yes (hardware token) | Yes |
| Revocable | Yes (change password) | Yes (revoke token) | Hard (re-enrol) |
| KTZ Exam Weight | High | Highest | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MFA Verification Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Requests Access to ICS Zone] --> B[Factor 1: Password Submitted]
    B --> C{Password Hash Match}
    C -->|No| D[Access Denied - Logged]
    C -->|Yes| E[Factor 2: Token TOTP Generated]
    E --> F{TOTP Valid within 30s Window}
    F -->|No| D
    F -->|Yes| G[Factor 3 Optional: Biometric Scan]
    G --> H{Bio Match Above Threshold}
    H -->|No| D
    H -->|Yes| I[Session Token Issued - RBAC Applied]
    I --> J[Access Granted to HMI / SCADA]
    D --> K[Account Lockout after N Fails]
    K --> L[Alert to SIEM / SOC]
```

## 4.2 Purdue Model Authentication Zones (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Zone0["Zone 0 - Process"]
        PT0A[Sensor / Actuator]
    end
    subgraph Zone1["Zone 1 - Basic Control"]
        PT1A[PLC - Shared Service Account]
    end
    subgraph Zone2["Zone 2 - Supervisory"]
        PT2A[HMI - Operator MFA Required]
        PT2B[SCADA Server - Engineer MFA]
    end
    subgraph Zone3["Zone 3 - Operations"]
        PT3A[Historian - Service Account]
        PT3B[Engineering Workstation - MFA + PKI]
    end
    subgraph Zone4["Zone 4 - Enterprise"]
        PT4A[Vendor Remote Access - Hardware Token MFA]
    end
    subgraph DMZ["DMZ Zone 3.5"]
        PTDMZ[Jump Server - Centralized Auth RADIUS]
    end
    PT0A --> PT1A --> PT2A --> PT3A --> PT4A
    PT4A --> PTDMZ --> PT3B
```

## 4.3 Password Hashing with Salt (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[User Password P] --> B[Generate Random Salt s]
    B --> C[Concatenate: P s]
    C --> D[Apply SHA-256 Hash Function]
    D --> E[Store h and s in DB]
    F[Attacker obtains DB] --> G[Rainbow Table Useless]
    G --> H[Must Crack Per User Individually]
```

## 4.4 Challenge-Response Authentication (CHAP) Sequence (Mermaid)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Client as HMI Operator
    participant Server as Authentication Server
    Client->>Server: CONNECT Request
    Server->>Client: CHALLENGE (random nonce N)
    Client->>Client: Compute R = Hash(P || N)
    Client->>Server: RESPONSE R
    Server->>Server: Independently compute R' = Hash(P_stored || N)
    alt R == R'
        Server->>Client: SUCCESS (session established)
    else R != R'
        Server->>Client: FAILURE (re-challenge)
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]
**Define the term "Multi-Factor Authentication". Why is it preferred over single-factor password authentication in Industrial Control Systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) is a security mechanism that requires users to present **two or more independent credentials** (factors) from different categories — **knowledge** (password), **possession** (token/smart card), or **inherence** (biometric) — to gain access. **(1 mark)**

In ICS, MFA is preferred because: (i) passwords alone are vulnerable to brute-force, phishing, and keyloggers (recall 3-hour crack time for 8-char passwords); (ii) a single compromised credential can grant an attacker control over physical processes causing safety incidents; (iii) IEC 62443 mandates multi-factor controls for safety instrumented systems. **(2 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model]
**What is a "rainbow table" attack, and how does password salting defeat it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A rainbow table is a pre-computed database of hash values $H(P)$ for millions of common plaintext passwords, allowing an attacker to reverse-look up a stolen hash to recover the password instantly. **(1.5 marks)**

Salting defeats it by appending a unique random string $s$ to each password before hashing: $h = H(P \,\|\, s)$. Since every user has a different $s$, the attacker cannot reuse a single pre-computed table — they must recompute hashes for each salted password, making rainbow tables infeasible. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 End Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern, Module 3 questions appear as **14-mark questions** with sub-parts and **internal choice**. Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of Multi-Factor Authentication in an Industrial Control System. Differentiate between the three authentication factor types with one ICS example each.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark): Define MFA Architecture**
MFA architecture in ICS integrates an **Authentication Server** (RADIUS/TACACS+) with the **Identity Provider** (Active Directory) and **Hardware Tokens** (RSA SecurID, YubiKey), gating access to HMIs, SCADA, and Engineering Workstations at the boundary of Level 3.5 (DMZ).

**Step 2 (2 marks): Three Factor Types**

- **Type 1 (Knowledge):** Something the user knows. *ICS Example:* Operator password to log into a Siemens WinCC HMI.
- **Type 2 (Possession):** Something the user has. *ICS Example:* A YubiKey hardware token issued to authorized engineers for remote SCADA programming.
- **Type 3 (Inherence):** Something the user is. *ICS Example:* Fingerprint biometric at the control room door (less common in Zone 0/1 due to contamination).

**Step 3 (2 marks): MFA Architecture Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid flow)**

**Step 4 (2 marks): ICS-Specific Justification**
- **Defense in Depth:** Even if password is phished, token blocks access.
- **Compliance:** ISA/IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.7 mandates strong authentication.
- **Auditability:** Each factor event is logged for forensics.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An operator password at a plant is 10 characters long, drawn from a 95-symbol printable ASCII set. Calculate:**
**(i) Password entropy in bits.**
**(ii) Average brute-force cracking time at $10^{12}$ guesses/sec.**
**(iii) Comment on whether this password alone is sufficient for a Safety Instrumented System (SIS) access.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 10$, $N = 95$, $R = 10^{12}$ guesses/sec

**Step 1 (2 marks): Entropy**
$$
E = L \cdot \log_2(N) = 10 \cdot \log_2(95)
$$

$$
\log_2(95) = \frac{\ln(95)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.5539}{0.6931} \approx 6.570
$$

$$
E = 10 \times 6.570 = 65.70 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Total Search Space**
$$
S = 95^{10} \Rightarrow \log_{10}(S) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(95) = 10 \times 1.9777 = 19.777
$$
$$
S \approx 10^{19.777} \approx 5.99 \times 10^{19}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Average Cracking Time**
$$
T_{\text{avg}} = \frac{S}{2R} = \frac{5.99 \times 10^{19}}{2 \times 10^{12}} = 2.995 \times 10^{7} \text{ sec} \approx 0.95 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): SIS Suitability Verdict**
> [!WARNING]
> **Insufficient for SIS access.** Although entropy is reasonable (~65.7 bits), cracking time of **0.95 years** is unacceptable for safety-critical SIS (IEC 61508 SIL-3 requires ≤ 1 ms response for protection). MFA + hardware token + dual-control is mandatory for SIS — single password is never allowed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the following authentication protocols used in ICS environments with their operational advantages:**
**(i) CHAP**
**(ii) Kerberos**
**(iii) RADIUS**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) CHAP (2 marks):** Challenge Handshake Authentication Protocol operates in three steps — server sends a random nonce $N$, client computes $R = H(P \,\|\, N)$ using a one-way hash, and server compares. Password $P$ is never transmitted. *Advantage:* Resistant to replay attacks since $N$ changes per session. Used in legacy RTU links.

**(ii) Kerberos (2.5 marks):** Ticket-based protocol using a Key Distribution Center (KDC) with Authentication Server (AS) and Ticket Granting Server (TGS). User obtains a Ticket Granting Ticket (TGT) once, then service tickets for each HMI/SCADA session. *Advantage:* Mutual authentication, single sign-on, time-limited tickets prevent reuse. Used in Windows-based SCADA workstations.

**(iii) RADIUS (2.5 marks):** Remote Authentication Dial-In User Service centralizes AAA (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) for remote users connecting via VPN to the plant network. *Advantage:* Centralized policy enforcement, scales for hundreds of remote engineers, encrypts only the password (not entire packet) — legacy compatible. Often paired with TACACS+ for finer-grained authorization.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare and contrast password-based, token-based, and biometric authentication for ICS. Justify which is most suitable for Zone 1 (Basic Control) access.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Comparison Table (4 marks)**

| Criteria | Password | Token | Biometric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security Level | Low | High | Medium |
| Revocability | Easy | Medium | Hard |
| ICS Zone 0/1 Use | Poor (shared) | Acceptable | Unsuitable |
| Cost | Negligible | Moderate | High |
| Hygiene Issues | None | None | Major (gloves) |

**Step 2: Zone 1 Suitability Analysis (3 marks)**

Zone 1 (Basic Control — PLCs) operates in **harsh environments** (oil mist, metal shavings, temperature extremes, mandatory gloves). Therefore:
- **Biometric is unsuitable** (false rejection due to dirty/gloved fingers).
- **Password alone is insufficient** (shared service accounts common, weak complexity enforced by legacy PLC firmware).
- **Token-based (smart card or RFID badge)** is **most suitable** — contactless, fast, hygienic, and integrates with physical access control to the control cabinet.

**Verdict:** A combination of **token + short PIN** (effectively MFA) is the industry standard for Zone 1 PLC programming access (per IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1, 1.7).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> - **Don't confuse authentication with authorization** — many students mix the two. Authentication = "Who are you?" Authorization = "What can you do?" (1 mark deducted if confused).
> - **Don't write $|x|$ in tables** — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` to preserve markdown rendering.
> - **Always mention IEC 62443 / ISA 99** when discussing ICS authentication — generic IT answers will lose 2–3 marks.
> - **Show units** for entropy (bits) and time (seconds/hours/years) — unmarked units cost 0.5 mark each.
> - **Do not skip the formula substitution step** — examiners award partial marks only when each substitution is shown.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Authentication** verifies identity; **Authorization** verifies permission — never interchange.
- ✅ **Three factor types:** Knowledge (Type 1), Possession (Type 2), Inherence (Type 3) — Type 4 is location/behavior.
- ✅ **Entropy formula:** $E = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ bits — always in **bits**.
- ✅ **Brute-force time:** $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{N^L}{2R}$ where $R$ is guesses/sec.
- ✅ **Salting** is per-user random string concatenated before hashing — defeats rainbow tables.
- ✅ **MFA joint probability:** $P_{\text{joint}} = P_1 \cdot P_2 \cdots P_n$ (independent factors).
- ✅ **CHAP** uses random nonce + hash; **password never sent on wire**.
- ✅ **Kerberos** uses TGT + service tickets; **time-limited** to prevent replay.
- ✅ **RADIUS** = AAA framework (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting) for remote access.
- ✅ **ICS-specific:** Biometric is poorly suited for Zone 0/1 (contamination); **token + PIN** is industry standard for PLC access.
- ✅ **Standards:** ISA/IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1, 1.5, 1.7 govern ICS authentication.
- ✅ **Real-world attacks:** 2015 Ukraine BlackEnergy used stolen VPN credentials → MFA could have prevented pivot.
- ✅ **NIST SP 800-63B** is the definitive modern reference for authenticator assurance levels (AAL1, AAL2, AAL3).
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** $E$ in bits not nats; $N$ is pool size, not password length; average case divides by 2, worst case does not.
- ✅ **Mandatory buzzwords for 14-mark answers:** Defense-in-Depth, Least Privilege, Zero Trust, IEC 62443, Purdue Model, DMZ Zone 3.5.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Authorization (Role-Based Access Control, Attribute-Based Access Control)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 User Authorization — Formal Definition

In the context of **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** and **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** environments, **User Authorization** is the security mechanism that determines *what an authenticated user is permitted to do* within the system after their identity has been verified. It governs the rights, privileges, and access boundaries assigned to operators, engineers, maintenance staff, and external integrators interacting with **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)**, **RTUs (Remote Terminal Units)**, **HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** workstations, and **Engineering Stations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Authentication ≠ Authorization** — Authentication answers *"Who are you?"* (Identity verification) while Authorization answers *"What are you allowed to do?"* (Permission enforcement). Both are mandatory, but they are independent security layers under the **AAA framework (Authentication, Authorization, Accounting)** prescribed in **IEC 62443-3-3** for industrial automation.

## 1.2 Access Control as a Triad

Authorization in modern ICS is best understood through the **subject–object–action** triad:

- **Subject** — The active entity requesting access (operator, engineer, automated agent, service account).
- **Object** — The passive resource being protected (valve controller, sensor data, ladder-logic file, recipe database).
- **Action** — The operation being attempted (read telemetry, modify setpoint, push firmware, acknowledge alarm).

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pharmaceutical Plant Floor**
> Think of an industrial plant as a **multi-story pharmaceutical factory**:
> - *Authentication* is the **security gate** that checks your ID badge (username + password / smart card / biometrics).
> - *Authorization* is the **colour-coded wristband** the gatekeeper issues — *red* lets you into the chemical storage vault, *blue* lets you operate the mixing tanks, *green* lets you only view the dashboard screens. You cannot enter a room your wristband colour does not permit, even if your badge is valid.
> - In **RBAC**, wristbands are pre-printed for job roles (Chemist, Operator, Janitor).
> - In **ABAC**, the wristband is **printed dynamically** based on *current* attributes — time of day, your recent training certifications, the room's current hazard level, and whether a senior chemist is present.
>
> This wristband model is exactly how an **HMI** rejects an operator trying to push a new control loop parameter without the proper role/attribute clearance.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Model

The **NIST SP 800-162** *Guide to Attribute Based Access Control (ABAC) Definition and Considerations* and **ANSI/INCITS 359** *RBAC Standard* form the canonical references. For ICS, the binding standard is **IEC 62443-3-3 System Security Requirements and Security Levels**, which mandates **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** as the minimum requirement and **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)** as the recommended evolution for **Security Level 3 (SL-3)** and above.

## 1.5 The Two Principal Models

| Model | Core Logic | Decision Basis | Granularity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RBAC** | Permissions are bound to *Roles*; users acquire permissions through role membership | Static role assignment | Coarse-to-medium |
| **ABAC** | Permissions are derived from *Attributes* of subject, object, action, environment evaluated against *Policies* | Dynamic, policy-driven | Fine-grained, contextual |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

### 2.1.1 Conceptual Building Blocks

RBAC, formalized by **Sandhu et al. (1996)** and standardized in **ANSI/INCITS 359-2012**, is built upon four foundational sets:

- **U** — Set of **Users** (human operators, service accounts)
- **R** — Set of **Roles** (Operator, Maintenance Engineer, Process Supervisor, Security Administrator)
- **P** — Set of **Permissions** (read sensor, write setpoint, acknowledge alarm, configure HMI)
- **S** — Set of **Sessions** (an active login context)

### 2.1.2 Core Relations

The model is defined by the following functional mappings:

1. **User–Role Assignment (UA):** A many-to-many relation $UA \subseteq U \times R$.
2. **Role–Permission Assignment (PA):** A many-to-many relation $PA \subseteq P \times R$.
3. **Session–User Mapping:** $user: S \rightarrow U$.
4. **Session–Active Role Mapping:** $roles: S \rightarrow 2^{R}$ — a user may activate a *subset* of their assigned roles per session.

### 2.1.3 The NIST RBAC Hierarchy (Four Levels)

| Level | Name | Added Capability |
|---|---|---|
| **RBAC₀** | Flat RBAC | Base user–role–permission relations; users require permission to perform roles |
| **RBAC₁** | Hierarchical RBAC | Role inheritance: $r_1 \succeq r_2 \Rightarrow$ all permissions of $r_2$ propagate to $r_1$ |
| **RBAC₂** | Constrained RBAC | **Separation of Duties (SoD)** — mutually exclusive roles; cardinality constraints |
| **RBAC₃** | Combines RBAC₁ + RBAC₂ | Symmetric (hierarchical + constrained) — the *de facto* industry model |

> [!NOTE]
> In an ICS, a **Maintenance Engineer** role may *inherit* the read permissions of the **Operator** role (RBAC₁), but a *Static Separation of Duty* (SSD) rule must prevent the same individual from being assigned both **Operator** *and* **Safety Officer** simultaneously to avoid a single person authorizing and overriding their own unsafe actions (RBAC₂).

### 2.1.4 RBAC Constraints (The Security Hardening Layer)

- **Static Separation of Duty (SSD):** Enforced at role-assignment time. $|UA(r) \cap UA(r')| \leq 1$ for mutually exclusive roles $r, r'$.
- **Dynamic Separation of Duty (DSD):** Enforced at activation time; a user may *hold* both roles but cannot *activate* them in the same session.
- **Cardinality Constraints:** Limits the number of users per role — useful for emergency-override roles.

## 2.2 Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)

### 2.2.1 Conceptual Building Blocks

ABAC generalizes RBAC by replacing discrete roles with **multi-dimensional attribute tuples** evaluated against **boolean policy rules**. The four canonical attribute classes are:

| Attribute Class | Symbol | Example in ICS |
|---|---|---|
| **Subject attributes** | $S_{attr}$ | Operator ID, certification level, shift schedule, clearance level |
| **Object (resource) attributes** | $O_{attr}$ | PLC firmware version, safety integrity level (SIL), zone classification |
| **Action attributes** | $A_{attr}$ | Read, write, delete, execute, push, acknowledge |
| **Environment (context) attributes** | $E_{attr}$ | Time of day, network zone, threat-level, plant mode (startup/running/shutdown) |

### 2.2.2 The Canonical ABAC Decision Function

A formal access decision is a function:

$$
\text{Decision}(s, o, a, e) =
\begin{cases}
\text{PERMIT} & \text{if } \exists \, p \in P \; \mid \; \text{Rule}(s_{attr}, o_{attr}, a_{attr}, e_{attr}) = \text{true} \\[4pt]
\text{DENY} & \text{otherwise (default deny)}
\end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $s$ = subject (the requesting user/agent)
- $o$ = object (the protected resource)
- $a$ = action (the requested operation)
- $e$ = environment (current contextual state)
- $P$ = the set of all policy rules
- $p$ = a single policy rule expressed as a boolean predicate

### 2.2.3 XACML — The Standardized ABAC Reference Architecture

The **eXtensible Access Control Markup Language (XACML)**, standardized by **OASIS**, is the most widely adopted ABAC framework. Its architecture comprises four primitives:

1. **Policy Administration Point (PAP)** — Authors and stores policies.
2. **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** — Evaluates the applicable policy and returns Permit/Deny/NotApplicable/Indeterminate.
3. **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** — Sits in-line with the request, intercepts it, and enforces the PDP verdict.
4. **Policy Information Point (PIP)** — Supplies attribute values to the PDP (e.g., LDAP, SCADA historian, time server).

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **essential formalisms** required to answer KTU examination questions on this topic. No vertical pipe `|` characters are used inside cell content to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept / Formula | Formal Expression | ICS Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | RBAC Permission Set of User $u$ | $P(u) = \bigcup_{r \in roles(u)} P(r)$ | All permissions obtained via role membership |
| 2 | RBAC Role Hierarchy (Inheritance) | $r_1 \succeq r_2 \iff P(r_2) \subseteq P(r_1)$ | Senior role inherits junior role's permissions |
| 3 | Static Separation of Duty (SSD) | $\forall\, (r_i, r_j) \in SSD, \; \vert UA(r_i) \cap UA(r_j) \vert \leq 1$ | No shared user between conflicting roles |
| 4 | Dynamic Separation of Duty (DSD) | $\forall s \in S, \; \vert roles(s) \cap DSD\text{-pair} \vert \leq 1$ | Conflicting roles not co-activated in a session |
| 5 | ABAC Decision Predicate | $f(s_{attr}, o_{attr}, a_{attr}, e_{attr}) \to \{0, 1\}$ | Boolean rule evaluation across four attribute classes |
| 6 | XACML Four-Point Flow | $\text{PEP} \to \text{PDP} \to \text{PIP} \to \text{PDP} \to \text{PEP}$ | Request–response lifecycle of an ABAC decision |
| 7 | Default-Deny Principle | $\text{No matching rule} \Rightarrow \text{DENY}$ | Fail-secure posture mandated by IEC 62443 |
| 8 | RBAC vs ABAC Granularity | $G_{ABAC} \gg G_{RBAC}$ | ABAC supports per-attribute, per-context decisions |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Grid Substations:** RBAC restricts which operators can reclose breakers; ABAC additionally checks shift handover status and the substation's current operating mode.
- **Smart Manufacturing:** ABAC dynamically grants *read* access to machine telemetry only when the machine is in **SAFE-STOP** state, preventing data exfiltration during live operation.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** RBAC + ABAC combined enforce IEC 62443 SL-2/SL-3 zones — operators from Zone 1 cannot write to Zone 2 PLCs even if they hold the role, unless VPN and time-of-day attributes are satisfied.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Effective Permission Set of an RBAC User

**Problem Context:** An ICS plant has the role hierarchy and permission assignments shown. Derive the *effective* permission set for a user assigned multiple roles, factoring in inheritance.

### 3.1.1 Given Definitions

Let the role hierarchy be defined as:

$$
\text{PlantManager} \succeq \text{ShiftSupervisor} \succeq \text{SeniorOperator} \succeq \text{JuniorOperator}
$$

The base permission assignments are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{JuniorOperator}) &= \{ \text{read\_sensor}, \; \text{ack\_alarm} \} \\[4pt]
P(\text{SeniorOperator}) &= \{ \text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{start\_pump} \} \\[4pt]
P(\text{ShiftSupervisor}) &= \{ \text{override\_alarm}, \; \text{assign\_task} \} \\[4pt]
P(\text{PlantManager}) &= \{ \text{reconfigure\_HMI}, \; \text{push\_firmware} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Establish the inheritance rule.**

For any $r_i \succeq r_j$, every permission in $P(r_j)$ is transitively available to $r_i$.

$$
r_i \succeq r_j \;\Longrightarrow\; P(r_j) \subseteq P(r_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the effective permission set for SeniorOperator.**

Because $\text{SeniorOperator} \succeq \text{JuniorOperator}$ (transitively via the chain), the effective permission set is the *union* of the role's own permissions and all inherited permissions:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{SeniorOperator}) = P(\text{SeniorOperator}) \cup P(\text{JuniorOperator})
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{SeniorOperator}) = \{ \text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{start\_pump} \} \cup \{ \text{read\_sensor}, \; \text{ack\_alarm} \}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{SeniorOperator}) = \{ \text{read\_sensor}, \; \text{ack\_alarm}, \; \text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{start\_pump} \}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective permission set for ShiftSupervisor.**

Following the same rule, ShiftSupervisor inherits from SeniorOperator (which itself inherits from JuniorOperator):

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{ShiftSupervisor}) = P(\text{ShiftSupervisor}) \cup P_{\text{eff}}(\text{SeniorOperator})
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{ShiftSupervisor}) = \{ \text{override\_alarm}, \; \text{assign\_task} \} \cup \{ \text{read\_sensor}, \; \text{ack\_alarm}, \; \text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{start\_pump} \}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{ShiftSupervisor}) = \{ \text{read\_sensor}, \; \text{ack\_alarm}, \; \text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{start\_pump}, \; \text{override\_alarm}, \; \text{assign\_task} \}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective permission set for PlantManager (the top role).**

PlantManager inherits from ShiftSupervisor, which transitively inherits all subordinate roles:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{PlantManager}) = P(\text{PlantManager}) \cup P_{\text{eff}}(\text{ShiftSupervisor})
$$

Substituting:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{PlantManager}) = \{ \text{reconfigure\_HMI}, \; \text{push\_firmware} \} \cup P_{\text{eff}}(\text{ShiftSupervisor})
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{\text{eff}}(\text{PlantManager}) = \{ \text{read\_sensor}, \; \text{ack\_alarm}, \; \text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{start\_pump}, \; \text{override\_alarm}, \; \text{assign\_task}, \; \text{reconfigure\_HMI}, \; \text{push\_firmware} \}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply SSD Constraint Check.**

Suppose SSD is declared on the pair $(\text{SafetyOfficer}, \text{PlantManager})$. The constraint is:

$$
\vert UA(\text{SafetyOfficer}) \cap UA(\text{PlantManager}) \vert \leq 1
$$

If a user $u_1$ is already assigned to SafetyOfficer, then the assignment $UA \leftarrow UA \cup \{ (u_1, \text{PlantManager}) \}$ must be **rejected** by the Role Administration tool, preserving the *four-eyes principle*.

> [!NOTE]
> The cardinality bound $\leq 1$ (instead of 0) is sometimes used to allow *temporary delegation* under joint approval — a pattern common in IEC 62443 SL-3 environments for emergency override.

## 3.2 Derivation of the ABAC Permit Decision

**Problem Context:** An engineer requests to write a new PID tuning setpoint to a PLC during night shift in a hazardous area.

### 3.2.1 Attribute Tuple Snapshot

The PEP captures the following live attributes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{attr} &= \{ \text{role}=\text{ProcessEngineer}, \; \text{cert}=\text{TUV\_Level2}, \; \text{shift}=\text{Night} \} \\[4pt]
o_{attr} &= \{ \text{device}=\text{PLC\_Boiler03}, \; \text{SIL}=2, \; \text{zone}=\text{Hazardous\_A} \} \\[4pt]
a_{attr} &= \{ \text{op}=\text{write\_setpoint}, \; \text{scope}=\text{PID\_tune} \} \\[4pt]
e_{attr} &= \{ \text{time}=02{:}14, \; \text{plant\_mode}=\text{Startup}, \; \text{threat\_level}=\text{Elevated} \}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Policy Rule Definition

A policy $p_1$ is written in pseudo-XACML:

$$
p_1 \equiv (\text{role}=\text{ProcessEngineer}) \;\land\; (\text{cert}=\text{TUV\_Level2}) \;\land\; (\text{op}=\text{write\_setpoint}) \;\land\; (\text{plant\_mode} \neq \text{Startup}) \;\land\; (\text{threat\_level} \neq \text{Critical})
$$

### 3.2.3 Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Step 1 — Substitute subject attributes:**

$$
(\text{ProcessEngineer}=\text{ProcessEngineer}) \;\land\; (\text{TUV\_Level2}=\text{TUV\_Level2}) = \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute action attributes:**

$$
(\text{write\_setpoint}=\text{write\_setpoint}) = \text{TRUE}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute environment attributes:**

$$
(\text{Startup} \neq \text{Startup}) = \text{FALSE}
$$

Because of the $\land$ (logical AND) operator across the full predicate, the rule short-circuits:

$$
\text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{FALSE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} = \text{FALSE}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply default-deny.**

No other policy $p$ yields TRUE. Therefore:

$$
\text{Decision}(s, o, a, e) = \text{DENY}
$$

The engineer is **denied the write operation** because the plant is currently in *Startup* mode — a high-risk state where setpoint changes are policy-prohibited.

## 3.3 RBAC vs ABAC Decision Matrix

| Decision Dimension | RBAC Verdict | ABAC Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| Night-shift write blocked when plant is in Startup | ✗ Allowed if role permits | ✓ Denied via $e_{attr}$ check |
| Hazardous-zone access for non-certified user | ✗ Allowed if role assigned | ✓ Denied via $s_{attr}$ check |
| Emergency override with senior co-sign | Hard to express | ✓ Native support via policy composition |
| Policy audit / compliance reporting | Moderate (role mining) | High (rule traceability) |
| Performance overhead | Low (lookup) | Higher (PIP fetches, predicate evaluation) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RBAC Functional Architecture (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Login] --> B[Authentication Module]
    B --> C{Identity Valid?}
    C -- No --> Z1[Access Denied - Log Security Event]
    C -- Yes --> D[Authorization Engine RBAC]
    D --> E[Look up User Role Assignments UA]
    E --> F[Resolve Role Hierarchy RBAC1]
    F --> G[Apply SSD and DSD Constraints RBAC2]
    G --> H{Constraints Satisfied?}
    H -- No --> Z2[Reject - Separation of Duty Violation]
    H -- Yes --> I[Compute Effective Permission Set P eff of u]
    I --> J[Establish Session with Active Role Subset]
    J --> K[Subject Requests Action on Object]
    K --> L{Action in P eff of u?}
    L -- No --> Z3[Deny Request]
    L -- Yes --> M[PEP Permits Action to ICS Resource]
    M --> N[Audit Log Entry Written]
```

## 4.2 ABAC Functional Architecture (XACML-Aligned Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Subject Initiates Request] --> B1[PEP Policy Enforcement Point Intercepts]
    B1 --> C1[PEP Constructs XACML Request]
    C1 --> D1[PDP Policy Decision Point]
    D1 --> E1[PDP Fetches Policies from PAP]
    D1 --> F1[PDP Queries PIP for Attribute Values]
    F1 --> G1[PIP Connects to LDAP SCADA Historian Time Server]
    F1 --> H1[PIP Returns Attributes to PDP]
    E1 --> I1[PDP Evaluates Boolean Policy Rules]
    H1 --> I1
    I1 --> J1{Any Policy Yields TRUE?}
    J1 -- No --> K1[Decision DENY Default Fail Secure]
    J1 -- Yes --> L1[Decision PERMIT]
    K1 --> M1[PEP Enforces DENY on ICS Resource]
    L1 --> N1[PEP Allows Action to Proceed]
    M1 --> O1[Audit and Alert Generated]
    N1 --> O1
```

## 4.3 RBAC Hierarchy with Separation of Duty

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Plant Manager] --> R2[Shift Supervisor]
    R2 --> R3[Senior Operator]
    R3 --> R4[Junior Operator]
    R1 -.SSD.-> R5[Safety Officer]
    R2 -.SSD.-> R5
    R3 -.DSD.-> R6[Maintenance Engineer]
    R4 -.DSD.-> R6
    classDef ssd stroke:#b00,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5
    classDef dsd stroke:#06c,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray:5
    class R5 ssd
    class R6 dsd
```

## 4.4 RBAC-to-ABAC Evolution Path (Compliance & Maturity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[No Formal Access Control] --> L2[Discretionary DAC Basic]
    L2 --> L3[Mandatory MAC Basic]
    L3 --> L4[Role Based RBAC IEC 62443 SL 2]
    L4 --> L5[Constrained RBAC with SoD IEC 62443 SL 3]
    L5 --> L6[Attribute Based ABAC IEC 62443 SL 3 plus]
    L6 --> L7[Context Aware Zero Trust ABAC SL 4]
```

## 4.5 XACML Request-Response Lifecycle (Detailed Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Subject] --> P1[PEP Receive Request]
    P1 --> P2[PEP Format XACML Request]
    P2 --> P3[PDP Receive Request]
    P3 --> P4[PDP Consult PAP for Policies]
    P3 --> P5[PDP Query PIP for Missing Attributes]
    P5 --> P6[PIP Resolve Subject Object Environment Attributes]
    P6 --> P7[PIP Return Attributes to PDP]
    P4 --> P8[PDP Evaluate All Applicable Policies]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Apply Combining Algorithm deny-overrides]
    P9 --> P10[PDP Return Decision to PEP]
    P10 --> P11[PEP Enforce Decision on ICS Resource]
    P11 --> P12[Subject Receives Response Permit or Deny]
    P11 --> P13[Audit Trail Updated]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — Definition Type
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
> Differentiate between **Authentication** and **Authorization** in the context of Industrial Control Systems. Why is the distinction critical for IEC 62443 compliance?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Authentication (1 mark):** The process of verifying the *identity* of a user, device, or process attempting to access the ICS — typically via username/password, smart cards, RFID badges, biometrics, or certificates. It answers *"Who are you?"*
- **Authorization (1 mark):** The process of determining *what actions* an authenticated entity is permitted to perform on specific ICS resources (PLCs, HMIs, historian data). It answers *"What are you allowed to do?"*
- **IEC 62443 Criticality (1 mark):** The standard mandates that authentication and authorization be *implemented as independent layers* under the **SR 1.1 — Human user identification** and **SR 2.1 — Authorization enforcement** requirements. Conflating the two creates a single point of failure and violates the *defense-in-depth* principle required for SL-2 and above.

### Question 2 — Conceptual Comparison
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> What is the fundamental difference between **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** and **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)**? State one industrial scenario where ABAC provides a security advantage over RBAC.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **RBAC (1 mark):** Permissions are assigned to *static roles* (e.g., Operator, Engineer); users acquire permissions only through role membership. The decision is based purely on the user's role(s).
- **ABAC (1 mark):** Permissions are derived from *attributes* of the subject, object, action, and environment, evaluated against *policy rules*. Decisions are dynamic and context-aware.
- **Industrial scenario (1 mark):** A **night-shift chemical plant operator** attempting to modify a PID setpoint on a boiler PLC during *plant startup mode* should be denied. RBAC would allow it (the operator role holds `write_setpoint`). ABAC denies it because the environment attribute `plant_mode = Startup` violates the policy. This context-awareness is impossible in pure RBAC without exploding the role count.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) pattern: each 14-mark question has two sub-parts typically of **7 + 7** marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Model solutions below follow the official marking-scheme style.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** as defined in the **NIST/ANSI INCITS 359** standard. Discuss the four levels of RBAC (RBAC₀, RBAC₁, RBAC₂, RBAC₃) and illustrate with an **ICS operator hierarchy** example. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Explain the concept of **Separation of Duties (SoD)** in RBAC. Differentiate between **Static Separation of Duty (SSD)** and **Dynamic Separation of Duty (DSD)** with a suitable industrial example each. Justify why SoD is a *constraint* and not a *permission* in the formal RBAC model. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **RBAC Architecture Diagram (2 marks):**
   The core components are Users, Roles, Permissions, and Sessions, connected through:
   - *User–Role Assignment (UA)* relation,
   - *Role–Permission Assignment (PA)* relation,
   - *Session* activation mechanism.
   *(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram.)*

2. **Four RBAC Levels (3 marks):**
   - **RBAC₀ (Flat):** Basic user–role–permission relations; minimum requirement.
   - **RBAC₁ (Hierarchical):** Adds role inheritance — senior roles inherit permissions of junior roles. *ICS example:* `PlantManager` ⊳ `ShiftSupervisor` ⊳ `SeniorOperator` ⊳ `JuniorOperator`.
   - **RBAC₂ (Constrained):** Adds Separation of Duty and cardinality constraints.
   - **RBAC₃ (Symmetric):** Combines RBAC₁ and RBAC₂; the recommended enterprise/ICS model.

3. **ICS Hierarchy Example (2 marks):**
   | Role | Inherited From | Effective Permissions |
   |---|---|---|
   | JuniorOperator | — | `read_sensor`, `ack_alarm` |
   | SeniorOperator | JuniorOperator | + `write_setpoint`, `start_pump` |
   | ShiftSupervisor | SeniorOperator | + `override_alarm`, `assign_task` |
   | PlantManager | ShiftSupervisor | + `reconfigure_HMI`, `push_firmware` |

   Inheritance math:

   $$
   P_{\text{eff}}(\text{PlantManager}) = P(\text{PlantManager}) \cup P_{\text{eff}}(\text{ShiftSupervisor}) \cup P_{\text{eff}}(\text{SeniorOperator}) \cup P_{\text{eff}}(\text{JuniorOperator})
   $$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Concept of SoD (2 marks):**
   Separation of Duty is the principle that *no single individual should have sufficient privileges to misuse the system on their own*. It enforces the *four-eyes* or *two-person* rule for sensitive operations.

2. **SSD — Static Separation of Duty (2 marks):**
   - Enforced at **role-assignment time** (offline).
   - Conflicting roles cannot be assigned to the same user *at all*.
   - *Industrial example:* A user cannot be assigned **both** `PlantManager` **and** `SafetyOfficer` — preventing the same person from authorizing a hazardous procedure *and* signing off on its safety validation.

   Formal rule:

   $$
   \forall\, (r_i, r_j) \in SSD, \quad \vert UA(r_i) \cap UA(r_j) \vert \leq 1
   $$

3. **DSD — Dynamic Separation of Duty (2 marks):**
   - Enforced at **role-activation time** (within a session).
   - A user may *hold* both conflicting roles but cannot *activate* them concurrently.
   - *Industrial example:* A process engineer may hold both `Operator` and `MaintenanceEngineer` roles for cross-functional coverage, but cannot *activate* both in the same HMI session — preventing live operation and maintenance on the same equipment simultaneously (avoiding arc-flash / lockout-tagout violations).

   Formal rule:

   $$
   \forall s \in S, \quad \vert roles(s) \cap DSD\text{-pair} \vert \leq 1
   $$

4. **Why SoD is a Constraint, Not a Permission (1 mark):**
   Permissions describe *what actions a role can perform*; SoD describes *which role combinations are forbidden*. SoD operates on the UA and session mappings, not on P — hence it is a *cardinality / mutual-exclusion constraint* layered on top of the base RBAC model.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — RBAC Question:** Students frequently **omit the inheritance direction** when stating the hierarchy. The correct form is `Senior \succeq Junior` (senior inherits junior's permissions), not the reverse. Drawing the hierarchy with arrows pointing the wrong way costs 1–2 marks. Also, do not confuse *role hierarchy* (RBAC₁) with *organizational hierarchy* — they are independent constructs.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Model Paper]**

> **(a)** Explain the **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC)** model with its four attribute classes. Describe the **XACML reference architecture** (PAP, PDP, PEP, PIP) and trace the flow of an access request from a subject to a final Permit/Deny decision. **(7 marks)**

> **(b)** Construct a formal **ABAC policy** for an ICS scenario in which a *Level-2 certified process engineer* is permitted to *write a PID setpoint* on a *SIL-2 boiler PLC* **only** during *normal plant running mode*, **only** during *day shift*, and **never** when the *threat level is Critical*. Evaluate the policy for the following request and justify the decision. **(7 marks)**

> **Request snapshot:**
> - *Subject:* Engineer Ravi, role = `ProcessEngineer`, cert = `TUV_Level2`, shift = `Day`
> - *Object:* `PLC_Boiler03`, SIL = 2, zone = `Safe`
> - *Action:* `write_setpoint`, scope = `PID_tune`
> - *Environment:* time = 10:30, `plant_mode` = `Running`, `threat_level` = `Elevated`

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **ABAC Model — Four Attribute Classes (3 marks):**

   | Class | Notation | ICS Example |
   |---|---|---|
   | Subject | $S_{attr}$ | Operator ID, certification, clearance, shift |
   | Object | $O_{attr}$ | PLC ID, firmware version, SIL rating, zone |
   | Action | $A_{attr}$ | read, write, push, acknowledge, override |
   | Environment | $E_{attr}$ | time, plant_mode, threat_level, network_zone |

2. **XACML Architecture (2 marks):**
   - **PAP** (Policy Administration Point) — authors and stores XACML policies.
   - **PDP** (Policy Decision Point) — evaluates policies against attribute values.
   - **PEP** (Policy Enforcement Point) — sits in-line; intercepts and enforces the PDP verdict.
   - **PIP** (Policy Information Point) — supplies attribute values from authoritative sources (LDAP, historian, time server).

3. **Request Flow Trace (2 marks):**
   1. Subject sends request → PEP intercepts.
   2. PEP formats an XACML request and forwards to PDP.
   3. PDP retrieves relevant policies from PAP.
   4. PDP requests missing attributes from PIP.
   5. PIP returns attribute values to PDP.
   6. PDP evaluates all applicable rules → returns Permit / Deny / NotApplicable / Indeterminate to PEP.
   7. PEP enforces the decision and writes an audit log.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

1. **Policy Construction (3 marks):**

   $$
   p_1 \;\equiv\; (\text{role}=\text{ProcessEngineer}) \;\land\; (\text{cert}=\text{TUV\_Level2}) \;\land\; (\text{op}=\text{write\_setpoint}) \;\land\; (\text{SIL}(o) = 2) \;\land\; (\text{plant\_mode}=\text{Running}) \;\land\; (\text{shift}=\text{Day}) \;\land\; (\text{threat\_level} \neq \text{Critical})
   $$

2. **Attribute Substitution (2 marks):**
   - $(\text{ProcessEngineer}=\text{ProcessEngineer}) = \text{TRUE}$
   - $(\text{TUV\_Level2}=\text{TUV\_Level2}) = \text{TRUE}$
   - $(\text{write\_setpoint}=\text{write\_setpoint}) = \text{TRUE}$
   - $(\text{SIL}=2) = \text{TRUE}$
   - $(\text{Running}=\text{Running}) = \text{TRUE}$
   - $(\text{Day}=\text{Day}) = \text{TRUE}$
   - $(\text{Elevated} \neq \text{Critical}) = \text{TRUE}$

3. **Final Conjunction (1 mark):**

   $$
   \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;\land\; \text{TRUE} \;=\; \text{TRUE}
   $$

4. **Decision (1 mark):**

   $$
   \text{Decision}(\text{Ravi}, \text{PLC\_Boiler03}, \text{write\_setpoint}, \text{10:30\_Day\_Running}) = \text{PERMIT}
   $$

   **Justification:** All seven conjuncts of policy $p_1$ are satisfied. Ravi is a certified process engineer on day shift, the PLC is SIL-2, the plant is in normal running mode, and the threat level is *Elevated* (not *Critical*). The XACML PDP returns **Permit**; the PEP allows the PID setpoint write and emits an audit entry.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — ABAC Question:** Students commonly **forget the default-deny** clause. If *any* conjunct is FALSE, the policy fails and — critically — the decision must be **DENY**, not Indeterminate or Permit. Also, do not write the policy using only role attributes; you must demonstrate the *multi-dimensional* nature of ABAC (subject **+ object + action + environment**). A response that omits the environment class will lose at least 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — User Authorization**

- **Authentication ≠ Authorization** — Identity verification vs permission enforcement; both mandatory under IEC 62443.
- **RBAC** binds permissions to *roles*; users acquire permissions through role membership (relations UA, PA).
- **NIST RBAC has four levels** — RBAC₀ (flat), RBAC₁ (hierarchical with inheritance $r_1 \succeq r_2$), RBAC₂ (constrained with SSD/DSD), RBAC₃ (combined).
- **Effective permission set** of a user: $P(u) = \bigcup_{r \in roles(u)} P(r)$ — always include inherited roles in the union.
- **SSD** = static (assignment-time) constraint; **DSD** = dynamic (session-time) constraint. Formal bound: $\vert UA(r_i) \cap UA(r_j) \vert \leq 1$.
- **ABAC** uses **four attribute classes**: Subject ($S_{attr}$), Object ($O_{attr}$), Action ($A_{attr}$), Environment ($E_{attr}$).
- **ABAC decision function** returns Permit only if $\exists$ a policy rule whose predicate over the four attribute classes evaluates TRUE; otherwise **default DENY**.
- **XACML reference architecture** = **PAP, PDP, PEP, PIP** — remember the order: request flows Subject → PEP → PDP → PIP → PDP → PEP → Subject.
- **RBAC vs ABAC** — RBAC is static and coarse; ABAC is dynamic, contextual, and fine-grained. ABAC satisfies IEC 62443 SL-3+ requirements that RBAC alone cannot.
- **Default-deny (fail-secure)** is a non-negotiable security principle in ICS — never default to Permit when no policy matches.
- **Inheritance direction in RBAC** is *senior ⊳ junior* (senior inherits junior's permissions), not the reverse.
- **Common exam trap** — confusing *role hierarchy* (RBAC₁, a permission relationship) with *organizational hierarchy* (a reporting relationship). Only the former affects permission sets.
- **Real-world ICS roles** to remember: `Operator`, `MaintenanceEngineer`, `ProcessEngineer`, `ShiftSupervisor`, `PlantManager`, `SafetyOfficer`, `SecurityAdministrator`.
- **Combining algorithm** in XACML — *deny-overrides* is the default; explicit Permit must survive all applicable Deny rules.
- **Performance trade-off** — ABAC is more flexible but introduces PIP latency and policy-evaluation overhead; hybrid *RBAC + ABAC* architectures are common in mature ICS deployments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Access Control Models (Discretionary, Mandatory, and Role-Based Access Control)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Access Control Models (Discretionary, Mandatory, and Role-Based Access Control)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Access Control Model?

An **Access Control Model** is a formal, mathematically-defined framework that specifies how subjects (users, processes, devices) are authorized to access objects (files, sensors, PLCs, SCADA resources, network segments) within an Industrial Control System (ICS). It is the policy backbone of any cybersecurity architecture governing authentication, authorization, and audit (the **AAA triad**).

In the context of **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)**, access control is critical because unauthorized access can cause physical damage, environmental hazards, production downtime, and even loss of human life. KTU's PECCT414 syllabus treats access control as the first line of defense after perimeter security.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Access control models are formal security policies that govern **who** can access **what**, **when**, and **how** within a system. The three classical models — DAC, MAC, and RBAC — form the foundational taxonomy for evaluating industrial cybersecurity compliance (aligned with **IEC 62443** and **NIST SP 800-82**).

### 1.2 The Three Foundational Models

| Model | Full Form | Core Principle | Decision Authority |
|-------|-----------|----------------|-------------------|
| **DAC** | Discretionary Access Control | Owner-based control | The resource owner decides |
| **MAC** | Mandatory Access Control | Classification-based control | The system/kernel enforces |
| **RBAC** | Role-Based Access Control | Role-based control | Central administrator |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Three Guards at a Factory Gate

Imagine a large industrial manufacturing plant (your ICS):

- **DAC (The Owner of the Workshop)**: Each workshop head holds the keys to *their own* room. The head of the welding workshop decides who enters the welding bay. If a trusted electrician needs to enter, the welding head personally hands over the key. **Discretion** lies with the owner of each resource.

- **MAC (The Military Bunker)**: Every room is painted with a security color — **Red (Top Secret), Yellow (Confidential), Green (Public)**. Every worker wears a color-coded badge. A Green-badge worker cannot enter a Red room, no matter what — the door will physically refuse to open. The system, not the owner, enforces this. The classification *mandates* the rules.

- **RBAC (The Hospital Ward System)**: A surgeon has a "Surgeon" badge that automatically grants access to Operating Rooms, Medicine Storage, and Patient Records. A nurse has a "Nurse" badge granting different access. The hospital administrator assigns roles; the access permissions are baked into the role, not into individuals. This scales beautifully across hundreds of employees.

### 1.4 Why Access Control Models Matter in ICS

> [!NOTE]
> In **Industrial Control Systems**, the consequences of access failures are not merely data loss — they are kinetic (physical) and potentially catastrophic. A compromised HMI (Human-Machine Interface) operator account can issue malicious setpoints to a PLC controlling a steam turbine, causing mechanical failure or explosion. Therefore, choosing the correct access control model is a **safety-critical engineering decision**, not just an IT policy.

The choice of model in a typical **Purdue Model (ISA-99)** architecture:
- **Levels 0–1** (Field devices, PLCs): Often use **MAC** with hardware-enforced rules
- **Levels 2–3** (SCADA, Engineering Workstations): Commonly use **RBAC** for operational roles
- **Levels 4–5** (Enterprise, Corporate): Frequently employ **DAC** due to collaborative file-sharing needs

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discretionary Access Control (DAC)

**Definition:** A model where the *owner* of an object has full discretion over who can access it and with what privileges. Access is based on the **identity of the subject**, validated through **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** or **capability lists**.

#### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Identification:** Subject presents identity (username, certificate, smart card).
2. **Ownership Verification:** System checks if the requesting subject is the object's owner.
3. **ACL Lookup:** System consults the object's Access Control List — a table mapping subject identities to allowed actions (Read, Write, Execute, Delete).
4. **Authorization Decision:** If subject is in ACL with the required permission, access is granted; otherwise denied.
5. **Propagation (Trojan Horse Risk):** Owners can grant their access rights to other subjects, creating chained trust — a critical security weakness.

#### Mathematical Representation of DAC:
An ACL for an object $O_i$ is a relation:

$$
ACL(O_i) = \{ (s_j, \text{perm}_k) \mid s_j \in S, \text{perm}_k \in \{r, w, x, d\} \}
$$

Where:
- $S$ = set of all subjects
- $\text{perm}_k$ = the permission being granted
- $r$ = read, $w$ = write, $x$ = execute, $d$ = delete

**Strengths:** Flexible, user-friendly, easy to implement in commercial OS (Windows NTFS, Linux POSIX ACLs).

**Weaknesses:** Vulnerable to **Trojan Horse attacks**, **malware propagation**, and insider threats. No centralized policy enforcement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse DAC with simple password protection. DAC requires *identity-based* authorization using ACLs, not just authentication. Authentication ≠ Authorization.

### 2.2 Mandatory Access Control (MAC)

**Definition:** A model where access decisions are made by a central security policy based on **security classifications (labels)** assigned to both subjects and objects. Subjects *cannot* override or transfer their access rights — access is **mandated** by the system kernel.

#### The Two Pillars of MAC: Bell-LaPadula vs. Biba

##### 2.2.1 Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model — *Confidentiality-Focused*
