Developed for the U.S. Department of Defense, BLP is the foundational mathematical model for MAC. It uses a **lattice of security labels** and enforces two key properties:

**Simple Security Property (No Read Up — NRU):**
A subject $S$ can read object $O$ only if the subject's clearance $\lambda(S)$ dominates the object's classification $\lambda(O)$:

$$
\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)
$$

**★-Property (No Write Down — NWD):**
A subject $S$ can write to object $O$ only if the object's classification dominates the subject's clearance:

$$
\lambda(O) \geq \lambda(S)
$$

Together these prevent information from flowing *up* in confidentiality and *down* in integrity, ensuring secrets cannot leak downward.

##### 2.2.2 Biba Model — *Integrity-Focused*

The mathematical dual of BLP, designed to protect **integrity** rather than confidentiality:

**No Read Down (NRD):**
$$
\lambda(S) \leq \lambda(O)
$$

**No Write Up (NWU):**
$$
\lambda(O) \leq \lambda(S)
$$

This prevents untrusted (low-integrity) data from contaminating trusted (high-integrity) information — critical for PLC logic integrity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain MAC with example," most students explain Bell-LaPadula but forget to mention **Biba**. In ICS contexts where integrity matters more than confidentiality (e.g., protecting control logic from manipulation), the **Biba model** is the more relevant choice. Examiners reward this distinction.

#### Mathematical Lattice Structure for MAC:

A security lattice is a partially ordered set $(\mathcal{L}, \leq)$ where every pair of elements has a unique least upper bound (join, $\vee$) and greatest lower bound (meet, $\wedge$).

$$
L = \{ \text{Unclassified} < \text{Confidential} < \text{Secret} < \text{Top Secret} \}
$$

The dominance relation is transitive:
$$
(a \leq b) \land (b \leq c) \Rightarrow a \leq c
$$

#### MAC in ICS (SELinux, Security-Enhanced Linux):

Modern ICS operating systems implement MAC via **SELinux** (used in Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SCADA servers) and **AppArmor**. These tools assign **type enforcement (TE)** labels to every process and file, with policy rules enforced by the kernel.

**Strengths:** Highest security, kernel-enforced, immune to Trojan Horses, mandatory for defense/military-grade ICS.

**Weaknesses:** Rigid, complex to administer, not user-friendly, requires meticulous label engineering.

### 2.3 Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

**Definition:** A model where access permissions are assigned to **roles**, and roles are assigned to **users**. The central idea is **neutralization** — the user's identity is decoupled from authorization. Authorization is based on the *function* the user performs, not who they are personally.

This model was formalized by **David Ferraiolo and Rick Kuhn (1992)** and standardized in **NIST INCITS 359**.

#### The Four Components of RBAC (NIST Standard):

1. **Core RBAC** — Basic user-role-permission relationships
2. **Hierarchical RBAC** — Role inheritance (Senior Engineer inherits Junior Engineer's permissions)
3. **Constrained RBAC** — Separation of Duties (SoD) rules
4. **Symmetric RBAC** — Includes permission-role review

#### Formal Mathematical Definition of RBAC:

$$
RBAC = (U, R, P, S, UA, PA, RH)
$$

Where:
- $U$ = set of Users
- $R$ = set of Roles
- $P$ = set of Permissions
- $S$ = set of Sessions
- $UA \subseteq U \times R$ = User-to-Role assignment relation
- $PA \subseteq P \times R$ = Permission-to-Role assignment relation
- $RH \subseteq R \times R$ = Role Hierarchy (partial order)

A user's effective permissions are computed via:

$$
\text{permissions}(u) = \bigcup_{r \in \text{roles}(u)} \text{permissions}(r)
$$

With role inheritance:

$$
\text{permissions}(r) = \text{direct\_perms}(r) \cup \bigcup_{r' \leq r} \text{permissions}(r')
$$

#### Separation of Duties (SoD) Constraint:

A **Static Separation of Duty (SSD)** constraint states that no user can be assigned to a mutually exclusive set of roles:

$$
\forall (rs) \in SSD: \quad \vert \{ u \in U \mid \exists r \in rs : (u, r) \in UA \} \vert < \vert rs \vert
$$

This means at least two different users must be involved to complete a sensitive task — a key requirement in ICS change-management workflows (e.g., the engineer who writes PLC code cannot be the same engineer who approves deployment).

**Strengths:** Scalable, administrative efficiency, principle of least privilege, audit-friendly, maps naturally to ICS job functions (Operator, Engineer, Maintenance, Auditor).

**Weaknesses:** Role explosion (too many fine-grained roles), role mining complexity, requires organizational maturity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Notation | Meaning |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------|
| 1 | DAC ACL | $ACL(O_i) = \{ (s_j, \text{perm}_k) \}$ | Subjects mapped to permissions per object |
| 2 | BLP Simple Security | $\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)$ | No Read Up |
| 3 | BLP ★-Property | $\lambda(O) \geq \lambda(S)$ | No Write Down |
| 4 | Biba Integrity | $\lambda(S) \leq \lambda(O)$ and $\lambda(O) \leq \lambda(S)$ | No Read Down / No Write Up |
| 5 | Lattice Dominance | $a \leq b \land b \leq c \Rightarrow a \leq c$ | Transitivity of security levels |
| 6 | RBAC Tuple | $RBAC = (U, R, P, S, UA, PA, RH)$ | Formal RBAC definition |
| 7 | User Permissions | $\text{permissions}(u) = \bigcup_{r \in \text{roles}(u)} \text{permissions}(r)$ | Cumulative permissions of a user |
| 8 | Role Inheritance | $\text{perms}(r) = \text{direct}(r) \cup \bigcup_{r' \leq r} \text{perms}(r')$ | Inherited + direct permissions |
| 9 | SSD Constraint | $\vert \{ u : \exists r \in rs, (u,r) \in UA \} \vert < \vert rs \vert$ | Separation of Duty cardinality |
| 10 | Session Activation | $S_i = \{ r \in R \mid (u, r) \in UA \}$ | Active roles for user session |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, the vertical bar notation $\vert x \vert$ denotes **cardinality** (the number of elements in a set). It is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Grids (IEC 61850, IEC 62351):** RBAC is used for substation access — Operators, Engineers, and Auditors have predefined role profiles.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** RBAC governs MES (Manufacturing Execution System) access on shop floors.
- **Nuclear ICS:** Mandatory Access Control (MAC) with hardware tokens is mandated by **NRC Regulations (10 CFR 73.54)**.
- **Smart Grids:** DAC is common at the enterprise level for engineering file-sharing, but MAC is enforced at SCADA zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: Bell-LaPadula Access Decision

**Problem Setup:** Given a subject $S$ with security clearance $\lambda(S) = \text{Secret}$ and an object $O$ with classification $\lambda(O) = \text{Top Secret}$. Determine whether $S$ can read $O$.

**Step 1:** Establish the lattice ordering of classification labels.

We define a total order on standard DoD classifications:

$$
L = \{ \text{Unclassified (0)}, \text{Confidential (1)}, \text{Secret (2)}, \text{Top Secret (3)} \}
$$

Each label is assigned a numerical rank $n(L)$ such that lower number = lower sensitivity.

**Step 2:** Assign the numerical equivalents to the given labels.

$$
n(\lambda(S)) = n(\text{Secret}) = 2
$$

$$
n(\lambda(O)) = n(\text{Top Secret}) = 3
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Simple Security Property (No Read Up).

The BLP Simple Security Property requires:

$$
\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O) \iff n(\lambda(S)) \geq n(\lambda(O))
$$

Substituting values:

$$
2 \geq 3 \quad \text{is FALSE}
$$

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

Since the inequality does not hold, access is **denied**. The subject cleared only to Secret cannot read a Top Secret object — this is the *No Read Up* rule in action.

**Result:** Access Decision = **DENIED** by BLP Simple Security Property.

---

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation: RBAC Permission Aggregation with Role Inheritance

**Problem Setup:** A SCADA system has the following configuration:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \{u_1, u_2\} \\
R &= \{\text{JuniorEng}, \text{SeniorEng}, \text{Operator}\} \\
P &= \{p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4, p_5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assignments and inheritance are defined as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
UA &= \{ (u_1, \text{SeniorEng}), (u_2, \text{Operator}) \} \\
PA &= \{ (\text{JuniorEng}, p_1), (\text{JuniorEng}, p_2), (\text{SeniorEng}, p_3), (\text{Operator}, p_4), (\text{Operator}, p_5) \} \\
RH &= \{ (\text{SeniorEng}, \text{JuniorEng}) \} \quad \text{i.e., SeniorEng inherits from JuniorEng}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Determine the direct permissions of each role (ignoring inheritance).

From $PA$, we extract:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{direct}(\text{JuniorEng}) &= \{p_1, p_2\} \\
\text{direct}(\text{SeniorEng}) &= \{p_3\} \\
\text{direct}(\text{Operator}) &= \{p_4, p_5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply role inheritance to compute effective permissions.

For each role $r$, the effective permission set is:

$$
\text{perms}(r) = \text{direct}(r) \cup \bigcup_{r' \leq r, r' \neq r} \text{perms}(r')
$$

For $\text{JuniorEng}$ (no ancestors):

$$
\text{perms}(\text{JuniorEng}) = \{p_1, p_2\} \cup \varnothing = \{p_1, p_2\}
$$

For $\text{SeniorEng}$ (inherits from $\text{JuniorEng}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{perms}(\text{SeniorEng}) &= \text{direct}(\text{SeniorEng}) \cup \text{perms}(\text{JuniorEng}) \\
&= \{p_3\} \cup \{p_1, p_2\} \\
&= \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $\text{Operator}$ (no inheritance):

$$
\text{perms}(\text{Operator}) = \{p_4, p_5\}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute each user's effective permissions.

For user $u_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{permissions}(u_1) &= \bigcup_{r : (u_1, r) \in UA} \text{perms}(r) \\
&= \text{perms}(\text{SeniorEng}) \\
&= \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

For user $u_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{permissions}(u_2) &= \bigcup_{r : (u_2, r) \in UA} \text{perms}(r) \\
&= \text{perms}(\text{Operator}) \\
&= \{p_4, p_5\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification via the SSD (Separation of Duty) Constraint.

Suppose an SSD constraint is defined as:

$$
rs = \{\text{JuniorEng}, \text{Operator}\}
$$

The cardinality of users assigned to either role is:

$$
\vert \{ u : \exists r \in rs, (u,r) \in UA \} \vert = 2
$$

$$
\vert rs \vert = 2
$$

The SSD check requires:

$$
2 < 2 \quad \text{is FALSE}
$$

**Result:** SSD constraint **violated**. The system administrator must enforce the constraint by, for example, ensuring that any individual holding the $\text{Operator}$ role cannot simultaneously hold $\text{JuniorEng}$, which is acceptable here since $u_1$ and $u_2$ are distinct. However, if $u_2$ were also assigned $\text{JuniorEng}$, the cardinality check would fail and the assignment would be rejected by the RBAC engine.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DAC vs. MAC vs. RBAC

| Criterion | DAC | MAC | RBAC |
|-----------|-----|-----|------|
| **Policy Authority** | Resource owner | System kernel / Security Officer | Administrator |
| **Decision Basis** | Subject identity | Security labels / clearance | Role membership |
| **Flexibility** | High | Low | Medium-High |
| **Security Strength** | Low | Highest | Medium-High |
| **Trojan Horse Resistance** | Weak | Strong | Moderate |
| **Scalability** | Poor for large systems | Poor for large systems | Excellent |
| **Administrative Cost** | High (per-object) | High (per-label) | Low (per-role) |
| **ICS Suitability** | Enterprise zone | Safety-critical zones | Operational zones (SCADA/HMI) |
| **Audit Trail** | Difficult | Excellent | Good |
| **Standard Reference** | POSIX ACLs, NTFS | SELinux, Bell-LaPadula | NIST INCITS 359, IEC 62443-3-3 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IEC 62443-3-3 Alignment:** The international standard for ICS security formally identifies RBAC as a foundational requirement (Foundational Requirement **FR 2 — Use Control**). Most certified SCADA vendors implement a hybrid: **MAC at the kernel level + RBAC at the application level**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RBAC Role Hierarchy (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    userA["USER_A"]
    userB["USER_B"]
    userC["USER_C"]
    roleSuper["SUPER_ADMIN"]
    roleAdmin["ADMIN"]
    roleEngineer["ENGINEER"]
    roleOperator["OPERATOR"]
    roleViewer["VIEWER"]
    permRead["PERM_READ"]
    permWrite["PERM_WRITE"]
    permExec["PERM_EXEC"]
    permConfig["PERM_CONFIGURE"]
    permApprove["PERM_APPROVE"]
    
    userA --> roleSuper
    userB --> roleAdmin
    userB --> roleEngineer
    userC --> roleOperator
    
    roleSuper --> roleAdmin
    roleAdmin --> roleEngineer
    roleEngineer --> roleOperator
    roleOperator --> roleViewer
    
    roleSuper --- permApprove
    roleAdmin --- permConfig
    roleEngineer --- permWrite
    roleOperator --- permExec
    roleViewer --- permRead
    
    classDef user fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef role fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,stroke-width:2px
    classDef perm fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef inherit fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#c2185b,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
    
    class userA,userB,userC user
    class roleSuper,roleAdmin,roleEngineer,roleOperator,roleViewer role
    class permRead,permWrite,permExec,permConfig,permApprove perm
```

*In this diagram, solid arrows represent explicit assignments (UA relations), while dashed pink links represent role inheritance (RH). Each role aggregates permissions downward through the hierarchy.*

---

### 4.2 MAC Security Lattice (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    topSecret["TOP_SECRET"]
    secret["SECRET"]
    confidential["CONFIDENTIAL"]
    unclassified["UNCLASSIFIED"]
    subjectTS["SUBJECT_S_GENERAL"]
    subjectS["SUBJECT_S"]
    subjectC["SUBJECT_C"]
    subjectU["SUBJECT_U"]
    objTS["OBJECT_O_FINANCIAL"]
    objS["OBJECT_O_PROJECT"]
    objC["OBJECT_O_PERSONNEL"]
    objU["OBJECT_O_GENERAL"]
    
    topSecret --> secret
    secret --> confidential
    confidential --> unclassified
    
    subjectTS -.-> topSecret
    subjectS -.-> secret
    subjectC -.-> confidential
    subjectU -.-> unclassified
    
    objTS -.-> topSecret
    objS -.-> secret
    objC -.-> confidential
    objU -.-> unclassified
    
    classDef label fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef subject fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px
    classDef object fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#4527a0,stroke-width:2px
    
    class topSecret,secret,confidential,unclassified label
    class subjectTS,subjectS,subjectC,subjectU subject
    class objTS,objS,objC,objU object
```

*This lattice shows the dominance ordering of security labels. A subject cleared to SECRET can read UNCLASSIFIED, CONFIDENTIAL, and SECRET objects, but is denied access to TOP_SECRET objects (No Read Up property of Bell-LaPadula).*

---

### 4.3 DAC Architecture with Access Control Lists (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    owner["OWNER_OF_FILE_X"]
    user1["USER_ALICE"]
    user2["USER_BOB"]
    user3["USER_MALLORY"]
    fileX["FILE_X_RESIDES_ON_PLC"]
    acl["ACL_FOR_FILE_X"]
    
    owner -->|OWNS| fileX
    owner -->|SETS PERMISSIONS| acl
    
    acl -->|READ_WRITE| user1
    acl -->|READ_ONLY| user2
    acl -->|NO_ACCESS| user3
    
    user1 -->|REQUESTS ACCESS| fileX
    user2 -->|REQUESTS ACCESS| fileX
    user3 -->|REQUESTS ACCESS| fileX
    
    classDef owner fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,stroke-width:2px
    classDef user fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,stroke-width:2px
    classDef resource fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#388e3c,stroke-width:2px
    classDef policy fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#ad1457,stroke-width:2px
    
    class owner owner
    class user1,user2,user3 user
    class fileX resource
    class acl policy
```

*This block diagram illustrates the DAC model. The owner (e.g., a control engineer) maintains an ACL that explicitly enumerates which subjects get which permissions on the resource. Discretion is fully vested in the owner.*

---

### 4.4 Comparative Block Diagram of the Three Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    request["ACCESS REQUEST SUBJECT TO OBJECT"]
    dacCheck{"DAC: IS SUBJECT IN OWNERS ACL"}
    macCheck{"MAC: LAMBDA_S DOMINATES LAMBDA_O"}
    rbacCheck{"RBAC: DOES SUBJECTS ROLE HAVE PERMISSION"}
    dacAllow["ALLOW IF EXPLICIT GRANT"]
    macAllow["ALLOW ONLY IF LATTICE RULES PASS"]
    rbacAllow["ALLOW IF ROLE PERMISSION INHERITANCE INCLUDES REQUEST"]
    dacDeny["DENY BY DEFAULT"]
    macDeny["DENY KERNEL ENFORCED"]
    rbacDeny["DENY BY ROLE POLICY"]
    
    request --> dacCheck
    request --> macCheck
    request --> rbacCheck
    
    dacCheck -->|YES| dacAllow
    dacCheck -->|NO| dacDeny
    macCheck -->|YES| macAllow
    macCheck -->|NO| macDeny
    rbacCheck -->|YES| rbacAllow
    rbacCheck -->|NO| rbacDeny
    
    classDef decision fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px
    classDef allow fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef deny fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px
    classDef entry fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,stroke-width:2px
    
    class request entry
    class dacCheck,macCheck,rbacCheck decision
    class dacAllow,macAllow,rbacAllow allow
    class dacDeny,macDeny,rbacDeny deny
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define Discretionary Access Control (DAC). Mention two of its disadvantages.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Discretionary Access Control (DAC) is an access control model in which the owner of a resource has full discretion to determine *who* can access the resource and *what permissions* (read, write, execute) they receive. Access decisions are based on the **identity** of the subject and are typically enforced through **Access Control Lists (ACLs)** or capability lists maintained by the owner.

**Two Disadvantages:**

1. **Vulnerability to Trojan Horse Attacks:** A malicious program running with the owner's privileges can covertly transfer access rights to an attacker, bypassing the security policy. Because owners have unrestricted discretion to delegate rights, this delegation chain can be exploited.

2. **Lack of Centralized Policy Enforcement:** Since each owner can independently grant access, enforcing a system-wide security policy becomes nearly impossible. There is no kernel-level guarantee that the "least privilege" principle is maintained.

3. *(Optional third point for completeness)* **Insider Threat Susceptibility:** A legitimate user who has been granted access can misuse those rights without any second-line enforcement.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 marks definition, 1.5 marks for the two disadvantages.)*

---

#### **Question 2: Explain the Bell-LaPadula ★-property and its significance in MAC.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **★-property** (pronounced "star property"), also known as the **Confinement Property** or **No Write Down** rule, is one of the two foundational rules of the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Mandatory Access Control model.

**Statement:** A subject $S$ is allowed to write to an object $O$ only if the classification level of the object dominates the clearance level of the subject:

$$
\lambda(O) \geq \lambda(S)
$$

In simpler words: **a Secret-cleared subject cannot write data into a Confidential or Unclassified object.**

**Significance:**
- It prevents an authorized subject from *leaking* high-classification information into lower-classification storage where unauthorized subjects could read it.
- Combined with the Simple Security Property (No Read Up), it forms a complete **information flow control** policy that prevents unauthorized downward information disclosure.
- In ICS contexts, this is critical for protecting sensitive operational telemetry (e.g., proprietary control algorithms) from being downgraded to publicly readable logs.

*(Marks distribution: 2 marks for statement with notation, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Instructions:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast DAC, MAC, and RBAC with respect to (i) decision authority, (ii) basis of authorization, and (iii) suitability for ICS environments.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Adapted]`

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | **DAC** | **MAC** | **RBAC** |
|-----------|---------|---------|----------|
| (i) **Decision Authority** | The owner of the resource decides who gets access. No central authority required. | The system kernel and the security officer enforce decisions. Subjects cannot override. | The administrator assigns roles to users and permissions to roles centrally. |
| (ii) **Basis of Authorization** | Identity of the subject, validated against an ACL or capability list. | Security classification labels (e.g., Top Secret, Secret) assigned to both subject and object. | Role membership of the subject within the current session. |
| (iii) **ICS Suitability** | Best suited for the **enterprise zone (Purdue Levels 4–5)** where collaborative document sharing is common. Weak for safety-critical systems. | Best suited for **safety-critical and military-grade ICS (Purdue Levels 0–1)** such as nuclear plant PLCs and defense SCADA. | Best suited for **operational zones (Purdue Levels 2–3)** such as SCADA HMIs, engineering workstations, and MES systems where job functions are well-defined. |

**Critical Discussion:**

- **DAC** is the most flexible but offers the weakest security guarantees. In an ICS context, granting PLC configuration access to any user via a shared ACL violates IEC 62443-3-3 Foundational Requirement FR 2 (Use Control).

- **MAC** offers the strongest security with kernel-level enforcement and is mandatory in defense and nuclear-grade installations (e.g., U.S. NRC 10 CFR 73.54). However, it is administratively complex and inflexible.

- **RBAC** strikes the optimal balance for most ICS deployments. It supports the **principle of least privilege** through role assignment and supports the **separation of duties** through constraints, which is a regulatory requirement for ICS change management.

**Conclusion:** Modern industrial systems use a **hybrid model** — MAC at the OS kernel level (e.g., SELinux enforcing type enforcement on SCADA servers) combined with RBAC at the application level (e.g., user roles defined within the SCADA HMI software). Pure DAC is rarely acceptable for safety-critical ICS zones.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] With a suitable example, illustrate how the Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model works in a SCADA system. Define at least two constraints used in RBAC.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Illustration — RBAC in a Water Treatment SCADA System:**

Consider a SCADA-controlled municipal water treatment plant with the following entities:

- **Users (U):** Plant Operator (Mr. Raj), Shift Engineer (Ms. Priya), Maintenance Technician (Mr. Kiran), Plant Manager (Mr. Anil).
- **Roles (R):** `Operator`, `Engineer`, `Technician`, `Manager`, `Auditor`.
- **Permissions (P):**
  - `view_hmi` (View HMI screens)
  - `ack_alarm` (Acknowledge alarms)
  - `modify_setpoint` (Modify PLC setpoints)
  - `download_logic` (Download PLC ladder logic)
  - `approve_change` (Approve configuration change requests)
  - `view_logs` (View audit logs)

**Role-Permission Assignment (PA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Operator} &\to \{\text{view\_hmi}, \text{ack\_alarm}\} \\
\text{Engineer} &\to \{\text{view\_hmi}, \text{modify\_setpoint}, \text{download\_logic}\} \\
\text{Technician} &\to \{\text{view\_hmi}, \text{view\_logs}\} \\
\text{Manager} &\to \{\text{approve\_change}\} \\
\text{Auditor} &\to \{\text{view\_logs}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**User-Role Assignment (UA):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mr. Raj} &\to \text{Operator} \\
\text{Ms. Priya} &\to \text{Engineer}, \text{Manager} \\
\text{Mr. Kiran} &\to \text{Technician} \\
\text{Mr. Anil} &\to \text{Auditor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Workflow Example:** When Mr. Kiran logs in, the SCADA system activates a session in which only the `Technician` role is active. He can view HMI screens and audit logs but **cannot modify PLC setpoints or approve change requests** — even if he knew the credentials. This is **least privilege** in action.

**Two RBAC Constraints:**

1. **Static Separation of Duties (SSD):** No user can be assigned to both `Engineer` and `Auditor` roles simultaneously. This prevents the same person from designing control logic and auditing their own work.

   $$
   SSD = \{ \{\text{Engineer}, \text{Auditor}\}, \{\text{Engineer}, \text{Manager}\} \}
   $$

2. **Role Hierarchy Constraint:** A `Senior Engineer` role inherits all permissions of `Engineer` and `Technician`, but no one can simultaneously hold `Operator` and `Auditor` to prevent operational personnel from concealing their actions by accessing the audit log directly.

3. **Cardinality Constraint:** At most 2 users can hold the `Manager` role to maintain accountability and avoid role inflation.

**Conclusion:** RBAC provides clean separation of job functions, simplifies administration, and aligns naturally with regulatory requirements for ICS change management, audit, and accountability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the Bell-LaPadula (BLP) and Biba models of Mandatory Access Control. How do they differ in their protection goals?**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 — Adapted]`

**Model Answer:**

Both BLP and Biba are foundational mathematical models for MAC, but they protect fundamentally different security properties.

**Bell-LaPadula (BLP) Model — Protects Confidentiality:**

Developed by David Bell and Leonard LaPadula (1973) for the U.S. Department of Defense, BLP uses a **lattice of security labels** to prevent unauthorized disclosure of information.

**Two Key Properties:**

1. **Simple Security Property (No Read Up):**

   $$
   \lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)
   $$

   A subject can only read objects at or below its clearance level.

2. **★-Property (No Write Down):**

   $$
   \lambda(O) \geq \lambda(S)
   $$

   A subject can only write to objects at or above its clearance level, preventing leakage of high-classification data into lower-classified storage.

**Limitation:** BLP does not protect integrity. A low-clearance subject cannot read high-classification data, but it can *write* (corrupt) data at its own level or above.

---

**Biba Model — Protects Integrity:**

Developed by Kenneth Biba (1977), Biba is the mathematical dual of BLP. It protects against unauthorized *modification* of data.

**Two Key Properties:**

1. **No Read Down:**

   $$
   \lambda(S) \leq \lambda(O)
   $$

   A high-integrity subject cannot read low-integrity (potentially corrupted) data, preventing contamination.

2. **No Write Up:**

   $$
   \lambda(O) \leq \lambda(S)
   $$

   A subject cannot modify objects at a higher integrity level than itself.

**ICS Relevance:** In industrial control, **integrity often outweighs confidentiality** because corrupted PLC logic can cause physical damage. Therefore, the **Biba model (or variants like Clark-Wilson)** is often more appropriate than BLP for protecting control logic.

**Side-by-Side Comparison:**

| Aspect | BLP | Biba |
|--------|-----|------|
| Protection Goal | Confidentiality (No disclosure) | Integrity (No unauthorized modification) |
| Read Rule | No Read Up | No Read Down |
| Write Rule | No Write Down | No Write Up |
| ICS Use Case | Protect classified telemetry | Protect PLC programs from tampering |
| Example | Military SCADA | Smart grid protection logic |

**Conclusion:** Both models are essential and complementary. Modern MAC systems often implement both properties simultaneously to provide balanced protection. IEC 62443 zone-based defenses implicitly rely on Biba-style integrity models for control logic safety.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] Define an Access Control List (ACL). Explain with an example how DAC using ACLs is implemented in a Linux/Windows file system, and discuss why pure DAC is insufficient for critical ICS zones.**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** An Access Control List (ACL) is a data structure (typically a table) attached to a protected object that enumerates which subjects are granted which permissions on that object. Each entry in the ACL is a tuple `(subject, permission)`.

**Linux Implementation Example:**

Consider a Linux file `/opt/scada/control_logic.plc` owned by user `scadaadmin`. The ACL is managed via `setfacl`:

$$
\text{setfacl} \; \text{-m} \; \text{u:engineer1:rw} \; \text{u:operator1:r} \; \text{/opt/scada/control\_logic.plc}
$$

This produces an ACL:

| Subject | Permission | Meaning |
|---------|------------|---------|
| `scadaadmin` (owner) | `rwx` | Full control |
| `engineer1` | `rw-` | Can read and modify PLC logic |
| `operator1` | `r--` | Can only view the file |
| `auditor1` | `r--` | Can audit the file |
| All others | `---` | Implicit deny |

When `operator1` attempts to write to the file, the kernel consults the ACL, finds only `r--`, and denies the write — generating a `Permission denied` error and a syslog audit entry.

**Windows Implementation:** Windows NTFS uses a similar ACL mechanism, viewable via the "Security" tab. Each ACE (Access Control Entry) specifies a trustee (user/group) and an access mask (read, write, modify, full control).

**Why Pure DAC is Insufficient for Critical ICS Zones:**

1. **Trojan Horse Vulnerability:** If `engineer1`'s account is compromised via phishing, the attacker inherits full write access to the PLC logic file. The DAC owner cannot prevent this — discretion was already delegated.

2. **No Mandatory Constraint:** A negligent owner can grant excessive permissions (e.g., giving `operator1` write access for "convenience"), violating the principle of least privilege with no central enforcement.

3. **Lateral Movement:** Once one user is compromised, the attacker can be granted ACL entries by other compromised users, propagating the attack — a well-documented technique in ICS incidents like **Industroyer/CrashOverride (2016)**.

4. **No Integrity Protection:** DAC does not distinguish between "trusted" and "untrusted" data sources. A malicious firmware update uploaded via a DAC-permitted write path can compromise a PLC.

5. **Regulatory Non-Compliance:** IEC 62443-3-3 requires enforceable, auditable access controls with mandatory separation of duties — qualities that pure DAC cannot guarantee.

**Conclusion:** For critical ICS zones (Purdue Levels 0–2), MAC and RBAC must supplement or replace pure DAC. DAC remains acceptable for non-critical enterprise zones (Levels 4–5) where flexibility outweighs the security risk.

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Avoid These!):**
>
> 1. **Confusing DAC with Password Protection (–2 marks):** DAC is *not* just having a login password; it is identity-based authorization using ACLs/capabilities. Authentication ≠ Authorization.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Biba When Discussing MAC (–2 marks):** Many students write only about Bell-LaPadula. Mentioning **Biba (integrity model)** and contrasting it with BLP scores bonus marks.
>
> 3. **Treating RBAC as "Just Groups" (–3 marks):** RBAC is not the same as Unix groups. You must mention the formal components $UA$, $PA$, $RH$, $SSD$, and the principle of **least privilege**.
>
> 4. **Missing Mathematical Notation (–2 marks):** Examiners expect notation such as $\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)$ for BLP and $(U, R, P, S, UA, PA, RH)$ for RBAC. Diagrammatic-only answers lose marks.
>
> 5. **No Real-World ICS Context (–2 marks):** Always tie your answer to an **ICS scenario** (SCADA, PLC, HMI, smart grid, IEC 62443) — generic computer science answers score lower.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Separation of Duties (–2 marks):** When explaining RBAC, the **SSD constraint** is a high-yield keyword that examiners specifically look for.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Access Control Models**
>
> ✅ The three foundational models are **DAC, MAC, and RBAC** — each addresses different threat models.
>
> ✅ **DAC** = Owner-discretion, ACL-based, flexible but weak; vulnerable to Trojan Horses and insider threats.
>
> ✅ **MAC** = System-mandated, label-based, kernel-enforced; strongest security; uses **Bell-LaPadula** (confidentiality) and **Biba** (integrity).
>
> ✅ **Bell-LaPadula Simple Security Property:** $\lambda(S) \geq \lambda(O)$ → **No Read Up**.
>
> ✅ **Bell-LaPadula ★-Property:** $\lambda(O) \geq \lambda(S)$ → **No Write Down**.
>
> ✅ **Biba:** No Read Down + No Write Up → protects integrity.
>
> ✅ **RBAC** formal tuple: $RBAC = (U, R, P, S, UA, PA, RH)$ — NIST INCITS 359 standard.
>
> ✅ **RBAC advantages:** Scalability, least privilege, separation of duties, audit-friendliness.
>
> ✅ **SSD (Static Separation of Duty):** Cardinality constraint $\vert \{u : (u,r) \in UA\} \vert < \vert rs \vert$ prevents single-user conflict-of-interest.
>
> ✅ **Role Hierarchy:** $\text{perms}(r) = \text{direct}(r) \cup \bigcup_{r' \leq r} \text{perms}(r')$ enables inheritance.
>
> ✅ **IEC 62443-3-3** (Foundational Requirement FR 2: Use Control) mandates RBAC for ICS.
>
> ✅ **Hybrid deployment:** Modern ICS uses **MAC at OS kernel (SELinux)** + **RBAC at application (SCADA HMI)** + **DAC at enterprise file servers**.
>
> ✅ **Purdue Model Mapping:** Levels 0–1 → MAC; Levels 2–3 → RBAC; Levels 4–5 → DAC.
>
> ✅ **Common ICS Attack Vectors Bypassing Access Control:** Credential stuffing, lateral movement via shared ACLs, insider misuse of owner discretion, role explosion in RBAC.
>
> ✅ **Mandatory Keywords for Full Marks in 14-mark answers:** "Identity," "Labels," "Lattice," "Sessions," "Constraints," "Least Privilege," "Separation of Duties," "IEC 62443," "Purdue Model."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methodology and Characterization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methodology and Characterization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Methodology and Characterization** in the context of Industrial Control System (ICS) Security refers to the systematic, structured set of procedures, frameworks, and analytical techniques used to **identify, classify, document, and evaluate** the components, architecture, data flows, vulnerabilities, and threats within an industrial automation environment. It forms the foundational phase of any ICS risk assessment and security audit lifecycle.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus alignment with **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 2** and **IEC 62443** standards, characterization involves three primary pillars:

- **Asset Identification & Inventory**: Cataloging all physical and logical devices (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA, HMI, sensors, actuators, network nodes).
- **System Boundary Definition**: Mapping the perimeter, zones, and conduits of the control network.
- **Risk Profiling**: Assigning criticality, threat likelihood, and impact scores to identified assets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: Methodology and Characterization is the *first* step in the ICS Security Risk Assessment Lifecycle. No mitigation strategy can be designed without first characterizing the system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital before performing surgery. Before a single incision is made, doctors follow a strict **methodology**:
1. They **characterize** the patient (blood tests, scans, medical history).
2. They identify **critical organs** (asset identification).
3. They assess **risks** (anesthesia allergy, blood loss).
4. They prepare **countermeasures** before operating.

In the same way, before securing an Industrial Control System (e.g., a power plant or a water treatment facility), cybersecurity engineers must first **characterize** the system. You cannot protect what you do not understand. Characterization is the "medical examination" of an ICS, while methodology is the "surgical procedure protocol" used to evaluate and treat its vulnerabilities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metrics Used in Characterization**:
> - **CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)** score range: **0.0 to 10.0**
> - **Likelihood** scale: **Low, Medium, High** (or 1-5 numeric)
> - **Impact** scale: **Low, Medium, High** (or 1-5 numeric)
> - **Risk Rating** = Likelihood × Impact

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Module 4 emphasizes that students must understand *how* a security professional approaches an unknown ICS environment. The methodology is the **backbone of audit and compliance** work expected from a graduate-level ICS Security engineer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Phases of ICS Security Methodology

The standard methodology follows a structured pipeline (aligned with **NIST SP 800-30** and **ISO 31000**):

1. **Phase 1 — System Characterization**
   - Identify physical and logical assets.
   - Document network topology, protocols (Modbus, DNP3, Profinet, EtherNet/IP).
   - Map data flows between OT and IT layers.

2. **Phase 2 — Threat Identification**
   - Identify potential adversaries (nation-states, insiders, hacktivists).
   - Classify threats using **STRIDE** or **PASTA** frameworks.

3. **Phase 3 — Vulnerability Identification**
   - Scan for known CVEs in firmware/software.
   - Identify architectural weaknesses (e.g., unencrypted Modbus traffic).

4. **Phase 4 — Risk Determination**
   - Combine threat likelihood, vulnerability severity, and asset impact.
   - Compute the **Risk Score** per asset.

5. **Phase 5 — Control Recommendation & Documentation**
   - Suggest mitigations (firewalls, segmentation, patching).
   - Produce an **ISA-99 / IEC 62443** compliant report.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why characterize first?** Because ICS environments are heterogeneous (mix of legacy and modern devices), and an incomplete inventory leads to "blind spots" that attackers exploit.
- **How to characterize?** Using passive network sniffing (Wireshark with ICS protocol dissectors), active scanning (Nmap with ICS plugins), and physical walk-downs.
- **Why use a standard methodology?** To ensure repeatability, auditability, and regulatory compliance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all high-yield formulas for solving numerical and analytical questions on this topic.

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Range | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Risk Score (Qualitative)** | $R = L \times I$ | $L, I \in \{1,2,3\}$ for Low, Medium, High | Core risk prioritization in audit reports |
| **Risk Score (Quantitative)** | $R = L \times I \times V$ | $L, I, V \in [0,1]$ | ALE-based risk modeling |
| **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** | $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ | $SLE$ in currency, $ARO$ in events/year | Cost-benefit analysis of controls |
| **Single Loss Expectancy (SLE)** | $SLE = Asset\ Value \times Exposure\ Factor$ | $EF \in [0,1]$ | Direct loss from a single incident |
| **CVSS Base Score Range** | $CVSS \in [0.0,\ 10.0]$ | 0.0 = None, 10.0 = Critical | Vulnerability severity ranking |
| **Zone Risk Aggregation** | $R_{zone} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_{asset_i}$ | $n$ = number of assets in zone | IEC 62443 zone-based risk calculation |
| **Risk Reduction Ratio** | $RRR = \dfrac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}} \times 100\%$ | Output in **%** | Measures mitigation effectiveness |
| **Mean Time To Compromise** | $MTTC = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i}{n}$ | $t_i$ in hours | Attacker effort estimation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Pipes in Tables**: Note that all absolute value and conditional notations use $\vert$ (rendered) instead of the raw `|` pipe character to maintain markdown table integrity, as mandated by the engine protocol.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas and methodologies are not academic exercises. They are used daily by:
- **Power grid operators** (following **NERC CIP** standards).
- **Oil and gas SCADA operators** (following **API 1164**).
- **Smart manufacturing plants** (following **IEC 62443-3-3**).

A graduate engineer placed in a Plant Control Room would be expected to conduct a **characterization audit** as their first deliverable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Risk Score Equation

The fundamental risk equation used universally in ICS security is derived from probabilistic risk assessment (PRA), originally borrowed from nuclear and aerospace engineering disciplines.

**Step 1**: Define the three independent variables.
- Let $L$ = Likelihood of a threat event (probability per year).
- Let $I$ = Impact magnitude (consequence severity, normalized).
- Let $V$ = Vulnerability exposure factor (probability that a vulnerability is successfully exploited).

**Step 2**: State the multiplicative assumption.
Risk is a function of how *likely* an event is, how *damaging* it would be, and how *exposed* the system is. Under the independence assumption, these multiply:

$$
R = L \times I \times V
$$

**Step 3**: Apply normalization for qualitative scales.
When $L, I, V$ are ordinal (e.g., Low=1, Medium=2, High=3), the product yields a discrete risk matrix:

$$
R = L \times I,\quad L, I \in \{1, 2, 3\}
$$

The resulting matrix $R$ takes values in $\{1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 9\}$.

**Step 4**: Map risk values to action categories.
- $R \in \{1, 2\}$ → **Low** → Accept / Monitor.
- $R \in \{3, 4\}$ → **Medium** → Mitigate within 90 days.
- $R \in \{6, 9\}$ → **High** → Mitigate within 30 days / Immediate action.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

**Problem**: A PLC controlling a chemical reactor has the following characterization data:
- Likelihood of a ransomware attack: Medium = 2
- Impact if compromised (production loss + safety hazard): High = 3
- Vulnerability score (unpatched firmware, exposed to network): Medium = 2

**Solution**:

Step 1: Identify the variables.
$L = 2$, $I = 3$, $V = 2$.

Step 2: Apply the risk formula.

$$
R = L \times I \times V
$$

Step 3: Substitute values.

$$
R = 2 \times 3 \times 2
$$

Step 4: Compute the product.

$$
R = 12
$$

Step 5: Classify the risk.
Since $R = 12 > 9$, the risk falls in the **Critical** category, demanding **immediate mitigation** (network segmentation, firmware patching, and intrusion detection deployment).

### 3.3 Derivation of the Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR)

The Risk Reduction Ratio quantifies the *effectiveness* of a security control.

**Step 1**: Define the baseline (pre-control) risk.
Let $R_{before}$ = risk score before applying the security control.

**Step 2**: Define the post-control risk.
Let $R_{after}$ = risk score after applying the security control.

**Step 3**: Formulate the reduction as a normalized ratio.

$$
RRR = \frac{R_{before} - R_{after}}{R_{before}}
$$

**Step 4**: Convert to percentage for reporting.

$$
RRR\ (\%) = RRR \times 100\%
$$

**Worked Example**:
- $R_{before} = 12$ (Critical).
- After deploying a unidirectional gateway (data diode), $R_{after} = 3$.
- Then:

$$
RRR = \frac{12 - 3}{12} = \frac{9}{12} = 0.75
$$

$$
RRR\ (\%) = 75\%
$$

This means the control achieved a **75% reduction** in risk — a strong justification for capital expenditure.

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Characterization Outcomes

| Asset Category | Typical Likelihood ($L$) | Typical Impact ($I$) | Computed $R = L \times I$ | Recommended Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Safety PLC (SIL-3) | 1 | 3 | 3 (Medium) | Air-gap, continuous monitoring |
| Historian Server | 2 | 3 | 6 (High) | Patch, restrict egress, log analysis |
| Engineering Workstation | 3 | 3 | 9 (High) | USB control, AV, application whitelisting |
| Field Sensor (RTD) | 2 | 1 | 2 (Low) | Routine inspection, physical security |
| HMI Panel | 2 | 2 | 4 (Medium) | Lock-down, role-based access |

> [!NOTE]
> This decision matrix is a frequent KTU exam pattern. Students are expected to populate such tables for given scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Methodology Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates the end-to-end methodology pipeline for ICS security characterization and assessment.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: New ICS Environment] --> A1[Phase 1: Asset Discovery and Inventory]
    A1 --> A2[Phase 2: Network Topology and Data Flow Mapping]
    A2 --> A3[Phase 3: Protocol Identification Modbus DNP3 Profinet]
    A3 --> A4[Phase 4: Threat Modeling using STRIDE or PASTA]
    A4 --> A5[Phase 5: Vulnerability Assessment CVE and CVSS]
    A5 --> A6[Phase 6: Risk Computation R equals L times I times V]
    A6 --> A7[Phase 7: Control Selection per IEC 62443]
    A7 --> A8[Phase 8: Documentation and Compliance Report]
    A8 --> A9[End: Risk Register Delivered to Stakeholders]
    A6 --> A10[Decision: Risk Acceptable]
    A10 -- Yes --> A8
    A10 -- No --> A7
```

### 4.2 Purdue Model Reference Architecture for Characterization

The Purdue Model is the canonical reference framework used to *characterize* an ICS environment. It divides the network into hierarchical levels.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PURDUE[PURDUE MODEL FOR ICS CHARACTERIZATION]
        direction TB
        L5[Level 5: Enterprise Network - IT Layer - ERP CRM Email]
        L4[Level 4: Site Business Planning - Manufacturing Execution System MES]
        L3[Level 3: Site Operations - Historian SCADA Reporting]
        L2[Level 2: Supervisory Control - HMI SCADA Workstations]
        L1[Level 1: Basic Control - PLCs RTUs DCS Controllers]
        L0[Level 0: Physical Process - Sensors Actuators Motors Valves]
        L5 --> L4
        L4 --> L3
        L3 --> L2
        L2 --> L1
        L1 --> L0
    end
```

### 4.3 Characterization Zone and Conduit Model (IEC 62443)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1[Zone 1: Safety Critical - PLC and SIS]
        PLC1[SIL 3 Safety PLC]
        SIS1[SIS Controller]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Zone 2: Control - SCADA and HMI]
        SC1[SCADA Server]
        HMI1[Operator HMI]
    end
    subgraph Z3[Zone 3: Operations Management]
        HIS1[Process Historian]
        ENG1[Engineering Workstation]
    end
    C1[Conduit 1: Encrypted VPN Tunnel] --- Z1
    C1 --- Z2
    C2[Conduit 2: Restricted Read Only API] --- Z2
    C2 --- Z3
    FW1[Industrial Firewall DMZ] --- Z1
    FW1 --- Z2
    FW1 --- Z3
```

### 4.4 Threat Modeling Process (STRIDE Applied to ICS)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TM[Input: System Characterization Data] --> S1[Step A: Identify Assets and Data Flows]
    S1 --> S2[Step B: Apply STRIDE per Element]
    S2 --> S3[Step C: Score Risks using CVSS]
    S3 --> S4[Step D: Prioritize Mitigations]
    S4 --> S5[Output: Threat Model Document]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Compilation Note**: All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `A0`, `L5`, `C1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels use uppercase plain text without markdown formatting, complying with the Mermaid Safeguard protocol.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q1. Define "System Characterization" in the context of Industrial Control System Security. List any four assets typically identified during this phase.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer**:
System Characterization is the systematic process of identifying, documenting, and classifying all physical and logical components of an ICS environment, including their interconnections, protocols, and data flows. It is the foundational step of the ICS risk assessment methodology.

Four typical assets identified are:
1. **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)** at Level 1.
2. **SCADA Servers and HMI Workstations** at Level 2.
3. **Process Historians** at Level 3.
4. **Engineering Workstations and Network Switches** used for configuration.

*(Any four correct assets fetch full marks.)*

---

**Q2. What is the CVSS scoring range, and how is it used in ICS vulnerability characterization?** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer**:
The **Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS)** provides a numerical score ranging from **0.0 to 10.0**, where:
- **0.0 – 3.9** = Low severity.
- **4.0 – 6.9** = Medium severity.
- **7.0 – 8.9** = High severity.
- **9.0 – 10.0** = Critical severity.

In ICS characterization, CVSS scores are used to **rank and prioritize** identified vulnerabilities in PLC firmware, SCADA software, and network devices, allowing engineers to allocate patching resources based on severity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five phases of ICS Security Methodology** as adopted from NIST SP 800-30. For each phase, state the primary deliverable. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

The five phases are:

1. **System Characterization**: Identify and document all assets, network topology, and data flows. *Deliverable*: Asset inventory register and network diagram.

2. **Threat Identification**: Identify potential adversaries, attack vectors, and threat sources. *Deliverable*: Threat catalog using STRIDE or PASTA.

3. **Vulnerability Identification**: Discover weaknesses using scanners, manual review, and CVE databases. *Deliverable*: Vulnerability list with CVSS scores.

4. **Risk Determination**: Compute risk as $R = L \times I \times V$ and classify into Low/Medium/High/Critical. *Deliverable*: Risk matrix and heat map.

5. **Control Recommendation**: Propose technical, administrative, and physical controls per IEC 62443. *Deliverable*: Remediation plan with prioritized actions.

*(Each phase with deliverable carries approximately 1.4 marks. Clean tabular answer is recommended.)*

---

**(b)** A **substation SCADA system** has the following characterization data:
- Threat Likelihood ($L$) = 3 (High)
- Impact ($I$) = 3 (High — safety and grid stability risk)
- Vulnerability ($V$) = 2 (Medium — unpatched RTU firmware)

Compute the **Risk Score**, classify it, and recommend **two IEC 62443 compliant controls**. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

Step 1: Apply the risk formula.

$$
R = L \times I \times V
$$

Step 2: Substitute.

$$
R = 3 \times 3 \times 2 = 18
$$

Step 3: Classify. Since $R = 18 > 9$, the risk is **CRITICAL** — demand immediate action.

Step 4: Recommend controls (per **IEC 62443-3-3 SR 1.1** and **SR 2.1**):
- **Control 1**: Network segmentation using a **unidirectional security gateway (data diode)** to enforce one-way data flow from OT to IT.
- **Control 2**: **Role-based access control (RBAC)** with multi-factor authentication for the SCADA HMI, and immediate firmware patching of the RTU.

Step 5: Compute the Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR) if both controls reduce the risk to Medium ($R_{after} = 4$):

$$
RRR = \frac{18 - 4}{18} = \frac{14}{18} \approx 0.778
$$

$$
RRR\ (\%) \approx 77.8\%
$$

This justifies the investment in the proposed controls.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **Purdue Reference Model** with a neat diagram and explain how it is used for **system characterization** in an ICS environment. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

The Purdue Model, developed by ISA and ISA-99, segments industrial networks into **six hierarchical levels**:

- **Level 0 (Physical Process)**: Sensors, actuators, motors, valves — the physical plant.
- **Level 1 (Basic Control)**: PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers executing control logic.
- **Level 2 (Supervisory Control)**: SCADA servers, HMI workstations.
- **Level 3 (Site Operations)**: Historians, alarm management, MES interfaces.
- **Level 4 (Site Business Planning)**: ERP, scheduling, supply chain systems.
- **Level 5 (Enterprise Network)**: Corporate IT, email, internet, cloud services.

**Use in Characterization**:
- Each level is treated as a separate **zone** during characterization.
- Inter-level communications occur through **conduits** with controlled traffic.
- Assets at each level are inventoried separately, and their criticality is assessed independently.
- This stratification allows engineers to apply **defense-in-depth** strategies.

*(A clean ASCII or labeled Mermaid diagram earns full credit. Verbal diagram description also acceptable.)*

---

**(b)** Differentiate between **STRIDE** and **PASTA** threat modeling frameworks. Apply STRIDE to a **Level 2 HMI workstation** and list at least **four threats** with examples. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution**:

| Aspect | STRIDE | PASTA |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Microsoft, 1999 | Tony UcedaVélez & Morana, 2012 |
| Type | Categorical mnemonic | Risk-centric, multi-stage |
| Focus | Threat categories | Attacker-centric simulation |
| Stages | 6 categories (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, Elevation of Privilege) | 7 stages from business objectives to technical impact |
| Suitability for ICS | Quick categorization | Deep, attacker-perspective analysis |

**STRIDE Applied to a Level 2 HMI Workstation**:

1. **Spoofing**: An attacker forges the HMI's identity on the network to send fake setpoints. *Example*: ARP spoofing attack against the HMI's IP.

2. **Tampering**: Unauthorized modification of control screen values or recipe files. *Example*: Malware alters the displayed reactor temperature.

3. **Repudiation**: Operator performs an unsafe action and deletes log entries to hide it. *Example*: Clearing the Windows Event Log.

4. **Information Disclosure**: Sensitive process parameters leak to unauthorized users. *Example*: Wireshark capture of unencrypted Modbus traffic.

5. **Denial of Service**: HMI screen freezes, preventing operator intervention during a process upset. *Example*: ICMP flood on the HMI's network port.

6. **Elevation of Privilege**: Standard user gains admin rights to push malicious logic to a PLC. *Example*: Exploiting a vulnerable HMI service to escalate privileges.

*(Any four threats with valid examples fetch full marks for part b.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Do not** write $R = L + I$ in exams. Risk is multiplicative, not additive. This is a top-priority KTU marking trap.
> 2. **Do not** confuse CVSS base score with CVSS temporal/environmental scores. KTU 2024 expects only the base score range (0.0–10.0).
> 3. **Do not** mix up the Purdue Model levels in the diagram. Level 0 is the *physical* process, not the corporate IT.
> 4. **Always** pair STRIDE threats with **ICS-specific examples**. Generic IT examples (e.g., "email phishing") lose marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Methodology** = the *procedure*; **Characterization** = the *output* of identifying and classifying the system.
- The **five-phase methodology** = Characterize → Threat ID → Vulnerability ID → Risk Compute → Controls.
- **Risk equation**: $R = L \times I \times V$ (multiplicative, not additive).
- **ALE equation**: $ALE = SLE \times ARO$ for quantitative cost-benefit.
- **CVSS range**: **0.0 to 10.0** — used for vulnerability ranking, not risk.
- **Purdue Model** has **6 levels (0–5)** — used for zone-based characterization.
- **IEC 62443** uses **Zones and Conduits** — a Zone is a grouping of assets, a Conduit is the communication path.
- **STRIDE** = 6-category mnemonic; **PASTA** = 7-stage attacker-centric model.
- **Risk Reduction Ratio (RRR)** is expressed as a **percentage** in compliance reports.
- **Likelihood and Impact** are scored on a **1–3 ordinal scale** in qualitative ICS audits.
- A **High × High** combination always yields a **Critical** rating requiring immediate action.
- **Unidirectional gateways (data diodes)** are the gold-standard control for Critical infrastructure ICS segmentation.
- **Always** document the **asset inventory** as the first deliverable — without it, the entire risk assessment is invalid.
- The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **NEP 2020 outcome-based learning** — students must be able to *apply* the methodology to a *given* ICS scenario, not just memorize definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Assessment and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** System Assessment and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**System Assessment and Classification** in the context of Industrial Control System (ICS) Security is a structured, methodical, and standards-driven process of identifying, analyzing, evaluating, and categorizing the security posture, criticality, and risk exposure of industrial automation assets, processes, and the networks that interconnect them. It establishes the foundational knowledge base required to apply proportionate, risk-based cybersecurity controls, primarily aligned with **IEC 62443**, **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3**, and the **NIST Cybersecurity Framework (CSF) 2.0**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"System Assessment and Classification is the systematic process of inventorying industrial control assets, evaluating their security levels (SL 1–4), classifying them into security zones and conduits, and assigning appropriate Security Level Targets (SL-T) based on the consequence of compromise to safety, operations, finances, and the environment."*

The process has three formally distinct yet interdependent phases:

1. **System Identification & Inventory:** Cataloguing all hardware (PLCs, RTUs, SCADA servers, HMIs, historians, sensors, actuators), software (firmware, SCADA/DCS applications, engineering workstations), network infrastructure (switches, firewalls, routers), and the data flows between them.
2. **Security Assessment:** Evaluating the system's existing safeguards, identifying vulnerabilities, estimating threats, and computing the inherent and residual risk.
3. **Classification & Categorization:** Grouping assets into **Security Zones** (logical or physical groupings of assets sharing common security requirements) and **Conduits** (communication paths between zones), then assigning a **Security Level Target (SL-T)** from **SL 1** to **SL 4**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hospital Triage Analogy 🏥**
> Imagine a hospital emergency room. Before treating any patient, the staff performs **triage**:
> 1. They **inventory** every patient (who is here, what's their condition?).
> 2. They **assess** the severity (critical, serious, stable).
> 3. They **classify** them into priority zones — Red Zone (life-threatening, needs immediate surgery), Yellow Zone (serious, needs monitoring), Green Zone (minor, can wait).
> 4. They assign **protection levels** — Red Zone patients get a 1:1 nurse, Green Zone patients share a general ward.
>
> **System Assessment and Classification does exactly the same thing for an industrial plant:**
> - **Inventory** = Which PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, and servers exist?
> - **Assess** = What threats and vulnerabilities do they face?
> - **Classify** = Group them into zones (e.g., Safety Instrumented System Zone, Control Zone, DMZ).
> - **Assign SL-T** = How much protection does each zone need (SL 1 casual to SL 4 nation-state-grade)?

In an oil refinery, for example, a **safety shutdown system (SIS)** protecting against catastrophic over-pressure would be classified into a high-criticality zone with **SL-3** protection, while a non-critical building management HVAC controller might be **SL-1**.

### 1.3 Standardized Security Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Reference | Range/Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Security Level Target | **SL-T** | IEC 62443-3-3 | **1** to **4** |
| Security Level Achieved | **SL-A** | IEC 62443-3-3 | **1** to **4** |
| Security Level Capability | **SL-C** | IEC 62443-3-3 | **1** to **4** |
| Risk Score | **R** | NIST SP 800-30 | **0** to **25** (5×5 matrix) |
| Criticality Tier | **CT** | ISA-99 / Purdue | **Level 0** to **Level 5** |
| Zone Identification | **Z** | IEC 62443-3-2 | String (e.g., *Zone-Cell*) |
| Conduit Identification | **C** | IEC 62443-3-2 | String (e.g., *Conduit-IT-OT*) |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "**SL-T** is what you **T**arget. **SL-A** is what you **A**chieve. The gap between them drives your remediation roadmap."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Triad: Threat × Vulnerability × Impact

The entire discipline of system assessment rests on a single conceptual triad formalized in **NIST SP 800-30 Rev. 1** and adopted by IEC 62443-3-2:

- **Threat:** The potential cause of an unwanted incident that may harm a system. Threats can be *adversarial* (hacktivists, nation-states, insider), *accidental* (human error), *structural* (equipment failure), or *environmental* (natural disaster, power loss).
- **Vulnerability:** A weakness in an asset, control, or system that can be exploited by a threat. Vulnerabilities may be technical (unpatched firmware), procedural (no password rotation policy), or architectural (flat Layer-2 network).
- **Impact:** The magnitude of harm that would result if the threat successfully exploited the vulnerability. Impact is measured across four dimensions per IEC 62443-3-2:
  - **Safety** (loss of life or injury)
  - **Operations** (loss of production)
  - **Financial** (direct monetary loss, regulatory fines)
  - **Environment** (toxic release, ecological damage)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** Examiners love asking *"List and explain the four impact categories as per IEC 62443-3-2."* Memorize: **Safety, Operations, Financial, Environment** (acronym: **S.O.F.E.**).

### 2.2 The Five-Step Assessment Lifecycle

The assessment process is **iterative** and follows these formal stages:

1. **Step 1 — System Under Consideration (SUC) Definition:**
   Establish the scope, boundary, and purpose. For a refinery, the SUC might be "Crude Distillation Unit DCS — Sections 100 through 500."

2. **Step 2 — Asset & Dataflow Inventory:**
   Document every device using tools like **Nmap**, **ICS-specific passive discovery (e.g., Claroty, Nozomi Networks)**, manual walk-downs, and asset configuration databases. Build **dataflow diagrams** showing who talks to whom and over which protocol (Modbus/TCP, DNP3, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA, Profinet).

3. **Step 3 — Zone & Conduit Partitioning:**
   Group assets that share common security requirements into **Zones**, and define **Conduits** for the communication paths. The partitioning logic is: *"Assets within a zone trust each other; assets across a conduit do not."* This is a direct application of the **Purdue Reference Model (PERA)**, which stratifies ICS into six levels (0–5).

4. **Step 4 — Risk & Criticality Analysis:**
   For each zone, determine the worst-case impact per dimension. Use the **risk matrix** to compute the inherent risk. Apply existing controls to compute **residual risk**.

5. **Step 5 — Security Level Target (SL-T) Assignment:**
   Map the worst-case impact to one of four Security Levels as per IEC 62443-3-3:
   - **SL 1 — Casual / Coincidental:** Protection against opportunistic attacks.
   - **SL 2 — Intentional / Simple:** Protection against low-resources, low-motivation attackers using simple means.
   - **SL 3 — Intentional / Sophisticated:** Protection against sophisticated attackers with moderate resources (e.g., organized crime, hacktivists).
   - **SL 4 — Intentional / Sophisticated / Well-Resourced:** Protection against nation-state-grade adversaries with extensive resources (APT groups).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = f(T, V, I)$ | Risk is a function of Threat, Vulnerability, and Impact | Dimensionless index |
| 2 | $R_{\text{inherent}} = T \times V \times I$ | Inherent (pre-control) risk score | **0** to **25** |
| 3 | $R_{\text{residual}} = R_{\text{inherent}} - C_{\text{eff}}$ | Residual risk after control effectiveness | **0** to **25** |
| 4 | $I_{\text{composite}} = w_1 I_S + w_2 I_O + w_3 I_F + w_4 I_E$ | Weighted composite impact (S.O.F.E.) | **1** to **5** |
| 5 | $P_{\text{annual}} = T_{\text{rate}} \times V_{\text{prob}}$ | Annual probability of exploitation | Probability in $[0, 1]$ |
| 6 | $\text{ALE} = P_{\text{annual}} \times \text{SLE}$ | Annual Loss Expectancy (NIST/FAIR) | Currency / year |
| 7 | $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$ | Single Loss Expectancy (Asset Value × Exposure Factor) | Currency |
| 8 | $\text{SL-T} = \max(\text{SL}_{S}, \text{SL}_{O}, \text{SL}_{F}, \text{SL}_{E})$ | Overall SL-T is the maximum across S.O.F.E. dimensions | **{1, 2, 3, 4}** |
| 9 | $\text{SL-A} \geq \text{SL-T}$ | Required compliance condition | Boolean |
| 10 | $\text{CVSS Base} \in [0, 10]$ | Vulnerability severity (CVSS v3.1) | Continuous |
| 11 | $C_{\text{eff}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot \text{Control}_i$ | Aggregate control effectiveness (weighted) | **0** to **10** |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** The **|**| symbol for absolute value is never used in the table above. Use **$\vert x \vert$** or **$\mid x \mid$** in your exam scripts to avoid markdown/KTU answer-book rendering issues.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Sector:** Refineries like *Bharat Petroleum Kochi Refinery* or *Reliance Jamnagar* must classify their Distributed Control Systems (DCS) under SL-2/SL-3, while their Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS) are typically SL-3.
- **Power Grids:** *Kerala State Electricity Board (KSEB)* substations use IEC 61850 GOOSE messaging; the protection zones (P1, P2, P3 per NERC CIP) are classified by criticality.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Classification drives the placement of **Industrial Demilitarized Zones (IDMZ)** between Purdue Levels 3 and 3.5.
- **Water Treatment Utilities:** SCADA-controlled water plants must comply with the *American Water Works Association (AWWA) G430* standard, which mandates a formal risk classification process.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Composite Risk Score from the Risk Matrix

The **5×5 risk matrix** is the de-facto industry standard. We derive the composite risk score from first principles.

**Given:**
- Likelihood $L \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$
- Impact $I \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$

**Step 1 — Define the inherent risk scoring function.**

$$
R_{\text{inherent}} = L \times I
$$

**Step 2 — Categorize the score into qualitative risk levels.**

$$
\text{Level} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{Low} & \text{if } R_{\text{inherent}} \in [1, 4] \\
\text{Medium} & \text{if } R_{\text{inherent}} \in [5, 12] \\
\text{High} & \text{if } R_{\text{inherent}} \in [13, 19] \\
\text{Severe} & \text{if } R_{\text{inherent}} \in [20, 25]
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute composite Impact across S.O.F.E.**

Let individual impact scores be $I_S, I_O, I_F, I_E \in [1, 5]$ with weights $w_1, w_2, w_3, w_4 \geq 0$ and $\sum w_i = 1$.

$$
I_{\text{composite}} = w_1 I_S + w_2 I_O + w_3 I_F + w_4 I_E
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Consider an **oil & gas separator vessel SCADA** zone. We have the following:

| Dimension | Individual Score ($I$) | Weight ($w$) | Weighted Product |
|---|---|---|---|
| Safety ($I_S$) | **5** | 0.40 | 2.00 |
| Operations ($I_O$) | **4** | 0.25 | 1.00 |
| Financial ($I_F$) | **4** | 0.20 | 0.80 |
| Environment ($I_E$) | **5** | 0.15 | 0.75 |

Summing the weighted products:

$$
I_{\text{composite}} = 2.00 + 1.00 + 0.80 + 0.75 = 4.55
$$

Rounding to the nearest integer on the 5×5 scale: $I \approx 5$ (Catastrophic). Suppose the likelihood $L = 4$ (High). Then:

$$
R_{\text{inherent}} = L \times I = 4 \times 5 = 20
$$

Per the table above, $R_{\text{inherent}} = 20$ falls in $[20, 25]$, so the risk is **Severe**.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Security Level Target (SL-T) Assignment

The SL-T is the **maximum** SL required across any of the four impact dimensions.

**Step 1 — Map each impact score to a candidate SL.**

The mapping table (per IEC 62443-3-3 Annex A guidance) is:

| Impact Score | SL Candidate |
|---|---|
| **1** (Negligible) | **SL 1** |
| **2** (Minor) | **SL 2** |
| **3** (Moderate) | **SL 2** |
| **4** (Major) | **SL 3** |
| **5** (Catastrophic) | **SL 4** |

**Step 2 — Apply the max operator.**

$$
\text{SL-T}_{\text{zone}} = \max(\text{SL}_{S}, \text{SL}_{O}, \text{SL}_{F}, \text{SL}_{E})
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example.**

Using the separator vessel zone from §3.1:

- $I_S = 5 \Rightarrow \text{SL}_S = \text{SL 4}$
- $I_O = 4 \Rightarrow \text{SL}_O = \text{SL 3}$
- $I_F = 4 \Rightarrow \text{SL}_F = \text{SL 3}$
- $I_E = 5 \Rightarrow \text{SL}_E = \text{SL 4}$

Applying the max operator:

$$
\text{SL-T}_{\text{zone}} = \max(4, 3, 3, 4) = 4
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This zone therefore requires **nation-state-grade (SL 4) protection** — a fact that immediately justifies investment in cryptographic authentication, hardware security modules (HSMs), and air-gapped backup systems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE) — Quantitative Risk

**Step 1 — Define Single Loss Expectancy.**

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}
$$

where:
- $\text{AV}$ = Asset Value (e.g., ₹50,00,000)
- $\text{EF}$ = Exposure Factor (e.g., 0.6 = 60% loss)

**Step 2 — Define Annual Rate of Occurrence.**

$$
\text{ARO} = T_{\text{rate}}
$$

For a known ICS vulnerability, suppose $\text{ARO} = 0.5$ (once every 2 years).

**Step 3 — Compute ALE.**

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{ARO} \times \text{SLE} = 0.5 \times (\text{AV} \times \text{EF})
$$

Plugging in numbers:

$$
\text{ALE} = 0.5 \times (50,00,000 \times 0.6) = 0.5 \times 30,00,000 = 15,00,000
$$

**Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Decision Rule.**

If the cost of a proposed control $C_{\text{control}} < \text{ALE}$, the control is economically justified. For example, a firewall at ₹10,00,000 reduces ALE by 80%, so the *post-control ALE* is:

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 15,00,000 \times (1 - 0.80) = 3,00,000
$$

The **Annual Savings** are:

$$
\text{Savings} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 15,00,000 - 3,00,000 = 12,00,000
$$

The control is justified because:

$$
C_{\text{control}} = 10,00,000 < 12,00,000 = \text{Savings}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Asset Classification Example (KTU Exam-Style)

| Asset | Purdue Level | Zone | SL-T | Risk Score | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pressure Sensor (PT-101) | **L0** | Zone-SIS | **4** | **20** | Catastrophic over-pressure if spoofed |
| PLC (S7-1500) | **L1** | Zone-Control-A | **3** | **15** | Process manipulation, large production loss |
| HMI Workstation | **L2** | Zone-Operator | **2** | **8** | View-only risk, low direct impact |
| Historian Server | **L3** | Zone-DMZ | **2** | **6** | Data theft, not safety-critical |
| ERP Interface (SAP) | **L4** | Zone-Enterprise | **1** | **3** | Outside critical path |
| Engineering Laptop (laptop used to program PLCs) | **L1–L2** | Zone-Mobile-Maintenance | **3** | **12** | Vector for Stuxnet-style attacks |
| Firewall (Purdue L3.5) | **L3.5** | Conduit-IT/OT | **3** | **12** | Misconfig = full bypass |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: End-to-End System Assessment & Classification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["A: Define System Under Consideration"] --> B["B: Asset and Dataflow Inventory"]
    B --> C["C: Identify Threats and Vulnerabilities"]
    C --> D["D: Compute Inherent Risk R equals T times V times I"]
    D --> E{"E: Risk Acceptable?"}
    E -- No --> F["F: Define Security Zones and Conduits"]
    F --> G["G: Assign SL-T to Each Zone S O F E dimensions"]
    G --> H["H: Map to Purdue Model Levels 0 to 5"]
    H --> I["I: Apply Controls and Compute Residual Risk"]
    I --> J{"J: SL-A greater than or equal to SL-T?"}
    J -- No --> K["K: Remediation Roadmap with Prioritized Controls"]
    K --> I
    J -- Yes --> L["L: Document and Monitor Continuously"]
    E -- Yes --> L
    L --> M["M: Periodic Reassessment Every 12 Months"]
    M --> A
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Zone and Conduit Architecture (Purdue-Aligned)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L5["L5: Enterprise Network"]
        ERP["ERP System SAP"]
        EMAIL["Email and Internet"]
    end
    subgraph L4["L4: Site Business Planning"]
        MES["MES Historian Server"]
    end
    subgraph L3D["L3.5: Industrial DMZ"]
        FW1["Firewall External"]
        FW2["Firewall Internal"]
        JUMP["Jump Server Bastion"]
    end
    subgraph L3["L3: Site Operations"]
        SCADA["SCADA Master"]
        ENG["Engineering Workstation"]
    end
    subgraph L2["L2: Supervisory Control"]
        HMI1["HMI Area A"]
        HMI2["HMI Area B"]
    end
    subgraph L1["L1: Basic Control"]
        PLC1["PLC Reactor A"]
        PLC2["PLC Reactor B"]
        SIS["Safety Instrumented System"]
    end
    subgraph L0["L0: Physical Process"]
        SENS["Sensors PT TT FT"]
        ACT["Actuators Valves Pumps"]
    end
    L5 --- L4
    L4 --- FW1
    FW1 --- L3D
    L3D --- FW2
    FW2 --- L3
    L3 --- L2
    L2 --- L1
    L1 --- L0
    SIS --- PLC1
    SIS --- PLC2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: SL-T Determination Logic (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["A: Start: Per Zone"] --> Q1{"Q1: Loss of Life Risk?"}
    Q1 -- Yes and Catastrophic --> SL4["SL4: Nation State Grade"]
    Q1 -- Yes but Major --> SL3["SL3: Sophisticated"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Q2: Major Environmental Impact?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> SL3
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Q3: Major Financial or Operations Loss?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SL2["SL2: Intentional Simple"]
    Q3 -- No --> SL1["SL1: Casual Coincidental"]
    SL4 --> OUT["A: Final SL-T equals max of S O F E dimensions"]
    SL3 --> OUT
    SL2 --> OUT
    SL1 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Risk Matrix Heat Map (5×5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L5row["Likelihood 5: Almost Certain"] --> C55["Impact 5 R25 SEVERE"] & C54["Impact 4 R20 SEVERE"] & C53["Impact 3 R15 HIGH"] & C52["Impact 2 R10 MEDIUM"] & C51["Impact 1 R5 MEDIUM"]
    L4row["Likelihood 4: Likely"] --> C45["Impact 5 R20 SEVERE"] & C44["Impact 4 R16 HIGH"] & C43["Impact 3 R12 MEDIUM"] & C42["Impact 2 R8 MEDIUM"] & C41["Impact 1 R4 LOW"]
    L3row["Likelihood 3: Possible"] --> C35["Impact 5 R15 HIGH"] & C34["Impact 4 R12 MEDIUM"] & C33["Impact 3 R9 MEDIUM"] & C32["Impact 2 R6 MEDIUM"] & C31["Impact 1 R3 LOW"]
    L2row["Likelihood 2: Unlikely"] --> C25["Impact 5 R10 MEDIUM"] & C24["Impact 4 R8 MEDIUM"] & C23["Impact 3 R6 MEDIUM"] & C22["Impact 2 R4 LOW"] & C21["Impact 1 R2 LOW"]
    L1row["Likelihood 1: Rare"] --> C15["Impact 5 R5 MEDIUM"] & C14["Impact 4 R4 LOW"] & C13["Impact 3 R3 LOW"] & C12["Impact 2 R2 LOW"] & C11["Impact 1 R1 LOW"]
```

> [!TIP]
> In a printed KTU answer book, a hand-drawn version of the **5×5 matrix** with red, amber, and green color-coding is worth full marks even if you cannot draw Mermaid.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the term "Security Zone" as used in IEC 62443-3-2. How is it different from a network segment created by a VLAN?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Security Zone** (per IEC 62443-3-2) is a *logical or physical grouping of one or more assets that share common security requirements*. A zone is defined by a **common Security Level Target (SL-T)** and a **common security policy**, irrespective of the underlying network topology. The difference from a **VLAN** is that:

- A **VLAN** is a *Layer-2 network-broadcast isolation mechanism*; it groups ports/switches.
- A **Zone** is a *security-policy grouping*; multiple VLANs can belong to a single zone, and a single VLAN can span multiple zones only if the policy is identical.

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List the four impact categories used in IEC 62443-3-2 to determine the Security Level Target.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four impact categories are:
1. **Safety (S)** — Risk to human life or physical injury.
2. **Operations (O)** — Loss of production, degradation of process quality, or unavailability of service.
3. **Financial (F)** — Direct monetary loss, regulatory fines, or loss of brand reputation.
4. **Environment (E)** — Toxic release, ecological damage, or non-compliance with environmental regulations.

The mnemonic is **S.O.F.E.**

---

### Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️**
> - **Common Mark Loss #1:** Students write "Security Level" without specifying **SL-T vs SL-A vs SL-C**. This alone costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> - **Common Mark Loss #2:** Forgetting the **S.O.F.E. (Safety, Operations, Financial, Environment)** acronym and only listing 2–3 impact categories.
> - **Common Mark Loss #3:** Failing to mention the **iterative** nature of assessment — the process repeats every 12 months, not a one-time event.
> - **Common Mark Loss #4:** Not citing the **Purdue Reference Model** when discussing zone partitioning.

---

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

> **Q.3 (a)** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4] **Explain with a neat diagram the five-step methodology of system assessment and classification as per IEC 62443-3-2 and NIST SP 800-82.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **five-step methodology** for system assessment and classification of an Industrial Control System is as follows:

**Step 1 — System Under Consideration (SUC) Definition (1 mark):**
The scope, physical boundary, and operational context of the system are explicitly defined. For a power plant, the SUC could be "Boiler Feed Pump Control Loop, Unit 3, Tonnage 210 MW."

**Step 2 — Asset & Dataflow Inventory (2 marks):**
All assets are catalogued: PLCs, RTUs, IEDs, sensors, actuators, HMI stations, SCADA servers, historians, engineering workstations, network switches, firewalls, and routers. Dataflow diagrams are produced showing protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 61850, OPC UA), directionality, and ports used.

**Step 3 — Zone and Conduit Partitioning (1 mark):**
Assets with common security requirements are grouped into **Zones**, and the communication paths between zones are defined as **Conduits**. Example zones: *Safety Zone (SIS)*, *Basic Control Zone*, *Supervisory Zone*, *DMZ*, *Enterprise Zone*.

**Step 4 — Risk and Criticality Analysis (2 marks):**
For each zone, the worst-case impact is determined across the four dimensions (S.O.F.E.), and the inherent risk is computed using a 5×5 risk matrix ($R = L \times I$). A representative risk matrix and S.O.F.E. table should be drawn.

**Step 5 — Security Level Target (SL-T) Assignment (1 mark):**
