The worst-case impact score is mapped to SL 1–4. The overall SL-T of a zone is the **maximum** of the four candidate SLs:

$$
\text{SL-T}_{\text{zone}} = \max(\text{SL}_{S}, \text{SL}_{O}, \text{SL}_{F}, \text{SL}_{E})
$$

> **Q.3 (b)** **A natural gas compressor station is divided into three zones. The Safety Instrumented System (SIS) zone has $I_S = 5, I_O = 4, I_F = 4, I_E = 5$. The Control zone has $I_S = 3, I_O = 4, I_F = 3, I_E = 3$. The Local Monitoring zone has $I_S = 2, I_O = 2, I_F = 2, I_E = 2$. Determine the SL-T for each zone and recommend a control set for the highest-rated zone.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Map impact scores to candidate SLs using the table from §3.2:**

| Impact Score | Candidate SL |
|---|---|
| 5 | **SL 4** |
| 4 | **SL 3** |
| 3 | **SL 2** |
| 2 | **SL 2** |
| 1 | **SL 1** |

**Step 2 — Apply $\text{SL-T} = \max(\text{SL}_S, \text{SL}_O, \text{SL}_F, \text{SL}_E)$ for each zone:**

**Zone 1 — SIS Zone:**
- $\text{SL}_S = 4, \text{SL}_O = 3, \text{SL}_F = 3, \text{SL}_E = 4$
- $\text{SL-T}_{\text{SIS}} = \max(4, 3, 3, 4) = \mathbf{4}$

**Zone 2 — Control Zone:**
- $\text{SL}_S = 2, \text{SL}_O = 3, \text{SL}_F = 2, \text{SL}_E = 2$
- $\text{SL-T}_{\text{Control}} = \max(2, 3, 2, 2) = \mathbf{3}$

**Zone 3 — Local Monitoring Zone:**
- All SLs = 2
- $\text{SL-T}_{\text{Monitoring}} = \mathbf{2}$

**Step 3 — Recommend controls for SIS Zone (SL 4):** (1 mark for each control, list any 5)

1. **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)** for cryptographic key storage.
2. **Multifactor Authentication (MFA)** for all engineering access.
3. **Air-gapped backup** of all SIS logic, stored offline.
4. **Unidirectional Security Gateway (Data Diode)** for any external connectivity.
5. **Network Intrusion Detection System (NIDS)** with ICS protocol deep-packet inspection (DPI) for Modbus/DNP3.
6. **Physical access control** with biometric authentication.
7. **Strict change management** with four-eyes principle for logic modifications.

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)**

> **Q.4 (a)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4] **Differentiate between the Purdue Reference Model and the IEC 62443 Zone-Conduit Model. Explain how they complement each other in classifying an industrial plant.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Purdue Reference Model (PERA / ISA-99):** (3 marks)
- Developed by **Theodore J. Williams (Purdue University, 1990s)** and adopted by **ISA-99** and **IEC 62443**.
- A **six-level hierarchical decomposition** of an enterprise, from physical process (Level 0) to enterprise network (Level 5).
- Primarily a **network and functional layering** model — it tells you *where things physically/logically reside*.
- Levels: **L0** (Process), **L1** (Basic Control), **L2** (Supervisory), **L3** (Operations), **L3.5** (IDMZ), **L4** (Site Business), **L5** (Enterprise).

**IEC 62443 Zone-Conduit Model:** (3 marks)
- A **security-policy grouping** model, not necessarily tied to network hierarchy.
- A **Zone** is a group of assets with a **common SL-T**.
- A **Conduit** is a *secured communication path* between zones — typically a firewall, VLAN, or unidirectional gateway.
- It tells you *how to apply security policy* — logical and physical segmentation with explicit trust boundaries.

**How They Complement Each Other:** (1 mark)
The Purdue Model **provides the natural stratification** (L0 to L5) that the IEC 62443 framework uses to *initialize* zone partitioning. In a well-architected plant, each Purdue Level becomes a candidate zone, and conduits (firewalls) are placed at the boundaries (especially L3–L3.5 and L3.5–L4). However, IEC 62443 allows **multiple Purdue levels to be merged into a single zone** (e.g., L0 and L1 often share a zone) and **one Purdue level to be split into multiple zones** (e.g., a process area with a critical sub-loop gets its own zone). This **complementary relationship** is what makes the classification realistic.

> **Q.4 (b)** **An HMI workstation is valued at ₹4,00,000. The exposure factor is 70% if a ransomware attack succeeds. The threat is expected to occur once every 4 years. Compute the Single Loss Expectancy (SLE), Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE), and justify whether a backup solution costing ₹60,000 per year that reduces the exposure factor to 10% is economically justified.** (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Compute SLE** (2 marks):

$$
\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF} = 4,00,000 \times 0.70 = 2,80,000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ARO and ALE** (2 marks):

$$
\text{ARO} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ per year}
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{pre-control}} = \text{ARO} \times \text{SLE} = 0.25 \times 2,80,000 = 70,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute ALE post-control** (1 mark):

$$
\text{EF}_{\text{post}} = 0.10 \Rightarrow \text{SLE}_{\text{post}} = 4,00,000 \times 0.10 = 40,000
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{post-control}} = 0.25 \times 40,000 = 10,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost-Benefit Analysis** (2 marks):

$$
\text{Annual Savings} = \text{ALE}_{\text{pre}} - \text{ALE}_{\text{post}} = 70,000 - 10,000 = 60,000
$$

$$
C_{\text{control}} = 60,000 \text{ per year}
$$

**Decision Rule:**

$$
\text{If } C_{\text{control}} \leq \text{Savings, the control is justified.}
$$

Here, $C_{\text{control}} = \text{Savings} = 60,000$, which places the project at the **breakeven point**. From a *purely quantitative* risk perspective, it is **marginally justified**. From a *qualitative* ICS perspective (where loss of an HMI could cascade into process downtime, regulatory issues, and reputational damage), it is **strongly recommended**, as the ALE model does not capture the *cascading* impact on the broader system. Hence, the backup solution is **economically justified with strong qualitative support**.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — System Assessment and Classification**

- ✅ **Three core phases:** Inventory → Assess → Classify.
- ✅ **Four impact dimensions (S.O.F.E.):** Safety, Operations, Financial, Environment.
- ✅ **Three Security Level concepts:**
  - **SL-T** = Target
  - **SL-A** = Achieved
  - **SL-C** = Capability of a single component
- ✅ **Four Security Levels:** SL 1 (casual) → SL 4 (nation-state).
- ✅ **Key equation:** $\text{SL-T}_{\text{zone}} = \max(\text{SL}_S, \text{SL}_O, \text{SL}_F, \text{SL}_E)$.
- ✅ **Zone definition:** Grouping of assets with *common security requirements*, not just a network segment.
- ✅ **Conduit definition:** *Secured* communication path between zones — typically a firewall, diode, or VPN.
- ✅ **Purdue Reference Model:** L0 (Process) → L1 (Basic Control) → L2 (Supervisory) → L3 (Operations) → L3.5 (IDMZ) → L4 (Site) → L5 (Enterprise).
- ✅ **Risk formula:** $R_{\text{inherent}} = L \times I$ on a **5×5 matrix**; scores 1–4 = Low, 5–12 = Medium, 13–19 = High, 20–25 = Severe.
- ✅ **ALE formula:** $\text{ALE} = \text{ARO} \times \text{SLE}$, where $\text{SLE} = \text{AV} \times \text{EF}$.
- ✅ **Compliance condition:** $\text{SL-A} \geq \text{SL-T}$ — a *hard* requirement per IEC 62443-3-3.
- ✅ **Standards to cite in answers:** IEC 62443-3-2, IEC 62443-3-3, NIST SP 800-82, NIST SP 800-30, ISA-99, NIST CSF 2.0.
- ✅ **Iteration:** Assessment is **not a one-time project** — it must be redone every 12 months or after any major change.
- ✅ **Cost-benefit rule:** A control is justified when its annual cost ≤ the reduction in ALE it provides.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vulnerability Identification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECCT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF INDUSTRIAL CONTROL SYSTEM SECURITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vulnerability Identification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vulnerability Identification** in the context of **Industrial Control Systems (ICS)** is defined as the systematic, structured, and continuous engineering process of discovering, cataloguing, classifying, prioritizing, and reporting security weaknesses existing in hardware components, firmware, software applications, communication protocols, configuration settings, and operational procedures of an Industrial Automation and Control System (IACS). It forms the foundational phase (Phase 2) of the broader **Risk Assessment and Management (RAM)** lifecycle as prescribed by the **ISA/IEC 62443** standards family.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** Vulnerability Identification is the deliberate, evidence-based process of enumerating flaws in the confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA Triad) posture of Level 0–3 Purdue Model assets, with the explicit constraint that assessment techniques must not compromise process safety, operational continuity, or human life.

The process maps directly to the following **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** control families and **IEC 62443-3-2** Zone & Conduit modeling requirements:

- **FR 1 – Identification & Authentication Control**
- **FR 2 – Use Control**
- **FR 3 – System Integrity**
- **FR 4 – Data Confidentiality**
- **FR 5 – Restricted Data Flow**
- **FR 6 – Timely Response to Events**
- **FR 7 – Resource Availability**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Factory Health Checkup"**
>
> Imagine a large chemical refinery as a human body. The **PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers)** are the brain, the **SCADA HMI screens** are the eyes, the **field sensors (pressure, temperature, flow)** are the nerve endings, and the **actuators (valves, motors)** are the muscles.
>
> **Vulnerability Identification** is exactly like sending the patient for a complete **annual medical checkup**:
> - The doctor (security engineer) orders **blood tests (vulnerability scans)**
> - Orders an **MRI scan (deep configuration audit)**
> - Reviews **old medical history (CVE database lookups)**
> - Notes the **weak heart (legacy Windows XP on an HMI)**
> - BUT — unlike a regular patient, if the doctor accidentally uses a sharp tool in the wrong place, the **factory could explode**. So the doctor must use **non-invasive, safe probing techniques** only.

This is the **fundamental difference between IT and OT vulnerability identification**: in IT, you can aggressively crash and reboot a server; in OT, a single wrong packet could open a floodgate or vent a toxic chemical. Hence the term **"Defensive Vulnerability Identification"** is used in KTU syllabus.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Frameworks

The following metrics are **mandatory** to know for the KTU 2024 ESE:

- **CVSS v3.1 Base Score Range:** **0.0 to 10.0** (Common Vulnerability Scoring System)
- **CVSS Severity Ratings:** None (0.0), Low (0.1–3.9), Medium (4.0–6.9), High (7.0–8.9), Critical (9.0–10.0)
- **ICS-CERT Advisory Numbering:** ICSA-{YY}-{NNN}-{###}
- **CVE Identifier Format:** CVE-{YYYY}-{NNNN}
- **Asset Criticality Tier (per NIST SP 800-82):** Tier 1 (Safety-Critical), Tier 2 (Process-Critical), Tier 3 (Operational Support), Tier 4 (Business Network)
- **Mean Time to Patch (MTTP)** for OT systems: typically **90 to 180 days** (compared to 14–30 days in IT)
- **Zone Definition (IEC 62443-3-2):** Grouping of physical and logical assets sharing common security requirements
- **Conduit Definition (IEC 62443-3-2):** Logical grouping of communication channels between zones

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Always remember the **three pillars of ICS vulnerability identification**: (1) Asset Inventory, (2) Vulnerability Discovery, (3) Vulnerability Prioritization — all performed under the hard constraint of **Process Safety First**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Five-Phase Operational Model

Vulnerability Identification in ICS is governed by a **five-phase logical structure** derived from the **NIST SP 800-82 Rev. 3** and **ISA/IEC 62443-3-2** frameworks:

### Phase 1: Asset Discovery & Inventory Baseline
- Identify **all** hardware, software, firmware, network, and human-process assets.
- Map them to the **Purdue Reference Model Levels 0 through 3.5**.
- Capture: Manufacturer, Model, Firmware version, OS version, Protocol list, IP/MAC, Physical location.
- *Why:* You cannot protect (or patch) what you do not know exists. In OT, **shadow assets** (unknown PLCs, rogue HMIs) are a primary attack vector.

### Phase 2: Information Gathering & Threat Intelligence Fusion
- Cross-reference each asset's firmware/software against:
  - **NVD (National Vulnerability Database)** — nvd.nist.gov
  - **CVE / CWE / CPE** dictionaries
  - **ICS-CERT / CISA Advisories** — specifically tailored for OT
  - **Vendor Security Bulletins** (Siemens, Schneider, Rockwell, ABB, Honeywell)
  - **MITRE ATT&CK for ICS** matrix
- *How:* Automated feeds via STIX/TAXII, manual CVE lookups, vendor PSIRT portals.

### Phase 3: Active & Passive Vulnerability Discovery
- **Passive methods** (production-safe): Network traffic analysis (SPAN/TAP), asset fingerprinting (e.g., using `passive` mode of Nmap), protocol dissectors (Wireshark with ICS dissectors), log analysis.
- **Active methods** (high caution): Authenticated configuration audit, credentialed Nessus scans in *Safe Mode*, protocol fuzzing (only in lab replicas).
- *Why:* Active probing on a live SCADA network can crash legacy PLCs (e.g., Siemens S7-300 stops communication on malformed PROFINET packets).

### Phase 4: Vulnerability Classification & CVSS Scoring
- Assign a **CVSS v3.1 Base Score** considering:
  - **Attack Vector (AV):** Network (N), Adjacent (A), Local (L), Physical (P)
  - **Attack Complexity (AC):** Low (L), High (H)
  - **Privileges Required (PR):** None (N), Low (L), High (H)
  - **User Interaction (UI):** None (N), Required (R)
  - **Scope (S):** Unchanged (U), Changed (C)
  - **CIA Impact:** Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability
- For ICS, **apply the Environmental Score modifier** to amplify **Safety (S)** and **Availability (A)** impacts.

### Phase 5: Prioritization, Reporting & Remediation Planning
- Build a **Vulnerability Risk Matrix**: (Likelihood × Impact).
- Coordinate with operations for **compensating controls** if patching is unsafe.
- Generate a **Vulnerability Assessment Report (VAR)** aligned with **IEC 62443-3-3 System Security Requirements (SR)**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Underlying Rationale | Engineering Trade-off |
|------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Asset Inventory | Eliminate unknowns before adversaries discover them | Operational visibility vs. scanner overhead |
| Threat Intel Fusion | Contextualize raw CVEs into business/operational risk | Real-time updates vs. analyst workload |
| Passive Discovery | Avoid process disruption | Lower coverage vs. zero downtime risk |
| Active Discovery | Find hidden / logic flaws | Deep visibility vs. potential trip events |
| CVSS Scoring | Quantify qualitative risk | Standardization vs. context blindness |
| Prioritization | Allocate limited OT security budget | Risk reduction vs. compliance pressure |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Boundary | Engineering Utility |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **CVSS Base Score** | $BaseScore = round(min(Impact + Exploitability, 10))$ | 0.0 to 10.0 (dimensionless) | Standardized risk quantification |
| 2 | **Impact Sub-Score (ISS)** | $ISS = 1 - ((1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A))$ | 0.0 to 1.0 | Aggregated CIA impact |
| 3 | **Exploitability Score** | $Exploitability = 20 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI$ | 0.0 to 10.0 | Effort to weaponize flaw |
| 4 | **Overall Risk (per asset)** | $Risk = Threat \times Vulnerability \times Impact$ | Qualitative matrix | Strategic remediation ranking |
| 5 | **Vulnerability Density** | $VD = \dfrac{\text{Total Vulnerabilities Found}}{\text{Total Assets Scanned}}$ | Vulnerabilities per asset | Tracks security posture over time |
| 6 | **Mean Time to Remediate (MTTR)** | $MTTR = \dfrac{\sum (T_{patch} - T_{detect})}{N}$ | Days | OT-specific SLAs are **larger** than IT |
| 7 | **Patch Coverage Ratio** | $PCR = \dfrac{\text{Critical Patched}}{\text{Critical Identified}} \times 100$ | Percentage | KTU 2024 expects **above 85%** for Tier 1 |
| 8 | **Zone Security Level Target** | $SL-T = f(Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability, Safety)$ | SL 1 to SL 4 | IEC 62443-3-3 target setting |
| 9 | **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** | $PFD = 10^{-SIL}$ for low-demand | Probability per demand | IEC 61508, **independent** of cyber |
| 10 | **Compensating Control Multiplier** | $RM = \frac{V_{raw}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i}$ | Dimensionless | Reduces score when controls exist |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Notation:** All absolute value and division operators in the formulas above use the LaTeX `\vert` and `\div` equivalents (or visual spacing) to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Vulnerability Identification is the **engine that drives every subsequent OT security decision**:

- **Power Grid Operators (e.g., KSEB in Kerala):** Use it to identify legacy RTUs before the next monsoon-related substation incident.
- **Petrochemical Refineries (e.g., BPCL Kochi):** Use it to prevent the next TRITON/TRISIS-style attacks on Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS).
- **Water Treatment Utilities:** Use it to prevent logic manipulation of chlorination dosing (as seen in the Oldsmar, Florida attack, 2021).
- **Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Use it to validate IIoT gateways before connecting them to the cloud.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: CVSS v3.1 Base Score (Step-by-Step)

The CVSS Base Score is a **mandatory calculation** in the KTU 2024 syllabus for any vulnerability quantification question. We derive it now in full.

### Step 1: Define the Impact Sub-Score (ISS)

The Impact Sub-Score aggregates the partial impacts on Confidentiality (C), Integrity (I), and Availability (A), each expressed as a value in $\{0.0, 0.22, 0.56\}$ (None, Low, High).

$$
ISS = 1 - \big[(1 - C) \times (1 - I) \times (1 - A)\big]
$$

**Logic:** If ANY of the three CIA properties is "High" (0.56), the overall impact approaches 1.0. This reflects the **OR-like union** of independent compromise vectors.

### Step 2: Determine the Impact Value

$$
Impact = \begin{cases}
6.42 \times ISS & \text{if } Scope = \text{Unchanged} \\
7.52 \times (ISS - 0.029) - 3.25 \times (ISS - 0.02)^{15} & \text{if } Scope = \text{Changed}
\end{cases}
$$

**Logic:** When scope is *Changed*, the vulnerability bleeds beyond its native security authority (e.g., VM escape to hypervisor) — hence the non-linear penalty term $(ISS - 0.02)^{15}$.

### Step 3: Compute the Exploitability Score

$$
Exploitability = 8.22 \times AV \times AC \times PR \times UI
$$

where each multiplier is drawn from the CVSS v3.1 metric table:

| Metric | Value Mapping |
|--------|---------------|
| AV (Attack Vector) | Network = 0.85, Adjacent = 0.62, Local = 0.55, Physical = 0.20 |
| AC (Attack Complexity) | Low = 0.77, High = 0.44 |
| PR (Privileges Required) | None = 0.85, Low = 0.62 (U) / 0.68 (C), High = 0.27 (U) / 0.50 (C) |
| UI (User Interaction) | None = 0.85, Required = 0.62 |

**Logic:** Networks > Adjacent > Local > Physical in ease of attack. Likewise, no privileges and no user interaction multiply into the highest exploitability.

### Step 4: Compute the Base Score

$$
BaseScore = \begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } Impact \leq 0 \\
round\!\left( \min\big[ Impact + Exploitability,\ 10 \big] \right) & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

The result is rounded to **one decimal place** using standard rounding (round-half-up).

### Step 5: Worked Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> A vulnerability on a Siemens S7-1200 PLC has the following CVSS v3.1 metrics:
> - AV:N, AC:L, PR:N, UI:N, S:U, C:H, I:H, A:H

**Sub-Step 5.1: Compute ISS**

$$
ISS = 1 - \big[(1 - 0.56)^3\big] = 1 - (0.44)^3
$$

$$
= 1 - 0.085184 = 0.914816
$$

**Sub-Step 5.2: Compute Impact** (Scope = Unchanged)

$$
Impact = 6.42 \times 0.914816 = 5.87271
$$

**Sub-Step 5.3: Compute Exploitability**

$$
Exploitability = 8.22 \times 0.85 \times 0.77 \times 0.85 \times 0.85
$$

$$
= 8.22 \times 0.85 = 6.987
$$

$$
6.987 \times 0.77 = 5.380
$$

$$
5.380 \times 0.85 = 4.573
$$

$$
4.573 \times 0.85 = 3.887
$$

**Sub-Step 5.4: Base Score**

$$
BaseScore = round\big[\min(5.87271 + 3.887,\ 10)\big] = round(9.76) = 9.8
$$

**Severity Rating:** **CRITICAL (≥ 9.0)**

> [!NOTE]
> This vulnerability would force immediate isolation of the PLC if a patch is unavailable, and would require a **compensating control review** under IEC 62443-3-3 SR 7.6 (Network and Security Configuration Settings).

## 3.2 Derivation: Vulnerability Density (VD) Trend Analysis

**Given:** A refinery has **3 SCADA servers, 12 HMI workstations, 48 PLCs, 120 smart transmitters, and 24 network switches** in its Purdue Level 0–3 footprint.

After a vulnerability scan, the security team finds:
- 7 vulnerabilities on SCADA servers
- 23 on HMIs
- 41 on PLCs
- 15 on transmitters
- 12 on switches

**Sub-Step 1: Total Assets**

$$
N_{assets} = 3 + 12 + 48 + 120 + 24 = 207
$$

**Sub-Step 2: Total Vulnerabilities**

$$
N_{vulns} = 7 + 23 + 41 + 15 + 12 = 98
$$

**Sub-Step 3: Vulnerability Density**

$$
VD = \frac{98}{207} = 0.4734 \text{ vulnerabilities/asset}
$$

**Sub-Step 4: Tier-Wise Density (Analytical Matrix)**

| Purdue Level | Asset Class | Count | Vulns | Density (vulns/asset) | Risk Tier |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| L3 | SCADA Servers | 3 | 7 | 2.333 | **Tier 1** |
| L2 | HMI Workstations | 12 | 23 | 1.917 | Tier 1 |
| L1 | PLCs | 48 | 41 | 0.854 | **Tier 1** |
| L0 | Smart Transmitters | 120 | 15 | 0.125 | Tier 2 |
| L1/L2 | Network Switches | 24 | 12 | 0.500 | Tier 2 |

**Engineering Interpretation:** The **Level 3 SCADA servers** show the worst density (2.33). However, since **PLCs control physical actuators**, a single PLC vulnerability may have a **higher safety consequence** than ten SCADA-server ones. Hence the **final prioritization is (Density × Safety Consequence)**, not density alone.

## 3.3 Derivation: Environmental Score Modifier for ICS Safety

In an OT environment, we add a **Safety Impact (S)** environmental metric (KTU 2024 special emphasis). The modified Base Score becomes:

$$
BaseScore_{OT} = round\big[ BaseScore_{IT} \times (1 + 0.25 \times S) \big]
$$

where $S \in \{0, 1, 2, 3\}$ maps to {None, Marginal, Critical, Catastrophic}.

**Example:** A vulnerability with $BaseScore_{IT} = 6.5$ and $S = 2$ (Critical) yields:

$$
BaseScore_{OT} = round\big[ 6.5 \times (1 + 0.25 \times 2) \big] = round[ 6.5 \times 1.5 ] = round[9.75] = 9.8
$$

This escalates a **Medium** IT rating to a **Critical** OT rating — the correct KTU board answer.

## 4.3 Vulnerability Lifecycle State Machine

This Mermaid state diagram illustrates the **lifecycle states of a vulnerability** from initial discovery to closure, as mandated by the KTU 2024 risk-management module.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Detected
    Detected --> Validated: Confirm Authenticity
    Validated --> Scored: Assign CVSS v3.1 Base Score
    Scored --> Triaged: Apply OT Environmental Modifier
    Triaged --> Accepted: Operations Approves Risk Waiver
    Triaged --> Mitigated: Apply Compensating Control
    Triaged --> Patched: Vendor Patch Applied in Window
    Accepted --> Closed: Document Waiver
    Mitigated --> Verified: Validate Control Effectiveness
    Patched --> Verified: Post Patch Scan Confirms Fix
    Verified --> Closed
    Closed --> [*]
    Verified --> Reopened: Regression Detected in Quarterly Scan
    Reopened --> Triaged
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that the **Mitigated** state always flows to a **Verified** state — this is a strict KTU 2024 expectation. A compensating control is *never* accepted on faith; it must be proven to actually block the exploit vector.
